item.title: Fears of chaos on L'Alpe d'Huez as crowds pack Alpine climb for Tour de France showdown
item.content:encoded: Fears of chaos on stage 19 of the Tour de France up L'Alpe d'Huez are growing after social media videos showed fans packed along the Alpine climb, partying into the night and slowing official vehicles and even police on the road. A video by and other people trying to reach L'Alpe d'Huez captured the atmosphere on the climb on Thursday, with groups of fans, perhaps drunk and excited for the Tour, singing and dancing on the road. Fireworks were set off, and the noise of the partying and police sirens could be heard miles away from the L'Alpe d'Huez ski resort above. "C'est la folie – It's crazy," one fan told , as his friends danced in the road. Baptiste Veistroffer of Lotto-Intermarché will race on L'Alpe d'Huez for the first time and told he is expecting "something crazy."L'Alpe d'Huez is considered a natural stadium for cycling, with fans from around the world travelling to see the race and ride the climb. A million people reportedly packed L'Alpe d'Huez when Lance Armstrong won a time trial on the climb. Similar numbers are possible for the final mountain stages of the 2026 Tour. "It's like a long tunnel that lasts 13km; there's constant noise. It’s what makes it special, more so than anywhere else. I really feel like I’m in a stadium," Guillaume Martin-Guyonnet (Groupama-FDJ United) said.The Tour de France returns to L'Alpe d'Huez for the first time since 2022 for a rare back-to-back stages to the ski resort. Friday's stage climbs the legendary 21 hairpins to the finish, while Saturday's stage starts in Bourg d'Oisans, includes the Col du Galibier and then returns to L'Alpe d'Huez via the Col de Sarenne and the back road to the ski resort. Tadej Pogačar will defend his yellow jersey and try to set up a fifth Tour victory, but illness has struck his UAE Team Emirates-XRG squad and so may offer his rivals an opportunity to attack him on L'Alpe d'Huez. If Pogačar is healthy, he will surely want to win on L'Alpe d'Huez and could even beat Marco Pantani's long-standing record for the climb. British fans have created the Beefeater bend and began partying on Thursday night. Their motto is "There are no strangers on Beefeater Bend; only friends who haven’t yet met," but they also call for fans to respect the race and the riders. The Dutch corner is at hairpin seven of the 21 and is often a key point in the race and the party atmosphere, with Dutch fans playing loud music and often spraying passing cars with water and other liquids. However, there are reports that police have put barriers along the Dutch corner, sparking a call on social media to move to the Danish corner "so we can have the crazy spectator tunnel."There appear to be longer sections of barriers, with other parts roped off. It remains to be seen if fans will respect these limits or try to crowd the road as the riders pass. Police closed the road early on Thursday afternoon, with reports of a 20km traffic jam on the valley road. spoke to several fans from around the world who revealed they secured spots on the climb for their tents and camper vans as early as Monday and Tuesday. Fears of chaos on L'Alpe d'Huez have sparked concern amongst the riders, who perhaps remember when Vincenzo Nibali crashed in the 2018 Tour de France after a spectator's camera strap caught his handlebars."It's going to be incredible, but most of all, I hope I don't crash on the climb because I think there will be a lot of spectators," Seixas told and other media."We've seen that it's quite dangerous. I just hope everything goes well. After that, it's my legs that will decide."Remco Evenepoel will race up Alpe d’Huez for the first time in his career and is riding for a place on the final podium. "I’m looking forward to discovering it, but it can also be frightening when you see how narrow it sometimes gets,” Evenepoel told ."Sometimes the fans can influence a stage. You might want to react to an attack, but you can't move forward because you are blocked in. We will see how extreme it gets."Hundreds of police are expected to try to control fans, but riders and teams are concerned about safety. Most cycling fans respect the riders, but there is a growing hooligan element amongst the fans."I can only hope that the fans behave themselves along the side of the road. Because it is becoming more and more of a problem," Visma-Lease a Bike team manager Richard Plugge told , suggesting that charging fans to access climbs could improve race safety. "Right now, there are also quite a few people standing along the road who think it’s carnival, who are guzzling beer all day and at some point don’t know what they are doing anymore. That is really going to become a problem."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Tour de France Braces for Chaos as Fans Flood Alpe d’Huez for Historic Double
item.author: ahood
item.content:encoded: An estimated 500,000 spectators pack Alpe d'Huez as Tour de France riders fear rowdy roadside crowds could derail the historic back-to-back summit weekend.

item.title: 'New opportunity, a fresh page' – Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney chases another Tour de France Femmes victory with team that delivers debuts for Bäckstedt, Klöser, Niedermaier
item.author: simone.giuliani@futurenet.com (Simone Giuliani)
item.content:encoded: Kasia Niewiadoma-Phinney, who has never failed to make the Tour de France Femmes overall podium, is heading back to the Grand Tour in 2026 with a fresh perspective and a group of support riders equally split between experienced players and debuting riders.Soraya Paladin and Agnieszka Skalniak-Sójka, who were both there when Niewiadoma-Phinney claimed the yellow jersey for Canyon-SRAM in 2024, are part of the squad, as is Chiara Consonni, who is returning after her debut in 2023. Then there are a strong trio of debuting riders from powerful gravel force Rosa Klöser – who won Unbound 200 in 2024 – Giro d’Italia runner-up Antonia Niedermaier, as well as dual British champion and Baloise Ladies Tour winner Zoe Bäckstedt.There is no question as to the squad's main target, given they have the GC force and proven experience of Niewiadoma on their team."Honestly, each season I just close the book and disconnect myself from the past because I always used to compare myself to previous years, and that would add extra, self-created pressure, and I'd feel like I needed to do better and better. So, the way I've been operating over the last few years is that each season is a new opportunity, a fresh page," said Niewiadoma in a team statement."So, coming into this year's Tour, I would definitely say that I'm richer in experience and have more knowledge of the Tour in general, which definitely gives me a little advantage. Because of that, I feel a bit calmer, and I also know what it takes to prepare perfectly for the race."This year is just a new opportunity. You're only as good as your last race. It's great to have a Tour victory on my palmarès, but I definitely come in with a fresh mind and body ready to go after new experiences and, of course, the victory."Once again grasping yellow, in a year where Niewiadoma lines up against two other former winners in Pauline Ferrand-Prévot (Visma-Lease a Bike) and Demi Vollering (FDJ United-SUEZ), is clearly the key focus for all the riders of the squad, though Canyon-SRAM have a group that is capable of shooting for stage wins too.The team said Skalniak-Sójka, who is racing her fourth edition, has always harboured an ambition to win a stage at the race, while a potential opportunity for the debuting Bäckstedt comes in the race against the clock on stage 4."It’s exciting, it’s really cool. I’ve dreamt a little bit about being selected for the Tour in the last years, and I think that with the course this year – it’s going to be brutal for sure – but time trialling is the one thing that suits me the most, so it’s really exciting. I think it’s going to be my favourite stage of the whole race, and it’s where I want to shine," said Bäckstedt of the 21km effort."I would obviously love to go for a result in the time trial, but I can’t put too big an expectation on it because it’s my first Tour and my first time against actually all of the big riders."Niedermaier, on the other hand, has proven her strength on the climbs and is also a potential threat on the GC, but the weight of expectation is not weighing on the 23-year-old."I'm not feeling too much pressure. I'm there for Kasia, but to also achieve a good GC result and maybe on a stage," Niedermaier said."For sure, I hope to be strong in the climbs, also in the TT, and to just give my best and prove that I belong in the TDFFAZ and with the best climbers. But, I also want to show that I can support my team and Kasia."For sure, she will also be super strong at the Tour, so let's see what we can achieve as a team. I hope that we can celebrate a podium with Kasia in the end – I think that's the main goal."Support our unrivalled coverage of the Tour de France Femmes and women's cycling by subscribing to Cyclingnews. In return, you get unlimited access to high-quality journalism, with breaking news, race analysis, and expert insight from inside the women's peloton. Plus, the Cyclingnews app lets you follow the action wherever you go!

item.title: ‘If you're not ready to adapt, you're not good enough’ - Norris addresses changing driving styles in F1 2026
Sickness Crisis Threatens Tadej Pogačar and His Dream Tour de France Finale
item.author: Jim Cotton
item.content:encoded: Team UAE torn apart by illness and injury as Pogačar looks to seal record-equalling fifth Tour de France yellow jersey in grueling Alpe d'Huez double.

item.title: 'I hope I don't crash' - Paul Seixas fears huge crowds on L'Alpe d'Huez could disrupt the Tour de France
item.content:encoded: Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM) is worried that the expected huge crowds on the hairpins of l'Alpe d'Huez on stage 19 could spark a crash and so disrupt the racing as the French super talent fights for a place on the Tour de France podium. French police closed the main road to l'Alpe d'Huez to ascending vehicular traffic early on Thursday afternoon after hundreds of thousands of people began to line the legendary road in cars and camper vans and packed the mountain top ski resort. Friday's stage climbs the famous Alpe d'Huez hairpins after a fast valley road from Gap. On Saturday, stage 20 returns to l'Alpe d'Huez via the small Col de Sarenne road. The crowds are expected to pack l'Alpe d'Huez for 48 hours, with a growing number of French fans returning to the Tour to cheer on Seixas. The 19-year-old has captured the hearts of the nation in recent weeks and is expected to fight for the GC podium in the Alps and could also take the best young rider's white jersey if he can distance Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates-XRG). "It's going to be incredible, but most of all, I hope I don't crash on the climb because I think there will be a lot of spectators," Seixas told L'Equipe and other media after finishing stage 18 in Orcières-Merlette. "We've seen that it's quite dangerous. I just hope everything goes well. After that, it's my legs that will decide."Vincenzo Nibali crashed near the summit of Alpe d'Huez in the 2018 Tour de France after a spectator's camera strap caught his handlebars. He got up and finished the stage but X-rays revealed a vertebrae fracture and he was forced to abandon the race. The Tour de France makes daily appeals for roadside fans to stay safe and respect the riders but Richard Carapaz almost crashed while on the attack as a fan waved to the television camera rather than look at the race. Seixas finished with his GC rivals in Orcières-Merlette. He sits fourth overall, 7:11 down on Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) but is only 20 seconds down on Del Toro and third place. He spent the stage trying to recover and prepare mentally and physically for the two days up to l'Alpe d'Huez. "With the fatigue of the third week, you have to pace yourself. We rode well today, we stayed together for a long time," Seixas said. "Daan [Hoole] was really impressive. I think it was a day that took its toll on everyone's legs but the main objective was to really conserve energy. The next two days will be very tough and will be where the Tour will be decided, at least for the podium. I'm happy to have had a smooth day."Sportingly Seixas said he didn't want UAE's Tour de France to be impacted by illness. "I think a few riders are a bit sick on their team, it's definitely a shame for them," Seixas said. "I hope Pogačar and Del Toro aren't sick, that's not how we want to race. But I think it will be physically tough for everyone."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: ‘Even when I was a rider, I wasn’t the type to wallow in my own sorrow’ – Geraint Thomas says Netcompany Ineos moving in ‘right direction’ despite Tour de France struggles
item.content:encoded: When Netcompany joined Ineos as title sponsors of the British WorldTour team just a couple of months ago, it came with a stated ambition of winning the Tour de France within the next five years. 18 stages into the first edition of this new era, and the AI systems powering this new era for the team may well be forced into some recalculations. Ineos have had a rotten Tour so far, and time is rapidly running out to salvage anything from it. The problems started well before the race began in Barcelona three weeks ago as the team’s leader, Oscar Onley, was sidelined through injury. With Carlos Rodríguez surprisingly non-selected and Kévin Vauquelin short of his top form, the GC leadership trio that was spoken about earlier in the year was non-existent. Instead, Ineos would chase stages, calling in their GC duo from the Giro d’Italia, Egan Bernal and Thymen Arensman, with Dorion Godon to try his hand in the sprints and Vauquelin to get up the road just about everywhere else. Add in the horsepower of Filippo Ganna and Josh Tarling, the experience of Michal Kwiatkowski, and the underlying quality of Tobias Foss, and there was no reason to be pessimistic. However, the opening team time trial was thrown off by a puncture, and things have hardly improved from there – in fact, punctures have been a recurring theme. Filippo Ganna’s fifth place on stage 9 is Ineos’ best individual result of the race so far, and Arensman’s farcical late-show to the stage 16 time trial seemed to say something about their Tour as a whole. “Even when I was a rider, I had some tough moments, but I wasn’t the type to just go home and wallow in my own sorrow and cry about it,” Geraint Thomas, the team’s Director of Racing told in Voiron at the start of stage 18. “You get up and you get a plan together and you go again.”He was speaking in the context of this Tour but also the season as a whole. After he did so, Bernal and Arensman made the break but would go on to finish in 47th and 48th place. That leaves three stages, with a double-header up at Alpe d’Huez followed by the punchy finale in Paris on Sunday. “The boys are still upbeat and positive and looking to get after it,” Thomas said. “We started on the back foot, and you’re up against it anyway at the Tour when you’re trying to go for stages, and you know there are only a few days when the breakaway is going to succeed anyway.“It’s tough when you don’t have an out-and-out sprinter, and you have Pogačar going for these mountain days. But all the boys are motivated, they want to try and get something for themselves and for the team. They’re ready to go after it and that’s all you can ask for really.”Thomas has been putting on this sort of brave face for many days now at this Tour de France, and while he knows it’s been a rough run, he insists the team is moving in the right direction when you zoom out. “For me, you can see it as like pressure and like ‘what if we don’t do this or that’, but we’re privileged to be involved in this sport. It’s sport at the end of the day, it doesn’t really matter, you know, we’re in the real world. We’re super lucky to be in this position, we’re ambitious, and we want to keep pushing forward. “If anything, this spurs us on to continue to improve because we have improved a lot since last year. Obviously this Tour has been a bit of a struggle at times, but I think when you look back at the season as whole, we’re definitely moving in the right direction. There are a lot of positives, it’s exciting. I just want to keep going now.”There’s a sense that Thomas is finding his stride now, nearly seven months after stepping into this management role following the conclusion of his illustrious racing career. “Since I’ve started I’ve been across a lot of things, just to get an understanding of how the team works. It’s all part of the process, and if I was to go back, I’d still do that because you still need that overview,” he said. “But now I can start to zone in, prioritise things, and spend more time on certain aspects. I think things will be better that way.”Whether Thomas can help steer Netcompany Ineos back towards somewhere approaching the sort of glories they enjoyed when he was a rider remains to be seen. In the meantime, a bad Tour can become a good Tour in a flash, so it’s not over until it’s over.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Sick soldiers and mixed messages – What's really going on at UAE Team Emirates-XRG and how will it change the last days of the Tour de France?
item.content:encoded: And just like that, the Tour de France changes again. We were only just getting accustomed to the tactical complexion of a race without Jonas Vingegaard but now, with two mountain stages remaining, we have the strongest team in the race, the team of the yellow jersey, dropping like flies. Brandon McNulty is in his hotel room booking a flight from Geneva airport. Adam Yates is battling a stomach issue and riding on empty. Tim Wellens has a sore throat and only just made the time cut on Thursday. And it also transpires that Florian Vermeersch is carrying an injury. That’s one man down for Tadej Pogačar, three below their best, and just three fully fit – as far as we know – going into a crucial Alpe d’Huez double header. The as ‘far as we know’ feels important here. This wave of illness and injury is dramatic enough, but UAE Team Emirates-XRG, in their efforts to play down the intrigue, have only hammed it up.Outside the team bus at the start of Thursday’s stage 18, both team boss Mauro Gianetti and sporting manager Joxean Fernández Matxin, played down the early rumours that other riders were coming down with illness. At the very same time, Wellens was in the mixed zone revealing to the Belgian media that he had a sore throat. Not only that, but Wellens’ stated that all riders ate separately in their rooms the previous night, at the same time as Gianetti was saying it was just Yates who was isolated.More remarkably still, at the finish in Orcières-Merlette, Matxin attempted to deny that Wellens was sick. Matxin’s English can be slightly unclear at times but this was unmistakable: “He’s not sick, he’s not sick.” He appeared to then claim it was normal that Wellens dropped back later in the stage to save his legs. That would be the same Tim Wellens who crawled to the line two minutes inside the time cut and told us: “It is pretty clear there is some illness in the team.”The mixed messages are bizarre, only adding to the sense that there’s something amiss and something to hide. Is it really respiratory issues for Wellens and McNulty? Is the stomach bug more widespread? The contradictions erode trust, and they also echo through the peloton.On the surface, it would seem that Pogačar is healthy, but already there are whispers about his pallor and a suggestion on French national television that he wore black shorts as it would be easier to hide a, let’s just say, stomach issue. Yes, this theory was not just confined to social media but seriously put forward by a former pro.And so, we go into a final double-header of mountain stages with the race not quite as wrapped up as we thought it was.Don’t get me wrong, the only way Pogačar loses this race is if he comes down with illness himself. Provided he stays relatively healthy, he can see this race through even if all his teammates go home. He has more than four minutes in hand and is so superior to his remaining rivals that he would surely be able to defend himself against anything thrown his way on the next two days.In this light, Jonas Vingegaard’s exit from the race on Sunday starts to feel like even more of a shame. If there was anyone capable of testing and exploiting that hint of weakness in the UAE Team Emirates camp it was the Dane and his Visma-Lease a Bike teammates.In his absence, you have a Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe) who has already stated he’s racing for second place and has lost the only teammate realistically capable of helping him in the mountains. You have Pogačar’s own teammate Isaac del Toro in third, and in fourth you have a 19-year-old, Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM), who is probably not looking beyond Del Toro’s podium spot and white jersey.Although it’s a long shot to envisage any sort of raids that truly threaten the yellow jersey, what we’re likely to see on the next two mountain stages is more open racing, and that starts with the breakaways.Stifled for much of this Tour by the collective might and will of UAE, George Bennett (NSN Cycling) said at the finish in Orcières-Merlette that the escape artists would now “smell blood”.A depleted UAE might struggle to control the breakaway as they have done in the past, which has two ramifications, the first being that they will have less of a grip on who exactly slips up the road, and the second that they will have less firepower to chase that break down to set up a stage win for Pogačar.Now, the 25-time stage winner with a four-minute lead doesn’t need stage wins, but the talk is that he has his sights firmly set on an Alpe d’Huez triumph to further cement his place in the legend of the Tour de France. That just became much harder to pull off.The real Alpe d’Huez comes on Friday on stage 19, but Saturday’s monstrosity over the Croix de Fer, Télégraphe, Galibier, and Sarenne is the truly pivotal stage from a general classification perspective. Any sign of weakness there will be punished in multiples, so while his teammates could continue to drop like flies, the threat of contagion hanging over Pogačar himself does inject a degree of jeopardy and tension back into this Tour de France.Suddenly, thanks in no small part to the race leader's own team, we have drama, we have intrigue, and we have a peloton that's determined to throw down some moves through these two last dances in the Alps.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Evenepoel Poised for Gripping Tour Showdown as Pogačar’s Team Fades
item.author: sstokes
item.content:encoded: Remco Evenepoel says he won’t take big risks heading into Alpe d’Huez, yet opportunities may arise with UAE under pressure.

item.title: 'Fight like a lion and fly like an eagle' - How Carlos Sastre's life changed when he won the Tour de France on L'Alpe d'Huez
item.content:encoded: So far there have been 29 winners on Alpe d'Huez in the Tour de France, and another name will be added to the list on Friday. But far fewer among that number - just three in fact since the Alpe was first tackled in 1952 - can say that when they reached the top of the climb's 21 curves, they were also en route to victory in the race overall."If you ask me how much it means to win on Alpe d'Huez, there's an easy way to explain that," says Carlos Sastre, who claimed the outright victory in Paris just 48 hours after he had conquered the climb in the 2008 Tour. Having retired in 2011, Sastre still feels the power of that accomplishment. "It means that 18 years later, I'm still getting calls from journalists like you, asking me about it. That tells you how much it matters. And I'm still getting emotional talking to you about it, too."For Sastre, victory on Alpe d'Huez arguably marked the high point of his professional career. After dropping the field at the foot of the climb and then riding the whole ascent alone, he also had enough of an advantage at the summit over favourite Cadel Evans to defend his lead in the Tour's closing time trial. But as Sastre also says, the memory of the victory on Alpe d'Huez, more than two minutes ahead of fellow Spaniard Samuel Sanchez, was in itself indelible."It's famous for being a really tough climb, and rightly so. Any really spectacular high mountain achievement in cycling needs a spectacular ascent to happen, and Alpe d'Huez certainly fits the bill," Sastre says."And this year obviously it depends on how they tackle it, but on a short, punchy stage like Friday's, for sure they'll go flat out to the foot of the climb. There'll be those who are going for the breakaway, and others who want some GC success, so that kind of distance does help the action."But what makes it so hard, no matter what happens before, is that Alpe d'Huez has a very similar kind of gradient all the way up, which means that there are no breaks in the steepness at all."On top of that, you reach the climb after a very flat run-in, and then suddenly it kicks up to 13%, 14%. It's very unforgiving, and it really benefits the out-and-out climbers because the gradient doesn't change."They can use that constant steepness both to accelerate hard, and then to open up a gap as well, on any of the non-specialists. It's like Alpe d'Huez was made for mountain climbers to have a real chance."Sastre first tackled Alpe d'Huez in the 2001 Tour, and he has ridden up it around 15 times in total, he says. He's been up it both in training when the climb is completely deserted, and in the 2004 Tour, when what is believed to be the biggest number of fans ever on the ascent, estimated at around 1.5 million people, lined the route."There's a really fantastic atmosphere, that's for sure, but I wouldn't say it was so different to the Tour in general. These days things are much more widely followed than they used to be, even on the quietest stage."Of course there are a few idiots who want to get on telly by causing crashes, but the vast majority on the climb are people who just enjoy cycling, who get really emotional when they see the riders go past, and I vividly remember how special it was, too, back in 2004, when we had the mountain time trial there. It's something magical."As for the best tactics to get up the climb, Sastre says it depends too much on each rider, and their goals on the day, to give a general rule, but in his own case in 2008 en route to yellow, "I just went for it, right from the bottom. I knew it was my best chance to win outright and I took it."I knew I had to get as much time on [key rival] Cadel Evans as possible because there was the time trial afterwards, so I went for it as soon as I could."I didn't use my power meter. I could see the number of kilometres left on my bike computer, but I didn't pay any attention to either. I just based it all on energy, experience and my level of motivation to try and win the stage. And to try and win the Tour."The hardest part, Sastre says, was right at the start, when he attacked but was shadowed by multiple Grand Tour winner Denis Menchov. That was not ideal for either him or the team, but fortunately Menchov made some kind of movement or gesture that Sastre knew was a sign the Russian's energy was ebbing, so he attacked him again."And that was it. The first four kilometres were very difficult because the guys behind were only 15 seconds further back for quite a while. But finally my effort began to pay off and [team director Bjarne] Riis came up in the team car, and he said, 'Carlitos, you've got them at 30 seconds now'."Then it went up to a minute, then it was even more, and that was when I said to myself - heck, I'm on my way now. That's when being an out-and-out climber turned into a real advantage, and by the summit, I had two minutes on them all."It's often commented that riders will tick off the 21 hairpins as a way of motivating themselves on Alpe d'Huez, but Sastre said that he was not doing that at all."I wasn't there to count curves, and I wasn't there to count the number of fans on the side of the road, either," Sastre said. "I was just focused 100% on my effort."A coach friend of mine had said to me in English before the stage - 'Today you are going to fight like a lion and fly like an eagle' - and that's all I was thinking about, to tell you the truth."I kept that in mind, and I was suffering. But there comes a point when your suffering fuses with your performance. You know it hurts, but at the same time you know you're getting more and more time on the riders behind. And that is something that feels like magic."But that's how it has got to be. Every morning of my life as soon as I've got up, it's been tough going, and some people might say they've felt great on the bike, but I've suffered even when I've won. So that feeling you get" - gaining time on the rivals on a climb - "that's a real privilege."As for what Alpe d'Huez means to him now, Sastre first points to the fact that journalists like are still calling him 18 years on, but then he goes a whole lot deeper on those feelings."To me it's everything, I'm still getting emotional talking to you about it. You remember it all your life."Alpe d'Huez is one of those epic, emblematic climbs that only a few of us have had the chance to be able to win there. In my case, not just that, but also take the yellow jersey and take it all the way to Paris."So for me, Alpe d'Huez is the place where I've lived through some magical moments. It's my magic mountain."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. item.title: 'Big shock to the system' - Former WorldTour rider Veronica Ewers resumes slow return to peloton with 'experiment' at French ProTeam's Tour de France Femmes training camp
item.author: jackie.tyson@futurenet.com (Jackie Tyson)
item.content:encoded: Veronica Ewers, a former WorldTour rider with success at Grand Tours and one-day races, will watch this year's Tour de France Femmes on television, with her family in Moscow, Idaho. She's eager to watch the action unfold, but competing in the fast-paced peloton with climbs of Mont Ventoux and Col d'Eze are still far off her radar.The closest she'll get to the Tour was riding three weeks with St Michel-Preference Home-Auber93 at their recent training camp to prepare for the Tour de France Femmes, August 1-9. The invitation was an easy 'yes' for Ewers, as the French ProTeam travelled to Colorado for riding at high elevation plus nutrition and hydration analysis with sponsor Skratch Labs, based in Boulder.The accomplished US rider continues with a planned full year away from racing to recover from REDs, Relative Energy Deficiency. This is now her second chance to improve after resting for just a few months in 2024. In December, she 'paused' for a substantial time, with EF Education-Oatly releasing her from her contract and Ewers moving back home to Idaho from a base in Spain."I think I was a bit burned out by the end of 2025, and that was because I had been in such a contradictory state of trying to recover and trying to perform at the same time, and now also that I'm in a better mental space from taking time away," she told this week."I mean, physically I'm still in the recovery process. I'm coming to the end of it, I think, and working with my dietitian Renee McGregor has been incredibly helpful. I've made a lot of headway in that front. And this opportunity kind of came at maybe not the most ideal time with my recovery, but ultimately was a positive experience."Ewers joined US compatriots Heidi Franz and Caroline Wreszin at the camp from July 6-21 for "incredibly hard training" in the front range of the Colorado Rockies. Also on the St Michel squad is Canadian Alison Jackson, who was a teammate with Ewers during her two seasons at EF Education-Oatly and one year at the TIBCO team before it shut down after 2023. Camp for the St Michel team, and for several guest riders including Ewers, took on the thin air and steep mountain roads, but also was about developing techniques for fueling and recovery that included collaborative time with Colorado University at Boulder. She said it was the most she had ridden a bike since last winter."This group of girls and being on the bike again and having fun on the bike really reminded me of my love for the sport and has really motivated me to continue this recovery process. Yeah, hopefully I'll get back out there on the bike in the WorldTour," Ewers admitted.The 31-year-old rocketed onto the pro cycling scene five years ago when she was a rider for the US Pacific Northwest club team, Fount Cycling Guild. She went from podiums at the Tour of Walla Walla to a bronze medal at the USPro Road Race Nationals. Her ride in Knoxville, Tennessee at US Road National Championships saw her almost out-sprint Coryn Rivera for second place, and she showed pure power against other proven riders including Kristen Faulkner, Leah Thomas and Taylor Wiles. Team TIBCO-Silicon Valley Bank wasted no time in adding her to their roster, which featured USPro road winner Lauren Stephens and Faulkner.She continued to use her prowess with climbing to rise rapidly, with success during two seasons at the WorldTour level, including a stage win and second on GC at Festival Elsy Jacobs in 2022. By 2023, she was on the podium at the Giro d'Italia Women and finished fourth overall. She called her second-place to winner Elisa Longo Borghini on stage 4 that year, sprinting ahead of Annemiek van Vleuten, a career highlight. However, the signals of REDs were not easily detectable to the former collegiate soccer player who began racing in 2019. Now she's in tune with her body, and the camp was a successful "experiment"."My numbers improved incredibly just within two weeks; I was a bit shocked. I mean, my VO2 improved by almost 10 units, which is pretty insane," Ewers said, clearly amazed by her tone."I'm coming from doing very, very little and joining this camp where the other ladies are at peak form right now. I was able to be there and not be harsh on myself. We would do some race simulations, and I was sticking in it."I think seeing that I took the time off that my body and my brain really needed, yeah, just shows that I have all of this capacity to get back to that top level at some point, and being in a healthier body is just gonna make me much stronger, both physically and mentally."There was no time for celebration yet, as she still has more recovery, plus studies to continue for a Kinesiology master's degree. But she's ready to watch the Tour de France Femmes and see how her friends at St Michel do in this fifth edition."It's going to be so much more dynamic than the men's race."Support our unrivalled coverage of the Tour de France Femmes and women's cycling by subscribing to Cyclingnews. In return, you get unlimited access to high-quality journalism, with breaking news, race analysis, and expert insight from inside the women's peloton. Plus, the Cyclingnews app lets you follow the action wherever you go!

item.title: What to know about Tour de France nighttime testing – The rules, the results timeline, the ITA, reactions and more explained
item.content:encoded: There hasn't been a major doping scandal at the for over a decade, but the decision to carry out nighttime anti-doping tests on multiple riders, including Tadej Pogačar and Jonas Vingegaard, at the 2026 Tour has resurrected the ghosts of the past, sparked a myriad of questions and huge debate about cycling's anti-doping strategy and whether the riders are treated fairly.Pogačar shrugged off the disruption to his sleep but also described the nighttime testing as 'inhumane' and went so far as to suggest that Vingegaard's crash was sparked by his loss of sleep.Since then, CPA rider's association president Adam Hansen has riders should pay so much for such disruptive anti-doping testing, while blew up the internet with a rare but succinct post on X."I’ll roll this grenade down the aisle: 2 am testing is one of the very effective ways to prevent peptide(EPO,hGH) and testosterone microdosing. If UCI had tested us in 2001 at 2 am? We’d all have been suspended and saved the sport 25 years of drama," the EF Education team manager said, in defence of nighttime testing.This is ' explainer of pro cycling's anti-doping system and what the recent nighttime test of the Tour de France means for the sport.Cycling's eternal doping problemEver since riders began to race long distances for glory, prize money and sponsors, doping has been part of their performances and so a threat to the fair results and image of the sport.The Tour de France pioneers drank cognac, used chloroform and took cocaine and other stimulants to dull the pain of racing."The truth is that we keep going on dynamite," Francis Pélissier once famously told French writer and journalist Albert Londres.Doping was made illegal in France in 1965, but doping scandals continued, as doping moved from amphetamines to steroids, blood doping, EPO abuse and the Lance Armstrong years and his eventual lifetime ban and the cancellation of his seven Tour de France victories. The Tour de France results have been rewritten many times over the years.Who manages the anti-doping process?For many years, the UCI managed anti-doping in the sport, but this arrangement was widely criticised as a conflict of interest, amid reports that some positive tests were not adequately pursued and long-standing concerns about the governing body's independence.The International Olympic Committee pushed for an independent body to distance itself from doping scandals, and so the International Testing Agency (ITA) was created in 2018 and began to work with International Federations and major sporting events, including the summer and winter Olympic Games.The ITA Cycling unit was created in 2021 and absorbed the UCI anti-doping operations. ITA Cycling had an initial budget of €7 million, paid for by riders via a 2.7% of all race prize money. The UCI also pays a contribution, while race organisers pay a percentage of their prize pot and teams pay a flat fee of €215,000.The ITA budget has risen to €10 million in recent years, with the UCI highlighting a 35% budget increase between 2021 and the end of 2024.In 2026, ITA took responsibility for anti-doping legal proceedings regarding anti-doping violations and whereabouts failures. This is alongside their operational anti-doping testing, the monitoring of athlete biological passports and intelligence-gathering and investigations.Anti-doping at the 2026 Tour de FranceBefore the 2026 Tour de France, ITA announced it carried out 360 out-of-competition tests in the month before the race, with 600 in-competition tests expected during the race, based on testing strategy, intelligence and investigations priorities and operational requirements.ITA said it would put an increased focus on the endogenous steroid markers measured in blood serum as part of the steroidal module of the Athlete Biological Passport and on the endocrine module of the ABP to better detect markers of human Growth Hormone (hGH) abuse.“The Tour de France remains one of the most visible and demanding events in international sport from an anti-doping perspective," ITA Director General Benjamin Cohen said."Our 2026 program reflects the continued evolution of cycling’s anti-doping strategy through intelligence-led testing, scientific advancements, strengthened investigative capabilities and close cooperation with our partners. We remain committed to protecting the integrity of the race and ensuring that all riders compete under the same rules and conditions."Being selected for a test, including out-of-hours, is a standard part of anti-doping controls and does not imply any suspicion of, or wrongdoing by, the riders concerned. Neither Pogačar nor Vingegaard nor the other riders who have been tested have been accused of any anti-doping violation.ITA stated that testing would be targeted and may occur at any point during the three-week race, not just at stage finishes. However, nobody expected the headline-grabbing, disruptive and divisive nighttime tests.One trusted source told that nighttime testing had only ever been done once before. Suddenly the two biggest Tour riders were awoken for testing between two key mountain stages.News of the testing only emerged after a report in and subsequent confirmation by Pogačar and Vingegaard.understands that other teams have been visited for testing outside of the usual 6:00 a.m. to 11:00 p.m. window, which is not typical but which is provided for within the UCI's rules.The WADA code and the UCI anti-doping rules are clear, with UCI rule 5.2.2 stating: "The UCI may require any Rider over whom it has Testing authority, who has not retired, including Riders serving a period of Ineligibility, to provide a Sample at any time and at any place."However, a comment below the rule specifies: "The UCI may obtain additional authority to conduct Testing by means of bilateral or multilateral agreements with other Signatories. Unless the Rider has identified a sixty (60) minute Testing window between the hours of 11:00 p.m. and 6:00 a.m., or has otherwise consented to Testing during that period, the UCI will not test a Rider during that period unless it has a serious and specific suspicion that the Rider may be engaged in doping. A challenge to whether the UCI had sufficient suspicion for Testing during this time period shall not be a defense to an anti-doping rule violation based on such test or attempted test."ITA carried out the nighttime tests within the UCI rules. As noted above, being selected for a test - including out-of-hours - does not in itself imply any suspicion of, or wrongdoing by, the riders concerned.What is the French law for nighttime testing?The Tour de France is held under UCI rules, but French law and nighttime tests are also permitted under specific circumstances.It is important to distinguish between the UCI’s own testing rules — which permit out-of-hours testing where there is “serious and specific suspicion” — and the separate French judicial authorisation process under the Code for Sport. The two regimes operate independently, and the fact that either procedure was invoked does not constitute a finding or allegation that any rider has committed an anti-doping rule violation.Under the French Code for Sport L232-14-4, an investigative judge 'des libertés et de la détention' (freedoms and detention) "verifies in all cases that there are serious and consistent suspicions against the athlete that he has contravened or will contravene the provisions of this chapter and a risk of disappearance of evidence." This provision governs judicial authorisation for testing where a rider's consent is not obtained, rather than defining the 11 p.m.-6 a.m. window itself, which sits in the related article L232-14-1.Vaughters suggested that a French judge may sign off permission for nighttime testing based on little concrete evidence, but so far the details of ITA's legal request to test have not been revealed, citing the confidentiality of their anti‑doping strategy."The timing of a control is determined through a risk-based assessment that takes into account a range of relevant factors and information. It is therefore not arbitrary, but nor should it be interpreted as evidence that a rider has committed an anti-doping rule violation," ITA told .Of course, there is a clear difference and long legal process between any "serious and specific suspicion" and a confirmed "anti-doping rule violation" that usually ends with a suspension.ITA claims its Tour de France anti-doping programme "features a targeted, intelligence-led testing plan grounded in the latest scientific developments and risk-based analysis.. However, on Monday's second rest day, they also carried out a mass testing of the peloton as part of the Athlete Biological Passport (ABP) programme.All riders in the peloton were included in the testing mission, which forms part of the ITA’s ongoing intelligence - and risk-based anti-doping program for the race," ITA said in another statement."Collecting additional blood samples enables the ITA to assess variations in relevant haematological biological markers and strengthen the longitudinal monitoring of each rider’s individual profile.""The UCI of course acknowledges that nighttime testing can disrupt riders' rest and recovery, and wishes to reiterate that carrying out such controls outside normal hours remains an exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances. While fully aware of how onerous such a measure is for riders, the UCI nevertheless underscores that these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping.The Tour de France climbs into the Alps and then ends in Paris on Sunday.Anti-doping tests usually take between seven and ten days to be analysed, with positive cases taking much longer due to the legal process and disciplinary process.In theory, we should know the initial outcome of the nighttime tests before the Tour de France ends on Sunday evening. It will be up to the athletes involved to decide if they will immediately reveal the results of the tests.The debate about the fairness and effectiveness of nighttime testing will surely run on much longer.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: 'I was on people's blacklists' - Tom Pidcock absent from Tour de France breakaway on first day in the Alps
item.author: jackie.tyson@futurenet.com (Jackie Tyson)
item.content:encoded: When breakaway opportunities have beckoned in the second half of this year's Tour de France, Tom Pidcock (Pinarello-Q36.5) has consistently pounded the pedals to cash in for a payday. However, by stage 18 there were no more surprises for his rivals, and the brash British rider could not escape."It wasn't possible to get in the break, no one wanted me there. I already knew from before the race that I was on people's blacklists, not gonna get in the break," Pidcock told broadcasters at the finish Thursday.A breakaway did get away on the first of three days in the Alps, but there was no representative from Pinarello-Q36.5. Instead, mountains classification points were a magnet on a climbing-heavy day for Valentin Paret-Peintre (Soudal-QuickStep) and Richard Carapaz (EF Education-EasyPost), with the Frenchman edging one point away from KOM leader Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) and Carapaz grabbing the second Tour stage win of his career."Not an easy day, but the last time was fairly controlled I think. But when Carapaz is up the road, this guy, I have too much respect to this guy to give him free time, you know? You don't give Carapaz free time," Pidcock said."I wanted to try one more time. But I guess now I just focus on the GC until Paris."On stage 13, Pidcock moved into fourth in the general classification with a podium finish in Belfort, moving to within 4:15 of the yellow jersey Pogačar. It was a short-lived gain, as he slipped back to eighth the next day as Pogačar pulverised the breakaway and the entire peloton with a solo win, riding 4:30 to his closest competitor and 7:59 ahead of the Briton. So what is a fierce competitor to do? Pidcock went back to work on stage 15 to make his third breakaway of the week, though it only produced penalties for pushing off against a car, not once but twice. That cost him some Swiss francs, KOM points but also 20 seconds in the GC.Those 20 seconds would only place him one spot higher that his current eighth place in the GC as of today, as he is eight seconds behind Lenny Martinez (Bahrain-Victorious). He has 6:07 between him and a spot on the podium, third place occupied by Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates-XRG), and then another 6:51 to Pogačar.Pidcock noticed that UAE Team Emirates-XRG were not firing on all cylinders headed to the final two days in the Alps on Friday and Saturday for double ascents of Alpe d'Huez. While the team led three classifications - overall, mountains and youth - several of their dependable climbers had come down with sickness, namely Brandon McNulty who withdrew during stage 18. Also suffering from health issues were Adam Yates and Tim Wellens."It's not good for them. They definitely weren't in a good way today," Pidcock said about UAE."But I think to be honest, these next two days, it's about the other teams fighting for the podium, so I think UAE might not have that much work to do."Pidcock has his own work to do, perhaps taking a chance on one day to the top of Alpe d'Huez, as he was the last rider to win on the iconic mountain at the Tour de France, scoring the stage 12 victory four years ago.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'An injury is holding me back' - Florian Vermeersch injury compounds UAE Team Emirates-XRG woes at Tour de France
item.content:encoded: A fresh setback at UAE Team Emirates-XRG for Tour de France leader Tadej Pogačar's teammates emerged after Thursday's summit finish stage when support rider Florian Vermeersch said that he has been fighting the effects of an unspecified injury.Pogačar is close to claiming a record-equalling fifth victory in the Tour and has a lead of over four and half minutes on closest rival Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), but two massive mountain stages remain between him and the overall triumph on Sunday in Paris and the illnesses and injuries amongst his teammates continue to stack up."I didn't have my best day. I'm struggling a bit with an injury and that is holding me back. Although it is relative, because I have seen that other teammates have suffered more," Vermeersch, 31:52 down on stage winner Richard Carapaz (EF Education-EasyPost) and 113th at the finish line, told .Vermeersch, a hugely talented Classics specialist brought to the Tour by UAE Team Emirates mainly to work in the first half of stages and on less hilly terrain, would never have been expected to finish higher up at a category 1 summit finish like Orcières-Merlette.A fourth UAE rider, 2023 Tour podium finisher and high mountains expert Adam Yates, is also on the backfoot, having suffered badly on stages 16 and 17, although Pogačar said on Thursday that the British climber was now improving and should be OK for Saturday's crunch day through the Alps.Vermeersch was keen too, to show that at the very least he was not ill, telling "I am not sick. I feel good, fortunately. So that is not a problem for me." The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'I couldn't stop thinking about this stage' - non-conformist Richard Carapaz makes the most of his last clear opportunity to seize Tour de France stage victory
item.content:encoded: Orcières-Merlette is famous in cycling for being the scene of one of the greatest ever victories in the history of the Tour de France, when way back in 1971, Luis Ocaña's stubborn refusal to accept the seemingly perpetual domination of Eddy Merckx finally bore dividends with a spectacular stage win - and the yellow jersey.At Le Lioran on stage 9, the Ecuadorian racer had been the one rider willing to try to wreck Tadej Pogacar's chances of victory, attacking on the penultimate climb with a solo move before the Slovenian charged away alone and blasted past.Stage 18, though, was another story altogether, with Carapaz determined to make it onto the front on Thursday's rugged approach to the final ascent of Orcières-Merlette in a move that - just like Ocaña's meticulously planned move 55 years ago - was very carefully planned from the outset.Not only did the 33-year-old manage to achieve his goal of getting in the break, but his blistering series of attacks on the steadily rising last climb also succeeded in sinking the five other riders who stayed away to the finale, netting him the ninth Grand Tour win of his career in the process.There were notable side benefits, too, to his second Tour stage victory in three years, with Carapaz both edging into the top ten overall and moving up to third overall in the mountains ranking thanks to his triumph. "I couldn't stop thinking about this stage during the Tour; I felt certain that it was one that suited me, and when I felt I had the legs this morning, I decided to really go for it," he said."I knew I could be in the game, so when QuickStep tried to bridge across to an earlier move with three riders, I was ready to go."The final climb wasn't great for me, to be honest, but I had the legs, and that was what mattered. From then on, it was just a question of waiting for the right distance."Unable to race in the Giro d'Italia, his prime objective of 2026, because of an operation on a cyst, Carapaz turned things round in time to go for stage wins in the Tour, with his second place overall in Switzerland behind Pogačar a promising sign. Third behind Pogačar on Les Angles was another boost to his morale, and so too was eighth at Le Markstein. But clinching his first victory since a Giro stage in 2025 remained an unachieved target - until today.A repeat of his 2024 assault on the polka-dot jersey is possible, given there are now just seven points between himself and Tadej Pogacar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG). But in any case, as he explained afterwards the main goal on Thursday was making the most of one of the last chances for the non-GC contenders to shine."There was no pressure, but I knew the next two days will be much harder to succeed, so today was a very important stage for us to try to get the win, and we managed it," Carapaz explained."I didn't want to try too hard for the mountain points on the preceding climbs, and the QuickStep rider" - Valentin Paret-Peintre, now second overall in the ranking - "was faster.""But the real high mountain stages are still to come, and I think Tadej has a lot of points too."If I can get the polka-dot jersey, that'd be great, so I will try for it from now on. But today was all about the stage." Carapaz first tested the waters some 4.4 kilometres from the summit, but even if that move was brought back, it turned out to be a move to test his rivals' strength rather than an all-out assault. That came just a kilometre later, and this time, nobody was able to answer."Getting a stage here is very tricky and compared to two years ago when I last won" - at Superdévoluy, not far from Orcières-Merlette, and also the last opportunity in the third week before the final setpiece GC showdown stages - "the terrain was similar, lots of flat in the middle and then some climbs at the end.""But there was a lot of quality in that final break, Mauro [Schmid] had already won a stage, and I respected them. It was a great day, and our group worked well together. But I had the legs to finish it off."Having conquered his primary objective, Carapaz will now look, just as he did in 2024, to taking home the mountains jersey for a second time, but he was realistic about his chances of another stage win being very low."Today was a breakaway day, and we'll see how UAE play it on the next two stages, as a lot of riders want to win on Alpe d'Huez. I never had that interest in GC; I actually lost time on purpose to have more chance of being in the break, but the mountains jersey is a goal, and we'll see what we can do about that. We'll keep trying."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. item.title: 'There are bodies everywhere' – Tadej Pogačar plays down wave of illness within UAE camp at Tour de France
item.content:encoded: However, it soon became clear that was not the case, as and Tim Wellens both started to struggle early in the stage to the Orcières-Merlette ski resort. McNulty did not finish the stage, becoming the first rider down for UAE at this Tour, while Wellens only narrowly made the time cut as the last rider over the line. “I’m really sad that today Brandon felt so bad and could not finish. We will miss him the next two days,” Pogačar said in his yellow jersey press conference after the stage.“But yeah, Felix [Grossschartner] is riding super well, Nils [Politt] also, Isaac [Del Toro] is amazing. Adam is getting better, Tim also. If I put on him a bit of motivation then he always grabs it and goes through all the pain. Florian [Vermeersch] can be helpful from the start.”The Tour now heads for a double dose of Alpe d’Huez, with Friday’s stage 19 taking the traditional hairpinned route up the famous mountain and Saturday’s monstrous stage approaching via the Col de Sarenne after three other high-mountain ascents. “Tomorrow will be go with the flow, we survive tomorrow the stage, try to come to Alpe d’Huez and see what we can do,” Pogačar said.“I think we can manage. It’s the third week, all the teams are a bit suffering. There are bodies everywhere, on a stage like today already. We will keep fighting, and I believe we can do it.“Adam is getting better, but the problem is only he cannot refuel fast enough,” he added on . “He’s going to be okay, maybe not tomorrow, a big performance of Adam, but hopefully Saturday will be pretty good.”The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: "You're not allowed to make a difference" – Verstappen says the fastest F1 drivers are penalised
'Doing half the watts' – Tim Wellens avoids time cut elimination by two minutes as illness ravages UAE Team Emirates-XRG at Tour de France
item.content:encoded: UAE Team Emirates-XRG's Tim Wellens made the time cut by just two minutes on stage 18 of the Tour de France, battling through the mountainous 185.2 kilometre stage to avoid elimination.Wellens was the very last rider to finish the stage to Orcières-Merlette alone, 43:02 down on the winner. He was close to missing the time cut, which was 45:17. He spent much of the day in the gruppetto, but had to complete the final climb alone."I had a lot of help in the gruppetto and then on the climbs I just had a time trial by myself, doing half the watts, normally I go double the speed," Wellens explained to and others at the finish of stage 18.Even if he is struggling, UAE will have breathed a sigh of relief that Wellens just made it, having already lost one important rider on Thursday when Brandon McNulty dropped out mid-stage.Concerns of illness in the UAE team have been swirling since Adam Yates struggled on stage 17, reportedly suffering stomach problems the night before the stage 16 time trial. The team said that his stomach illness hadn't spread, but other riders clearly had other illnesses, with McNulty and Wellens both reportedly feeling sick at the start of stage 18."I think it's pretty clear there's some sickness in the team," Wellens said. "Fortunately Tadej is OK and I hope it stays that way."The extent of the problem at UAE was clear by the fact that Pogačar finished stage 18 with only one rider, Isaac del Toro, in the GC group with him. Teams like Decathlon CMA CGM, Lidl-Trek and Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe all finished with their leader and one teammate in that group, so though UAE weren't worse off than any other team, we're accustomed to them being markedly stronger.Though Yates is reportedly feeling better, he also had a tough day on the bike, finishing 28:55 down.Pogačar currently leads the Tour by five minutes, so his GC is not at immediate risk with two mountain stages remaining, but illness in the UAE camp is still arguably a cause for concern.UAE sports manager Matxin Joxean Fernandez downplayed that angle, though, trying to calm any worries ahead of the Alpe d'Huez doubleheader on Friday and Saturday."I think it's different. Today was a breakaway day, so it was better to recover and other teams controlled the stage," he said. "Florian was dropped in the normal, when a group of sixty riders were left. Adam decided to stay calm."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. item.title: Russell: Data shows software calibration behind recent F1 struggles, not driving style
How to watch Tour de France Stage 19: All the broadcasters and live streams for opening ascent of Alpe d'Huez
item.content:encoded: Watch Stage 19 of the Tour de France as the race reaches it's second summit finish in as many days atop Alpe d'Huez.Stage 18 saw Richard Carapaz claim the second Tour de France stage victory of his career, while the general classification remained unchanged.The iconic Alpe d'Huez takes centre stage today, with this short 127.9km stage packing in more than 3,500m of climbing. The riders first tackle the Col Bayard (4.7km at 7.2%) and the Col du Noyer (7.2km at 8.5%) before the decisive finale, featuring the Col d'Ornon (5.4km at 6.4%) and the Hors Catégorie ascent of Alpe d'Huez (13.8km at 8.1%).The legendary climb's 21 hairpin bends were last conquered by Tom Pidcock in 2022, and a repeat triumph would be magical for the Briton. However, Tadej Pogačar starts as the overwhelming favourite. The Slovenian will be eager to add an Alpe d'Huez stage victory to his glittering palmarès while tightening his grip on the yellow jersey.So read on as we provide all the streaming details you need to watch Stage 19 of the Tour de France, including free viewing options.Watch Stage 19 of Tour de France 2026 for freeStage 19 of the Tour de France is free to watch in multiple countries across the world.In Australia, you can watch for free on SBS on Demand with English commentary. Use to unlock your free stream — more on that below.Not in your usual country for stage 19? A VPN can help you keep up with the action.VPNs (Virtual Private Networks) can change your device’s location, allowing you to bypass geo-restrictions on streaming services and access your usual coverage from anywhere. item.title: Tour de France Stage 18: Carapaz Gives EF Education-Easypost A Huge Tour Stage Win
item.author: sstokes
item.content:encoded: EF Education EasyPost rider Richard Carapaz strongest on the final climb to solo to victory, Matteo Jorgenson third.

item.title: Tour de France: Richard Carapaz flies solo across the final kilometres to land stunning mountaintop victory on stage 18
item.author: cyclingnews@futurenet.com (Dani Ostanek)
item.content:encoded: Richard Carapaz (EF Education-EasyPost) soloed to his second career Tour de France stage win as the race entered the Alps on stage 18 with a summit finish at Orcières-Merlette.The Ecuadorian, who two years ago won at Superdévoluy and took the mountain classification, proved the strongest man from a group of six breakaway survivors on the final climb of the 185.2km stage.He jumped clear 3.5km from the top, leaving behind mountain classification competitor Valentin Paret-Peintre (Soudal-QuickStep), Matteo Jorgenson (Visma-Lease a Bike), and Mauro Schmid (Jayco-AlUla) to race alone to the finish line.The four, along with the already dropped Tobias Halland Johannessen (Uno-X Mobility) and Raúl García Pierna (Movistar), had been among a 40-rider breakaway move which went clear with 120km to run of the stage.Paret-Peintre, wearing the polka dot jersey despite lying second in the classification behind race leader Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG), had collected the majority of the mountain points along the way, but Carapaz was the best climber on the day. He proved that on the closing 7.1km, 6.7% climb, with one move at 4km to go dislodging Johannessen and García Pierna before his second, which led to the big win.He'd cross the line 45 seconds up on stage 13 winner Schmid, while Jorgenson completed the podium close behind and Paret-Peintre finished fourth over a minute down.With two tough mountain stages finishing at L'Alpe d'Huez lying in wait on Friday and Saturday, the only change in GC action was Carapaz moving to 10th. On the climb to the finish, Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe led home a select group of contenders, 4:35 down on Carapaz."It's been a very special moment to win here on the Tour. It's something that you have to put everything into. I want to dedicate it to my family," Carapaz said after the stage."It's incredible to win stage 18. It's a really tough day. I managed to jump across when there was a gap. It was a really tough time, but I waited for the right moment and in the end, it happened to be at the right time."We knew what we were coming for, we knew what we had to do, and today ended up being that special day. We ultimately have managed to realise what we wanted for the last few weeks. We are really happy."All the big names finished together, including the UAE pairing, yellow jersey Pogačar and white jersey Isaac del Toro. The team will head into stage 19 without key rider Brandon McNulty, though, after the US rider was forced to abandon the Tour ill, having raced much of the stage alone ahead of the broom wagon.Pogačar continues in the race lead, 4:32 up on Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), while Del Toro remains in third at 6:51. The Slovenian also hangs on to the polka dot jersey by one point, his 70 points still clear of Paret-Peintre's new total of 69, while Carapaz is third at 63.Green jersey holder Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek), meanwhile, has extended his points lead after racing in the breakaway and surviving over multiple climbs to grab 25 points at the intermediate sprint. He now leads Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) with a total of 477 points to 445.The 18th stage of the Tour de France saw the race head to the Alps and a finish at Orcières-Merlette, with 185.2km of challenging hilly roads on the way to the first-category climb (7.1km at 6.7%).3,900 metres of ascent filled the route from Voiron, including the early first-category Côte d'Engins (11.4km at 5.4%), the second-category Côte de Monteynard (9.7km at 5%) in the middle of the stage, and a pair of third-category hills on the run to the final climb.As expected, with the top GC riders set to save their legs for the coming Alpe d'Huez stages, there was a massive battle for the breakaway right from the start of the day.A host of riders, including breakaway staple Baptiste Veistroffer (Lotto-Intermarché) were part of the attacks and counter-moves early in the day. Meanwhile, UAE Team Emirates-XRG lieutenant Brandon McNulty was one surprising name to drop from the peloton after just 25km.Mountain classification competitors Carapaz and Paret-Peintre were among those battling to make the breakaway, attacking repeatedly as the Frenchman led the Ecuadorian over the summit of the Côte d'Engins to grab 10 mountain points.Other notable names striving to break away included Jorgenson, Carapaz's teammate Ben Healy, points classification leader Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek), and former Tour winner Egan Bernal (Netcompany Ineos).In the end, it would take around 65km for a breakaway group to go clear as a group of around 40 riders got away before the Côte de Monteynard.Paret-Peintre and Carapaz made the move along with Healy, Pedersen, Bernal, and Jorgenson, while selected others to get away included Tobias Halland Johannessen, Jayco-AlUla duo Mauro Schmid, Michael Matthews, Damiano Caruso (Bahrain Victorious), Romain Grégoire (Groupama-FDJ United), Harold Tejada (XDS-Astana), Michał Kwiatkowski, Thymen Arensman (Netcompany Ineos), Alex Baudin (EF Education-EasyPost), Quinn Simmons (Lidl-Trek), and Movistar duo Einer Rubio and Raúl García Pierna.The move split apart on the Monteynard, however, with Paret-Peintre going on the attack once more for another five mountain points with Carapaz right behind. The pair were part of a 14-rider lead group down the other side, with Pedersen, Jorgenson, and Halland Johannessen also in there.The group carried a three-minute gap to the peloton into the closing 100km, while the second part of the breakaway – those dropped by the attackers – lay 1:30 down.The road to the next climb, the third-category Côte de Terrasses (3.4km at 6.6%), saw the break's lead grow to six minutes as once again Paret-Peintre accelerated to grab another two points ahead of Carapaz.Pedersen, meanwhile, was hanging in there at the front in anticipation of his finish line, the day's intermediate sprint at Corps with 56km to go. With the Dane's teammate Mathias Vacek having piloted the group to that point, Pedersen duly jumped out of the group to grab 25 points and build his green jersey lead to 32 points over Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech).His race was run at that point, but the fight for the stage win was still on, with the leaders lying 1:20 up on the chasers and eight minutes clear of the peloton.It wouldn't take long for the attacking to start again, with Jorgenson leading the way with 47km to go. The American took Johannessen, García Pierna, and Jayco-AlUla pairing Mauro Schmid and Mauro Engelhardt with him as Paret-Peintre and Carapaz launched the chase behind.The new lead group of five raced away to a margin of 20 seconds over those left behind, even if Engelhardt didn't last long out front, though the two mountain classification competitors continued their chase.Paret-Peintre and Carapaz made it six at the front again with 44km to go, with the new leaders looking set to fight for the stage win among themselves.The group quickly built a lead over the remainder of the first part of the early break, and the gap hit the minute mark as they raced into the closing 34km of the day.Only the third-category climb of the Côte de Saint-Léger-les-Mélèzes (2.5km at 6.9%) lay between the leaders and the final climb, and it was there where García Pierna showed the first sign of weakness among the six. The Spaniard dropped just before the climb but made it back in before the summit, where Paret-Peintre grabbed another two mountain points.Meanwhile, at the rear of the race, McNulty, who had been riding alone in front of the broom wagon for most of the day, ended his Tour de France. The American was unable to continue after falling ill and his departure now leaves Pogačar and UAE down a key rider for the upcoming Alpine challenges.His teammates, along with Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe contolled the peloton on the run-in to the final climb, five minutes behind the leaders.They hit the ascent to Orcières-Merlette as a sextet with three minutes in hand on those dropped from the break, despite a few digs from Jorgenson on the run-in. The group continued together up the early kilometres of the climb, seemingly playing cat-and-mouse as Carapaz and Paret-Peintre marked each other.The first big move came 4km from the finish, where Paret-Peintre put in a testing acceleration before Carapaz hit the front with a major attack. The pair stuck together once more, while Schmid and Jorgenson made it across. García Pierna and Johannessen weren't so lucky, however.Carapaz struck again from the new group of four with 3.5km to go, only this time there was no response. Paret-Peintre had nothing left to give, the Frenchman sticking with Schmid and Jorgenson as the Ecuadorian raced away.His acceleration put him 20 seconds clear by the 3km mark, as Paret-Peintre struggled to hold on to Schmid and Jorgenson, dropping further back. There would be no regrouping over the final kilometres of the race, with Carapaz racing well clear of his competitors and eventually crossing the line to celebrate the 25th win of his career.Richard item.title: Heavy traffic closes main road to Alpe d'Huez early on Thursday ahead of double mountaintop finishes of Tour de France
item.author: jackie.tyson@futurenet.com (Jackie Tyson)
item.content:encoded: The masses have arrived earlier than expected to the Oisans region for a doubleheader of mountain stages at the Tour de France. The main road connecting Bourg d'Oisans to Alpe d'Huez, RD211, was closed at 14:00 local time today, eight hours before the previously-announced closure, for motorised vehicles in the uphill direction.RD211 is the iconic serpentine road with the 21 hairpin turns that twists up the mountain, and it is the most direct way for any delivery vehicles, resort workers and Tour visitors to access Alpe d'Huez.Public safety official in the Isère region in collaboration with Tour organisers A.S.O. announced just this morning that the road will be shut down because of the heavy volume of vehicles on the 13.8-mile ascent."Due to exceptionally high traffic, the ascent of Alpe d’Huez (RD211) will be closed to all vehicles starting at the south roundabout, from this Thursday July 23 at 2 p.m. until Sunday July 26 2026 at 12 a.m. The descent from Alpe d’Huez will remain open until Thursday July 23 at 10 p.m. and from Saturday July 25 2026 at 1 a.m.," a joint press release said."Access to Alpe d’Huez will remain open to pedestrians and cyclists, except during the race on Friday July 24."So, can spectators ride their bikes up the Alpe d'Huez climb on Friday morning to watch the peloton in the afternoon? No. RD211 will close to bicycles from 22:00 Thursday, July 23 to 0:00 Sunday, July 26, which was announced earlier this week. In addition, the Col de Sarenne on the back side of the mountain will close to motorised vehicles beginning 14:00 CET Thursday to 11:00 Sunday, and the closure for bicycles begins at 18:00 Friday.The roundabout on the south-east side of Bourg d'Oisans is the 'official start' of the climb, where D1091 intersects with RD211. The original road closure was to take place at 23:00 Thursday evening.For residents and summer visitors who require access to Alpe d'Huez, officials confirmed that a valid identification will be required for permission to drive on RD211 Thursday afternoon and early evening, then restricted from 22:00.An update on gondola use was part of the advisory on Thursday, saying: "These lifts will also transport passengers who have already taken a one-way trip back down throughout the day on Friday July 24 2026 and after the start of Stage 20 on Saturday July 25 2026 beginning at 1pm".Stage 19 of the Tour of Friday begins in Gap and concludes atop Alpe d'Huez, using the on the 'front' approach with the iconic 21 switchbacks. Stage 20 on Saturday begins in Bourg d'Oisans and circles around to the back side for a new approach on Col de Sarenne and a final 14km circuit of the ski resort. The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. item.title: Mercedes identifies Russell's F1 power unit software issue
Tadej Pogačar Short-Handed for Alpe d’Huez After Brandon McNulty Exit
item.author: ahood
item.content:encoded: Tadej Pogačar is closing in on a fifth Tour de France title, but UAE suddenly enters the decisive final weekend in the Alps battered by illness and attrition.

item.title: Brandon McNulty abandons the Tour de France on stage 18
item.content:encoded: The USA's Brandon McNulty has abandoned the Tour de France on stage 18 after being dropped early on Thursday and riding just in front of the broom wagon for much of the stage.McNulty started Thursday's stage from Voiron but was reportedly suffering from a sore throat, compounding the fact that he was struck by a car driver just two days ago whilst training for stage 16's TT.Normally a strong climber and a key domestique for race leader Tadej Pogačar, it was therefore a surprise to see the American rider dropped fairly early on during the stage, fading away from the peloton alongside the sprinters only 25km into the stage.He soldiered on for most of the day, falling as far back as 25 minutes behind the peloton and trailing just in front of the broom wagon, but he eventually called it a day in the final 50km of racing, climbing off and quitting the race.UAE already had one rider suffer through illness on stage 17, when Adam Yates struggled with stomach problems and was shepherded to the finish by Tim Wellens. UAE told on Thursday morning that Yates' illness was isolated and stomach problems had not spread through the team, but that didn't mean none of the other riders were ill at all, with McNulty suffering from a different ailment."It's different to Adam's problem, he has a stomach problem and vomit. Today he's better," UAE sports manager Matxin Joxean Fernandez explained to Eurosport."Brandon doesn't have a fever, he doesn't have Covid, he doesn't have any positive results in these different tests. He just has bad symptoms. He's sick, it's his throat, no fever but he has bad symptoms."Wellens also admitted to Belgian media at the start of stage 18 that he was feeling somewhat ill, though he managed to stay with the peloton throughout the day.Losing McNulty means Pogačar has lost a key supporter for the final few – very hard – days of this Tour de France, with at least one other teammate ill.Following McNulty's abandon, a radio message from the UAE team car was broadcast on the Tour coverage, with a team DS telling the riders that the American had abandoned after he "was alone for 50 kilometres" before saying, "come on, you can do it still".UAE did some pacing on stage 18, but were able to sit back and let a strong breakaway go, and then leave pacing in the finale to Pinarello-Q36.5 and TotalEnergies, who were trying to control the gap to protect their riders' top 10 placings.According to Pogačar, speaking to on Thursday morning, the team had taken preventative measures against the spread of any illness, with Adam Yates eating separately from his teammates whilst he was unwell."We can get some sickness from alongside the road, with hundreds of thousands of fans screaming at us and spitting on us when they're yelling," he added. "So I think it's easier to catch some disease that way than in the team bubble."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage.
