item.title: 'He has made progress as a leader during the Tour de France' – Paul Seixas' teammate reveals how the Frenchman has adapted to the role
item.author: cyclingnews@futurenet.com (Dani Ostanek)
item.content:encoded: Paul Seixas is heading into the final days of his Tour de France debut in contention for the podium and the white jersey of best young rider.The Frenchman, leading Decathlon CMA CGM on his Grand Tour debut, lies just 20 seconds away from third-placed Isaac del Toro, with three key Alpine mountain stages filling this week before the race hits Paris.There have been a few things to get used to for 19-year-old Seixas and his teammates this July, though, as Tiesj Benoot told recently."In the first stage in Barcelona, ​​we rode through rows of fans," Benoot recalled."Paul asked me: 'Do you think it's going to be like this all day?' I answered: 'Paul, not all day, but all week'."He also noted that there is something of a generation gap between himself at 32 and his teenage leader. However, the differences between him and more experienced leaders aren't holding him back."There is a generation gap somewhere. Paul plays games on his phone, I don't," said Benoot."But you can also tell that he has what it takes to be a leader. He has already made progress in that regard during this Tour."He speaks up more and more often in the team meetings and clearly conveys what he expects. At that age, I find that quite remarkable."One minor difficulty arose during the opening days of the Tour, when Seixas struggled to make himself heard over the radio after puncturing on the hilly Montjuïc Park circuit on stage 2 in Barcelona."Paul said that on the radio, but they hadn't heard him," said Benoot. "Paul then immediately swapped bikes with Aurélien Paret-Peintre, while the team car was driving right behind him. That wasn't necessary."After that, he wanted to swap again to be on his own bike for the final, but that hadn't come across properly on the radio either. I eventually said: 'Paul wants to swap.'"You could see there that Paul hasn't raced with earpieces very often yet. You have to make sure you are audible. Really grab your radio and turn your head towards it."Those are small things, but that can be the difference between being understood or not."Despite some teething troubles, Seixas is well in with a shot of a podium place at the weekend, with two mountain stages to L'Alpe d'Huez the main challenges standing in his way.Benoot, racing his 10th Tour, is happy with his own level, meanwhile. The Belgian moved to Decathlon CMA CGM over the winter but didn't make his debut until the Tour de Suisse after undergoing surgery for a herniated disc back in February.He's currently 20th overall, raced in the breakaway on stage 17, and has proved a key lieutenant for Seixas."I am surprised by the level I am reaching. I doubted for a long time whether I would make the Tour," said Benoot."I had a second operation at the end of February. After that, I did exercises off the bike for four weeks. I only started my real training around Paris-Roubaix. It took a while before I really felt my legs starting to turn."But even then, there was doubt: how would my back react to long climbing training sessions? I also had to be able to time trial, with the team time trial at the start in Barcelona."He's racing at an even higher level than he reached with his old squad, Visma-Lease a Bike, Benoot said, with heat training before the Tour playing a big part in that."At Visma, I never rode a Tour without a bad day in the first two weeks. Here, I am performing at my best possible level every day," he said."Normally, I struggle in the heat. I sweat excessively. I was tested for that and ranked in the top three of all measurements ever, in both sweat volume and salt concentration. But here in the Tour, I haven't had much trouble with it."Due to my training camp in Sierra Nevada and the heatwave in Belgium, I trained in the heat for six weeks before the Tour. That is helping me now. The fact that I have so little competition in my legs before the Tour – only the Tour de Suisse – might just mean I am fresher."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'He's got grinta' – Josh Tarling praised for breakaway performance as Netcompany Ineos try to salvage their Tour de France
item.content:encoded: Josh Tarling was praised for his aggressive riding in the breakaway on stage 17 of the Tour de France after the Netcompany Ineos rider made several attacks and a late surge to try to win from the breakaway in Voiron. Teammate Michał Kwiatkowski highlighted Tarling's 'grinta' after the stage, using the Italian word to describe his fighting spirit and determination. The 22-year-old Welsh rider fractured his collarbone and a rib during the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes but trained through his recovery and was rewarded with a place in the Netcompany line-up. He struggled in the early stages but appears to have improved during the Tour. On Tuesday he finished tenth in the hilly and technical time trial, beating teammate Filippo Ganna and many of the other time trial riders in the Tour. On Wednesday, Tarling joined the breakaway of the day and then went on the attack with the likes of Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech) and Quinn Simmons (Lidl-Trek). He even tried a last-kilometre attack when the sprinters took control of the breakaway."It was exciting to be in the break. I had terrible legs at the start of the Tour, so it was nice that I could try and show them. I didn't quite have it in the final, but I had to try," Tarling told Eurosport after the stage to Voiron.Kwiatkowski was far more talkative, praising Tarling for his performance. "In the first week Josh didn't feel great but he was fighting for the win. He attacked in the final kilometre and that's just proof that he's got grinta," Kwiatkowski told Eurosport.Netcompany Ineos have endured a difficult Tour considering their historic results and usual high standards of performance. The British team were left without Tour team leader Oscar Onley after his crash at the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes and were forced to make late changes to their line-up, opting to leave Carlos Rodríguez at home. They appeared to specifically target victory in the opening Barcelona team time trial but finished second to Visma-Lease a Bike. Thymen Arensman soon fell out of the GC battle and other riders have struggled in the fight for stage victories. Stage 17 was perhaps their best performance as they placed Tarling, Kwiatkowski and Egan Bernal in the big break of the day. Kwiatkowski insisted the riders would continue to try to win the final stages of the Tour de France."We've been unlucky so far and pretty close on a few days. Obviously, we're never giving up. We have moral, so we're really trying to bring home a stage," he said. "Josh has a bright future. It was a super strong group and so just being there and trying to deliver the victory is the right mindset. The victory will come sooner or later." Kwiatkowski missed three months of racing after suffering severe bruising and muscular damage in a high-speed crash at Milan-San Remo. But he knows how to read a race and joined a dangerous attack with Mads Pedersen at one point during stage 17. "I was just on his wheel, basically, I got lucky," he admitted."I didn't move much the entire day because I don't have so much legs, but I can smell it when something's going on. Mads obviously was keen to attack. The group was big and so it was about just taking an opportunity." The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Yamaha names experienced crew chief to oversee Martin's adaptation in MotoGP
'There could be some very big explosions' – Juan Ayuso predicts carnage as Tour de France GC battle heads to the Alps
item.author: cyclingnews@futurenet.com (Dani Ostanek)
item.content:encoded: Lidl-Trek head into the final mountain stages of the Tour de France with both of their leaders in the top 10 on GC and both men hoping to do battle for a podium position.Spanish racer Juan Ayuso is also in contention for the white jersey, with a three-way battle between him, Paul Seixas, and Isaac del Toro on the cards as the race hits the Alps.He lies in fifth overall, 2:31 down on Del Toro's third place and 2:11 behind Seixas. Lidl-Trek co-leader Mattias Skjelmose is sixth, a further 52 seconds off the podium.Ayuso, however, has lost time on both recent GC days, the climb to the Plateau de Solaison and the stage 16 time trial. Speaking to , he said that he and his Danish teammate would try to move up the standings over the next three days."Both Skjelmose and I are in a position where we can't go much further backwards, and if it happens, well, it wouldn't be a tragedy," Ayuso said."We have to try to move forward, but for that, we also need the strength to do it."The upcoming stages include today's summit finish at Orcières-Merlette and double finishes at L'Alpe d'Huez. Ayuso and Skjelmose will aim for the white jersey and the podium, with the Dane on the up. He was 4:34 away from third place before stages 15 and 16, and now lies 3:23 off Del Toro.The pair will also be looking to help their team battle for the team classification with UAE Team Emirates-XRG. Following stage 17 to Voiron, which saw Lidl-Trek finish with three riders in the lead breakaway group, the team heads to the Alps with a far stronger 18:10 lead over their rivals."We'll see what situations arise and if we can take advantage of them," Ayuso said of stage 18."I think the next stages will be decided by elimination, and I believe there could be some very big explosions, and I include myself in that."I could also crack and lose a lot of time, but so could Seixas, Del Toro, or anyone else. We have to be there and seize any opportunity."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: As it happened: victory goes to the breakaway in the Alps on stage 18 of the Tour de France
item.content:encoded: The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France.Hello and welcome to stage 18 of the Tour de France!After yesterday’s thrilling battle in the race for the green jersey, today the attention turns back towards the general classification, as at long last the race heads into the Alps! This competition might seem a lot more closed off, with Tadej Pogačar’s huge lead of 4:32, but you never know what can happen in the final stages of a Tour de France - especially when we know what illness has struck the leader’s team…With today’s parcours not as severe as the two mighty Alpe d’Huez stages to come, the consensus appears to be that today will likely be one for the breakaway rather than the GC. The stage win specialists will know this, as will those targetting the polka-dot jersey, and therefore the battle to get into the break at the start of the day will be more vital and very competitive. The riders are in the neutralised zone, having set off from the picturesque unofficial start at Voiron. They will be racing for real in about 2km. And they're off! It's a subdued start, with only a few tentative moves attempted so far - they might be waiting for the first climb of the day, which isn't for another 20km. Lewis Askey, Jonas Abrahamsen, Marco Haller and Pascal Eenkhoorn managed to get a small gap for a little while, but have since been brought back. These aren't the kind of riders we imagine will make the definitive break when it forms - there is far too much climbing on the menu for them.A new group has formed, and it features some of those in the previous group - Askey and Abrahamsen. They’re joined by Le Berre, Gate and Vinokurov. They’ve built a lead of 25 seconds, but the action is far from done with the climb starting in about 10km. The King of the Mountains candidates are coming to the fore ahead of the climb. Richard Carapa (third in that competition) has his EF Education-EasyPost riders controlling at the front of the peloton, keeping the five-man break's lead at about 40 seconds; and Astana DS Pete Kennaugh states that Soudal-QuickStep are also wanting to control things, for their man Valentin Paret-Peintre (second in the competition). “So if I hear right, QuickStep are controlling, I guess to keep it together until the climb”Peter Kennaugh, XDS Astana DS These are the five riders out front...but for how much longer? The riders will start riding the Côte d'Engins in a few kilometres, which, at 5.5% for 11.6km, will see only real climbers remain at the front of the race for. The riders have just started the climb. EF had been setting the pace in the peloton, but now Visma-Lease a Bike take over. Now Vingegaard is out of the race, they must be targetting days like this to go for stage wins. This is an interesting developing, and a worrying one for UAE Team Emirates-XRG. Brandon McNulty, one of their key climbing domestiques, is dropped out of the peloton almost as soon as the road went uphill. He is, presumably, sick, which suggests that whatever Adam Yates came down with is contagious. So the big question is - has their leader Tadej Pogačar managed to avoid the illness that has struck the team, or is he also vulnerable? Abrahamsen, Le Berre and Vinokurov have dropped Askey and Gate on the lower slopes of the climb, and a chase group has formed behind them featuring Quinn, Slock, Van Eetvelt and Anders Halland Johannessen. The gaps are all very small though, with only ten seconds separating the front breakaway group and the front of the peloton. A new group of four is clear at the front of the race, and it contains the polka-dot jersey candidates - Carapaz and Paret-Peintre are here, along with the former’s teammate Healy, plus Caruso and Bernal. 4.5km from the summit, they’re preparing for battle for the KOM points.Meanwhile more riders are being shelled out of the back of the peloton, including Jasper Philipsen. If Mads Pedersen can hang in there on this climb and the net, there’s a chance for him to gain an advantage again in the points classification at the day’s intermediate sprint.That group has been brought back, instigating a new flurry of attacks, including one by Quinn Simmons - who you might have thought would prioritise setting a tempo for Mads Pedersen, who’s shrill right towards the front of the peloton and trying to survive this climb to contest the intermediate sprint later.Lidl-Trek have ridden brilliantly as a collective all race, but Simmons has spoken out at his frustration at the way the team prioritises Pedersen’s green jersey bid over his desire to win a stage. A group of three now leads the race, with 1km to go until the summit. Along with Schmid, It contains two of Carapaz's teammates, Healy and Quinn - but not Carapaz himself. Will he bridge over before the top?Carapaz did indeed attack from the peloton behind to join is teammates, but had to settle for second at the summit as he was joined by Paret-Peintre, who out-sprinted him at the top. That pair have become the clear candidates to challenge Pogačar for the polka-dot jersey.A new lead group has formed on the false flat that followed the official summit of the climb, and guess who’s present in it? Mads Pedersen! The Dane has Jorgenson, Arensman, Castrillo, Tobias Halland Johannessen, Engelhardt and Breuillard with him, and a gap of 13 seconds on the peloton. EF aren’t happy with the situation though, and Ben Healy is trying to drag the peloton back to them.Despite the best efforts of EF, who have also had Carapaz try an attack, this Pederen group still leads by over 20 seconds. The riders are still not descending, continuing up an uphill road that it not count as part of the official climb.While Pedersen is riding for the green jersey, EF are worried about letting this break up the road and taking some much-needed KOM points that Carapaz wants. Healy is attacking constantly to try and get out of the peloton and bridge up to the seven leaders, but is having no luck. He's being marked by the teammates of riders already up the road, including Netcompany's Bernal and Lidl's Simmons. As they near the start of the descent, the lead group is 30 seconds up the road. The lead group continue to maintain their advantage, of 35 seconds over the peloton. Romain Grégoire is the latest rider to set off out of the peloton in pursuit, but has no help with him and is only a handful of seconds ahead of them. Castrillo is one of the seven riders in the break today, and is on a mission to redeem what has been a difficult race for Movistar, who remain winless and lost their GC man Cian Uijtdebroeks in the first man. One positive story that has come from the team is Nelson Oliveira, who is quietly on the brink of making cycling history.Mission accomplished for Romain Grégoire, who has managed to bridge up to the seven leaders all by himself. They lead the peloton by 40 seconds, as they near the bottom of the descent. Enjoying our live race coverage? Subscribe to Cyclingnews for an all-access pass to our unrivalled reporting of WorldTour racing, bike tech and exclusive in-depth features. Plus, access to the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on-the-go! Ben Healy is on the attack again, and is part of a five-man group that is chasing the leaders, also featuring Schmid, Tratnik, Vacek and Garcia-Piena. They’re still 45 seconds adrift, but already 30 seconds ahead of the peloton.That’s not ideal for EF, who would have wanted Carapaz in it for the KOM points, but still gives them a shot at the stage win if these breakaways are the definitive ones. Carapaz and Paret-Peintre haven't thrown in the towel yet. They've just jumped out of the peloton, with the latter's teammates Stuyven and Vervaeke joining them to try to pace them to the two groups ahead. The peloton don't look happy to let them go, however, and are trying to chase them down, but the the combined strength of the two Soudal rouleurs has seen them open a lead of 15 seconds over them. They're still 13 seconds behind the Healy group, and 1:10 down on the leaders. Good work from the Soudal riders, who have brought the Paret-Peintre / Carapaz quartet up to the Healy group. They start the climb one minute down on the leaders and 50 seconds up on the peloton, from which more riders have tried attacking out of. The riders are climbing again, up the category two Côte de Monteynard (9.9km at 5%). Healy is paying for his earlier efforts and is dropped out of the chase group, but the rest of them are closing in on the eight leaders, now just 40 seconds ahead. Stuyven and Vervaeke have also since been dropped from the chase group having helped it go clear, leaving it up to Paret-Peintre, Carapaz, and the others (Bernal, Schmid, Garcia Pierna and Vacek) to bridge up to the leaders by themselves. They're making slow progress, still 25 seconds behind.Meanwhile a large chase group of about 25 riders has formed behind them, and are just under a minute behind, with the peloton a further minute and a half behind them. There are only about 1.5km left until the top of the climb, and still the chasers have not caught the leaders. Paret-Peintre and Carapaz look pretty desperate to do so, given the KOM points on offer at the top. They still have 15 seconds to make up - can they do it in time? The chasers did indeed manage to make the catch within 1km to the top, and once again Paret-Peintre beats Carapaz in the sprint to gain the five points at the top.Behind the 14-man lead group are about 30 chasers at 1:15, while the peloton is 3:45 adrift - the biggest lead there has been all day. As widely predicted, UAE Team Emirates XRG appear happy to let the break go for the stage win today. They’re riding tempo at the front of the peloton, and have allowed the leaders a gap of 4:25.The question now is whether the chase group in between can join the leaders. They still have 1:12 to make up to do so.The chase group is starting to make real inroads now, and are just 50 seconds behind. It's a very big one, and they're working well enough together to bring down this gap. The leaders are on the third climb of the day, the category three Côte des Terrasses. So another chance for the two polka-dot jersey chasers to get more points; but not so many this time, with just 2 and 1 on offer at the top. Meanwhile the chasers progress has been halted, the gap pegged at 55 seconds. More points for Paret-Peintre at the top, who again out-sprints his rival Carapaz. Here's what the rankings look like now:Attention now shifts in the breakaway group from the polka-dot jersey to the green jersey. The intermediate sprint is coming up in around 10km, which is the entire reason Pedersen has made it into the group. He has Vacek with him to control the group, and ensure no sneaky attacks are made before the sprint. This is set to be some comeback for Pedersen, whose plans backfired yesterday and saw his lead in the classification slashed. Enjoying our live race coverage? Subscribe to Cyclingnews for an all-access pass to our unrivalled reporting of WorldTour racing, bike tech and exclusive in-depth features. Plus, access to the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on-the-go! Having let the break’s lead grow to over seven minutes, the stage winner won’t come from the peloton today. But that doesn’t mean there won’t be a battle among the GC favourites on the final climb - particularly between Paul Seixas and Isaac del Toro, between whom the race for both the podium and the white jersey is intensifying. Job done for Mads Pedersen, who takes the points at the intermediate sprint uncontested. After yesterday's setback, suddenly his grip on the green jersey seems far firmer. With both Bernal and Arensman in the break, Netcompany are well-positioned to go for the stage win today.They’ve endured a difficult Tour, with Josh Tarling yesterday the latest to try and fail to land them a stage win. With no rider in GC contention, or for any of the other jerseys for that matter, they are all in for a stage win. There's a split in the lead group. Jorgenson, Schmid, Johannessen and Garcia Pierna have a gap over the rest. But as has been the case all day, it's Carapaz and Paret-Peintre who are trying to chase them down. Carapaz and Paret-Peintre have joined the four leaders, and are working well together to build a lead of 30 seconds over the chasers. Netcompany Ineos are the team that's really missed out, with Bernal and Arensman both still in the chasing group, which now only have seven riders.As for the large chase group behind them, they've been going backwards for a long time now, and find themselves in no-man's land with the leaders 3 minutes ahead, and the peloton 6 minutes behind. The leading 6 are working well together, growing their lead to the chasers to one minute. This is a key phase of the race, as the road drags slightly uphill for a long time (including a brief steeper incline of 6.7% for 2.5km when the reach a category three climb) all the way until the foot of the final climb. These are the kind of roads where working together in groups against the wind will be vital, and the chasers are currently in danger of being put out of contention for the stage even before the final climb begins. Still the leading 6's gap grows, to 1:15.Meanwhile the peloton are 8 minutes behind, with Tudor, Pinarello and TotalEnergies taking up the pace-setting in defence of their places in the top ten of GC, currently under threat from Carapaz. It’s been a comfortable day so far for Tadej Pogačar, but his team UAE are looking very vulnerable today. McNulty was dropped out of the peloton long ago, and Adam Yates has also sat up, leaving him short of his usual mountain domestiques.Things could therefore get interesting if the GC men really go for it on the final climb. Remco Evenepoel has played down his chances of taking yellow, but might he smell blood given the illness affecting the team? He looks in the form of his life following his atypical preparation going into the Tour, and has managed to get the better of Pogačar in both the last two GC stages. Jorgenson attempts an attack from the lead group. The others manage to bridge back up to him though - all, apart from Garcia Pierna, reducing the group down to five riders. They're about to start the penultimate climb of the day, the 2.5km, 6.7% Côte de Saint-Léger-les-Mélèzes.Garcia-Pienra is inching his way back into the lead group, as they come to within 1km of the summit. Meanwhile the main chase group containing Bernal is now a distant two minutes behind, and appear to be out of contention for the stage win.Two more points for Paret-Peintre at the top, and one more point for Carapaz. The real fight will come on the next and final climb, however, where there isn't just another ten points at steak, but also the matter of a stage win. Brandon McNulty has left the race having suffered all day.He becomes the first UAE rider to withdraw from the race, so they are still strong in terms of numbers. But how many of them are suffering with illness? And might they come under pressure on this upcoming final climb? It promises to be an intriguing ending to this stage, both in terms of the win, and the action among the GC contenders. “Brendan [McNulty] abandoned just now. He was alone for 50km. Come on guys you can do it still.”Paul Seixas still has lots of teammates with him in the peloton. This might be a real chance for him to try and exploit UAE's weakness and go after Del Toro's spot on the podium, as well as his lead in the white jersey classification. Red Bull-Bora Hansgrohe have seized control of the peloton on the approach to the climb, and have lots of numbers to intimidate UAE with. Will Remco Evenepoel try anything on it, or ride purely defensively?One team who have stated that they plan to ride aggressively in search of rising the standings rather than defend what they've already got are Lidl-Trek, who have Ayuso and Skjelmose in 5th and 6th respectively.The leaders have started the final climb, which rises for 7.1m at 6.7%. They remain together for now, but that's surely to change soon as the gradients rise and they start to attack each other. With the chasers a distant 3:50, and the peloton even further back at 5:35, one of these 6 will win the stage today. Of the 6 teams represented in this lead group, all of them have already won at least one stage at this year's race - with the exception of EF. As the best decorated climber on paper in this group, the pressure is on their man Carapaz to deliver. So far no attacks have come.It's cagey and tactical in the lead group, with some very tentative attacks being made, from Jorgenson, Schmid and Paret-Peintre, but nobody going all in.Until now! Carapaz explodes out of the group, and no-one other than Paret-Peintre can follow for now.Schmid and Jorgenson drag themselves back to the two leaders, but soon after Carapaz attacks again, 3.5km from the finish - and this time nobody has gone with him!Jorgenson and Schmid are chasing behind, but already at a distant 20 seconds. Paret-Peintre has been dropped by them and is still further behind. This is looking very good for the Ecuadorian. 30 seconds now for Carapaz, who appears to be riding away to victory! Carapaz goes under the flamme rouge, 37 seconds ahead of Jorgenson and Schmid, and 1 minute ahead of Paret-Peintre. Meanwhile in the peloton, the pace is up from the work from Red Bull-Bora Hansgrohe, but no attacks yet. Richard Carapaz crosses the line, and EF at last have the stage win they have spent the whole Tour so aggressively fighting for!Behind, Schmid wins the sprint for second ahead of Jorgenson.Interest now shifts to the GC group, where Red Bull continue to set a pace. They're riding tempo, and the peloton is reduced to about 15 riders, but it's not enough to put any of the GC men under real pressure. The group of favourites roll over the line together, about 4:30 after Carapaz. There are no time gaps between them, and we will have to wait for the two harder stages to come tomorrow and the following day for real GC attacks and action. Richard Carapaz (Ecu) EF Education-EasyPostMauro Schmid (Swi) Jayco-AlUlaMatteo Jorgenson (USA) Visma-Lease a BikeValentin Paret Peintre (Fra) Soudal-QuickStepTobias Halland Johannessen (Nor) Uno-X MobilityRaul Garcia Pierna (Spa) Movistar TeamPablo Castrillo (Spa) Movistar TeamLenny Martinez (Fra) Bahrain VictoriousRemco Evenepoel (Bel) Red Bull-Bora-HansgroheTom Pidcock (GBr) Pinarello-Q36.5Tadej Pogačar (Slo) UAE Team Emirates-XRGRemco Evenepoel (Bel) Red Bull-Bora-HansgroheIsaac Del Toro (Mex) UAE Team Emirates-XRGPaul Seixas (Fra) Decathlon CMA CGMJuan Ayuso (Spa) Lidl-TrekMattias Skjelmose (Den) Lidl-TrekLenny Martinez (Fra) Bahrain VictoriousTom Pidcock (GBr) Pinarello-Q36.5Jordan Jegat (Fra) TotalenergiesRichard Carapaz (Ecu) EF Education-EasyPostThis is stage win number two at the Tour de France in the career of Richard Carapa, and the ninth across all the Grand Tours. Even when he drops out of GC contention he invariably animates Grand Tours, and that has been especially true at this race; he was awarded today for his constant attacking throughout this race.And he’s not done yet. His efforts today have seen him move to just seven points behind Pogačar in the mountains classification. The Slovenian will surely pick up points on either one of or both the Alpe d’Huez stages, when he’s expected to chase for a stage win, but there will still be lots of points available for Carapaz - especially if he can get up the road on the penultimate stage, which has lots of points available over the early hors category mountains. He might not have quite had the climbing legs to defeat Carapaz, but it was nevertheless another great result for Mauro Schmid, who finished second. The Swiss is arguably in the form of his life, and has placed first, second and second in three of the last 6 stages.Thanks for joining us today for what was a classic in the genre of the drawn-out, hard-fought fight for the stage win, the kind you often get in the final days of a Grand Tour. Tomorrow could be a very different affair, if, as anticipated, Tadej Pogačar decides he wants to win the stage, atop the iconic Alpe d’Huez. But is his illness-struck team in the shape necessary to control the break? And could he himself even be suffering from it?Whatever the answers to those questions, tomorrow is Alpe d’Huez day, which is always essential at the Tour de France - so be sure to join us again tomorrow when we;ll once more bring you all the action!

item.title: How much do MotoGP tyres cost and who pays for them?
Tadej Pogačar Is Already Plotting His Ultimate Tour de France Coronation on Alpe d’Huez
item.author: Jim Cotton
item.content:encoded: Preview: Marquee records and iconic victories in play as back-to-back Alpe stages crown the hardest final of the Tour de France in a decade.

item.title: Adam Yates to race on at Tour de France despite suffering with stomach problem
item.content:encoded: Adam Yates will ride on in the Tour de France, with UAE Team Emirates-XRG hoping he can recover from a stomach problem during stage 18 and so help Tadej Pogačar and Isaac del Toro in the final mountain stages.UAE believe that no other riders have been hit by the stomach problem. Yates fought to finish inside the time limit on Wednesday, with Pogačar revealing "he suffered like a dog" due to the stomach problem that left him unable to eat and so fuel for stage 17. Yates was at the start of stage 18 in Voirons on Thursday, with UAE team manager Mauro Gianetti hoping he can survive the stage and recover in time for the back-to-back stages to L'Alpe d'Huez. "His stomach problem is almost sorted out, but the legs may be a bit empty," Gianetti told Deputy Editor Patrick Fletcher before the start of the stage. "He is going to suffer because it’s still soon. We’re hoping he can get through today and then play a role tomorrow and Saturday. If not tomorrow, then at least Saturday we hope he can be back to his best."Yates fell ill on Tuesday, the day of the time trial and suffered through stage 17. He was distanced with around 80km to race, and UAE sent back Tim Wellens to help him finish inside the time limit.They finished 26:36 down on stage winner Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) and 18 minutes down on the main part of the peloton but lived to race another day. Yates was treated by the UAE team doctor, but the team said his illness is "isolated", with no one else affected. UAE still have a team of eight riders in the Tour, with Pogačar in the leader's yellow jersey, with a lead of 4:32 on Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe). Del Toro is third overall at 6:51 and leads the best young rider white jersey competition. UAE also hope to win the team competition but lost time to Lidl-Trek on stage 17. The team prize is decided by the best three riders on each stage, so Yates could play a vital role if he can return to full health. The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'The best strategy is not thinking too much' – Nelson Oliveira closes in on all-time record of Grand Tour finishes at 2026 Tour de France
item.content:encoded: Should Tadej Pogačar head into Paris next Sunday wearing yellow, you'd have to have been living under a rock not to be aware that he'll be equalling the record of five Tour de France wins. But what is far less well-known is that a different Tour record will actually be broken.According to cycling writer , Movistar racer Nelson Oliveira is currently on equal footing with former Mont Ventoux winner Sylvester Szmyd's record of 23 Grand Tours, and assuming disaster doesn't strike in the last 96 hours of this year's race, the 37-year-old will move onto 24 next Sunday in Paris.With 484 stages across those 24 GTs completed on Sunday in Paris, Oliveira will also have made it safely home in 10 Tours, four Giros and 10 Vueltas.A pro since 2010 in the long-defunct Xacobeo-Galicia Continental squad, Oliveira raced his first Grand Tour at the Vuelta a España way back in 2011 for RadioShack, finishing in 117th. With his role as a domestique long established, he's completed two Grand Tours in eight different years, for the first time in 2015, then again from 2019 through to 2024 and again in 2026.Under contract with Movistar until 2027, Oliveira has no intention of stopping racing for now. "Until my head tells me otherwise," he tells , "I'm sticking around."As for the record, "The best strategy is to take it day by day and not to think too much. If we get there to Paris, we'll see how we handle it."I get a lot of interviews about the record, but really I just want to live through this day by day. When we get to Paris, we'll talk about it more, but honestly I'm just focussed on today's stage – no more, no less."It fits Oliveira's belief in not overthinking things, that he'd never even heard of the record until the Giro d'Italia this year. But when he looks back at what he's managed to achieve, he's more than satisfied with making it as far as he could."When I was back racing my first Vuelta in 2011, I never imagined I'd get to 24, and when I was a kid riding around Portugal, I never thought I'd even get to do one. So it's really rewarding that I've made it as far as here.""I'm braking a bit more, that's true, as I'm getting older, because I've got a head on my shoulders and I'm a human being and the peloton is going faster and faster. But we're not just in the Tour riding around and thinking this is great. We're lacking a victory, and we're here to try and win one as well."Part of four breakaways to date in the Tour – Movistar's sole goal after their leader Cian Uijtdebroeks had to abandon, ill – Oliveira has only ever taken one Grand Tour stage win, back in Tarazona in the Vuelta a España 2015."It was a transition stage, there was a very big group, and there was a bit of a climb about 30km from the finish. Then I put my head down and time trialled it" – something which, as three-time Portuguese TT champion, Oliveira knows a fair bit – "all the way to the finish. That's a day I'm never going to forget, for sure."The lone Portuguese racer in this year's Tour, Oliveira says that even without a big name in the race, back home it still has a big following."It's a shame there's no João Almeida" – normally part of the UAE Team Emirates-XRG Tour lineup but unable to ride because of a lengthy illness and uneven condition – "but people like cycling and that's what really matters."After Sunday's celebrations, assuming all goes well for Oliveira in the last four days of the Tour, there won't be a third Grand Tour this August.Next on the list, he says, is the Tour of Germany and the Hamburg Cyclassic, the two Canadian WorldTour races and the Road World Championships. But his unique achievement in the Tour de France won't be forgotten, either, and the hopes of a breakaway victory for Movistar remain, too."There are teams that want to win the stages and don't let the breaks get away," Oliveira says. "But we're motivated."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: Garmin acquires training platforms TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic
item.author: thomas.wieckowski@futurenet.com (Tom Wieckowski)
item.content:encoded: Garmin has this week announced the acquisition of the popular cycling training platform, TrainingPeaks, as well as strength training plaform TrainHeroic. The American technology company announced the news in a short press release on Wednesday, and stated 'financial terms of the acquisition will not be disclosed.'The TrainingPeaks headquarters are in Louisville, Colorado. The 120-person staff will join Garmin's workforce. Garmin, founded by Gary Burrell and Min Kao, has been a key name in the cycling world since its first bike computers were launched back in the mid 2000s.TrainingPeaks was founded in 1999 by Joe Friel, his son Dirk Friel, and Gear Fisher. The web and mobile platform allows coaches and athletes to plan and manage their training programmes. TrainHeroic, meanwhile, focuses on supporting gym users and strength and conditioning coaches. Both were owned by Peaksware, a holding company that also owns a host of software brands in the music industry. On the acquisition, Brad Trenkle, the Garmin Co-COO, said "TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic share Garmin’s passion for empowering athletes and coaches around the world with world-class training tools, performance metrics and actionable insights. "The addition of these highly successful platforms to the Garmin ecosystem will expand access to more authentic coaching experiences, connecting athletes with professional coaches who guide, motivate and inspire them to reach their goals.” Dirk Friel has confirmed in a statement on his LinkedIn page that he will remain involved as a strategic advisor, and TrainingPeaks President Lee Gerakos will continue to lead the company.He provided some insight into what the future may hold for the three platforms."Joining forces with an iconic, industry-leading brand like Garmin is the ultimate catalyst for our next era of innovation. "By combining our coaching expertise with Garmin’s world-class technology, we are positioning ourselves to pioneer the next generation of athletic training software and services," he said. In 2024, TrainingPeaks itself acquired indoor cycling platform IndieVelo, developing it into a new indoor cycling app, TrainingPeaks Virtual and established itself as a challenger to popular platforms like Zwift and MyWhoosh. The move from Garmin, with the capital behind it, could transform TrainingPeaks Virtual into a serious competitor in the space.

item.title: Kay back to top of Autosport National Rankings
item.content:encoded: He only relinquished the top spot for one week, and now Connor Kay is back in front in the Autosport National Rankings table. Kay’s all-out programme in historic machinery this season has meant he has been a regular at the summit of our top 50, but he lost the top spot last week to Junior Saloons title favourite Ollie Smith.But Kay was back out at the Snetterton Classic Sports Car Club ...Keep reading

item.title: 'I’m glad Urška is not coming up with my car' – Tadej Pogačar braced for 'insane' Alpe d’Huez atmosphere at Tour de France
item.content:encoded: The mountain stages at this year’s Tour culminate with an Alpe d’Huez double header, and while Saturday’s stage 20 is the far tougher day of racing, it’s Friday’s stage 19 that takes the traditional route up the ski resort by way of the famous 21 hairpin bends. As ever, fans have been setting up camp on the Alpe for several days already, with space at a premium on a mountain that never fails to produce a raucous atmosphere on race day. Pogačar has ridden up Alpe d’Huez once in his career in 2022, when he was tussling with Jonas Vingegaard behind the breakaway, and he has strong memories of that day. "It will be crazy. I’m kind of happy Urška [Žigart, his partner] is not coming up with my car like last time," Pogačar said in one of his yellow jersey press conferences this week. "In 2022, I remember how dirty the car was because all the people are touching and shaking the cars. I’m kind of glad I don’t have my personal car up there this year."It’s going to be crazy. Also, this year we have the two stages together, the two most hard stages with a finish at Alpe d’Huez. I’m expecting it to be insane. On the Markstein [on stage 14], I’ve never seen crowds like this, but maybe I think Alpe d’Huez can be even more. It’s going to be fantastic. I hope nothing goes wrong. We’ll see. It’s going to be a great end of the Tour."Having raced behind breakaway winner Tom PIdcock in 2022, Pogačar would surely love to add a victory on one of the Tour’s most iconic climbs to his palmarès. Asked where Alpe d’Huez ranked in his view of the Tour’s most iconic climbs, Pogačar put it in a bracket with two other famous names."Iconic climbs, apparently Alpe d’Huez has quite a lot of hype. But yeah, I think also Mont Ventoux is also quite iconic, I would say. One of those two must be the top of hype, no?"And Tourmalet, I think also. Definitely these three are not the hardest climbs in France, but I guess the history makes them iconic. Also this year it will be iconic because we have two days together on Alpe d’Huez."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Calendar change sets up Tour de Pologne Women as pre-Tour de France Femmes sprint tune-up in 2026
item.author: simone.giuliani@futurenet.com (Simone Giuliani)
item.content:encoded: There is a noticeable shift in the start list of the Tour de Pologne Women that has accompanied its move from August to July in the reshuffled calendar. A number of the riders who last year were absent as they recovered from the sprint showdowns at the Tour de France Femmes are now flocking to the three-day race for a tune up ahead of the Grand Tour.That means defending champion Chiara Consonni (Canyon-SRAM) will face a strong array of challengers in the race, which plays to the strengths of the fast finishers, as it unfolds from Friday July 24 to Sunday July 26.The start list this year includes Elisa Balsamo (Lidl-Trek), who swept up four stage wins and the points jersey at the Giro d'Italia Women this year, which also moved to an earlier slot in the calendar. The SD Worx duo of Lotte Kopecky and Lorena Wiebes, who won two stages of the Tour de France Femmes and the points classification in 2025, will also be lining up at Tomaszów Lubelsk on Friday. It will be a debut at the event for all three of the riders.Wiebes, in particular, has been light on racing in recent months, with her disqualification on stage 1 of the Giro d'Italia cutting out a big chunk of planned competition. Since then, on the road, she has just raced the Copenhagen Sprint, which she won, and the Dutch National Championships in June, although she has thrown some gravel racing in there as well.The fine-tuning to ensure the race readiness is dialled in right from the beginning of the Grand Tour is crucial this year, with potential opportunities for the fast finishers beginning from the opening stages of this year's Tour, which starts in Switzerland.The Tour de Pologne women, like the Tour de France, is not serving up all pancake-flat sprint stages but across the three days the chances that it will come down to a bunch finish are solid. Although, on the last stage to Lublin there is a chance that an upkick ahead of the line could provide a launch opportunity and one that may be crucial in the overall battle.The Tour de Pologne Women starts with a 138.4km stage 1 from Tomaszów Lubelsk to Zamość, the hilliest day with four short climbs along the route and just over 1,000m of elevation gain, but a relatively flat run to the line through the last 25km. Stage 2 will take riders from Włodawa to Lubartów, with the flattest route of the race, but there is still an uphill drag not far before the end but then it is a downhill run toward the line. The finale delivers 101.3km of racing from Janowiec to Lublin, with a ramp up within the final kilometre and then a sharp right before the final dash to the line."The Tour de Pologne is a race well suited to sprinters, offering plenty of opportunities for the fast finishers," said Human Powered Health in its preview of the event, where it lining up with Canadian champion Maggie Coles-Lyster who claimed a second place behind Balsamo in the stage 6 sprint at this year's Giro d'Italia."However, with a sting in the tail on the Stage 3 finale, the battle for the overall classification is likely to go down to the wire."

item.title: Former mountain bike World Champion set to line up at UCI Gravel Worlds in Nannup, while USPro U23 ITT champion DQ'd at FoCo Fondo - Gravel Bits
item.author: jackie.tyson@futurenet.com (Jackie Tyson)
item.content:encoded: Two-time mountain bike world champion Henrique da Silva Avancini was confirmed for the elite men's start list at the 2026 UCI Gravel World Championships in Nannup, Australia. He won the inaugural Brazilian Gravel Championships two weeks ago and qualified for Worlds by winning the UCI Gravel World Series race Gravel Brazil in March.The 36-year-old won his first marathon mountain bike world title in 2018 and a second five years later, then retiring from MTB competitions. He has moved into gravel and road racing, finding success this year in both disciplines, winning the GC at the Tour of the Gila in the spring and then two medals at his home road nationals, including the gold medal in the time trial.Organisers for the UCI Gravel Worlds in Western Australia have begun to announce on social media posts some of the elite riders and country rosters. Petr Vakoč of Czechia and Paul Voss of Germany were also noted as heading to Western Australia for the elite men's battle in two months.Australians Tiffany Cromwell and Nicole Frain are among the early confirmations to battle for the elite women's rainbow stripes alongside Tessa Neefjes of the Netherlands and Haley Smith of Canada.Elite women will race 125km on Saturday, October 10, with elite men taking on 143km on Sunday, October 11. Age group categories will also use the medium or short courses with the same start/finish, departing 20 minutes after the elites each day.On the spectator side, camping registrations are now open, which will be the closest overnight accommodations to the courses. The Nannup Recreation Centre Precinct is less than one kilometre from the start/finish line, with direct access to the training course via the Munda Biddi Trail. Tent/Car or Caravan/Car options are available for three-night, four-night and five-night stays, with the campground opening Wednesday, October 5 for first arrivals.For those new to Western Australia, the Busselton Margaret River Airport is the closest airport for domestic flights, located 35 minutes from Nannup. International flights are based at the Perth Airport, which is a three-and-a-half-hour drive by car or bus.The elite men's race was not so straightforward, with Caleb Bottcher (ENVE/Shimano/Neversecond) sprinting just ahead of Matthew Saldana (Cyclesport.com) for second and third places. However, they moved up one step each after breakaway rider Kade Kreikemeier (Meridian Racing), who crossed the finish nearly a minute ahead, was disqualified.The 21-year-old Colorado racer won the USPro U23 men's time trial last month in West Virginia, and used those time trial skills to make up a three-minute gap to early leaders Michael Garrison (SpeedStudio p/b Basso) and Connor Pulvidente. He then rode solo for more than 75 miles at the front that carried him across the line with no one in sight.However, during Kreikemeier's march, he encountered problems securing water bottles as the temperature soared to 95F(35°C). A livestream race moto operator following Kreikemeier, with the camera rolling, offered the rider a bottle, which was accepted. Organisers acknowledged this was a rules violation for a rider to accept outside aid, so when Kreikemeier completed the ride, he was told he was DQ'd.Bottcher's time of 5:26:46 landed him in first place, with Saldana runner-up by one second and Benjamin Perry (Guava x Castelli S.O.G.) third a minute later, and 7:40 ahead of former WorldTour pro Gavin Mannion.Stephens made it into the lead group of five riders as they made a separation 20 miles into the 118-mile race. The US gravel champion opened a gap on the first big climb over Alexis Skarda (Scott Bicycles/Q36.5/Shimano), Courtney Sherwell (Santa Cruz SRAM Kenda Tires), Cecile Lejeune (Trek Driftless), Charlotte Clarke(Ventum, NZL) and Hannah Shell (Goodlife Brands, Pirelli). Then Skarda clawed her way back, and the two rode together until Stephens gave it a big kick with under 15 miles to go.Stephens crossed the line first in 6:11:43, with Lejeune going second five minutes later and Shell in third. Skarda finished fourth, and Sherwell rounded out the top five.Defending nin-binary/gender expansive champion Robin Cummings (Nine Lives Cycling Collective) finished in 6:30:35 for another victory.Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our gravel cycling coverage in 2026. We'll be on the ground at the biggest races of the season, bringing you breaking news, expert analysis, in-depth features, and much more.

item.title: 'There's no time for nostalgia' - Increasingly fraught fight for last-chance breaks in Tour de France keep 2026 retiree Ion Izagirre focused on race
item.content:encoded: By the end of stage 17 of this year's Tour de France, just eight of the 23 teams had scored at least one stage win and the fight to make it into the break of the day was growing ever more fraught as a consequence.For Basque veteran Ion Izagirre (Cofidis), due to retire at the end of the season, his squad's constant pushing for a still-elusive victory was helping keep him on his toes, right through to the end of the race.As Izagirre, 37, told , the team was exclusively concentrated on getting a stage win in a sprint or a break, but the opportunities for the latter in particular have rarely been so scarce."I'm still focused, at the end of the day, we've still got those goals here in the Tour, in my case it's about getting in the breaks. And that goes both for myself and the teammates."But it's not been easy for anybody; this Tour has been very hot and very difficult with very few breaks," pointed out Izagirre, who raced his first Tour of 12 back in 2013, and has wins in all three Grand Tours - the first a stage of the Giro back in 2012 in the third week."Just three have made it to the finish so far and it's not ideal for us, particularly because we brought a team specifically for breaks and sprints."Izagirre said that with so much still to play for, even if there were only four days left to go, and even if he had much more to see in the rear view mirror than he does ahead in terms of his professional career. "I'm not yet thinking that this is the last time in the Tour de France, I'll have time to get used to that idea. There's no time for nostalgia."I've always been somebody who simply looks at where they are without thinking too much about what else is out there. And right now I'm in the Tour and this is my last big race, just taking it on the day by day."Maybe when I come to see the race next year in the Pyrenees with my family, I'll miss it. But not now."His family have been with him on the race, too, but only at the start in Catalunya for now."They were there in Tarragona and in the Grand Départ in Barcelona, too," Izagirre says good humouredly, "and my wife will come to Paris, too, to say goodbye to the Eiffel Tower."And after that? While it is possible that he will do the Vuelta a España in September, and despite a very strong season so far including a victory on home soil in the GP Miguel Indurain, there is no truth in the rumour, he said, that he'll go on to race in Euskatel Euskadi in 2027. "Everything's signed off, my promise is made," confirmed Izagirre, set to end his racing career in a small cyclo-cross race this winter near his home in the Basque Country.Meantime there's the possibility of a breakaway on stage 18 to Orcières-Merlette, but that will almost definitely be the last-chance saloon for Izagirre in the Tour. Come the two stages to Alpe d'Huez, he has a realistic outlook on what can and can't be done, while Sunday's stage will be dedicated to working for teammates."For sure I'll be in the gruppetto, right at the back of the race. These Alpe d'Huez stages are for the GC guys, although we'll do what we can, and Paris is a good chance for [Cofidis sprinter] Alex Aranburu. Alpe d'Huez is something I'll just try to enjoy."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'It's hard to deal with' - Tour de France stage winner Jasper Philipsen opens up on struggle against pressure of expectations
item.content:encoded: It's been a long time coming, but it's finally here: 17 days into the Tour de France, Jasper Philipsen managed to clinch a much-anticipated 11th Tour stage victory of his career on Wednesday. And even a hardened sprint warrior like the 28-year-old Belgian admitted that the pressure to succeed - with virtually no chances remaining before Paris, either - was beginning to get to him.To say the expectations were high that Philipsen would at some point take a Tour stage in 2026 is no exaggeration. But in a sense he has only his own almost ridiculously good strike rate in the Tour de France to blame. Since 2022, the Alpecin-Premier Tech fastman has taken at least one stage win per Tour, with a maximum of four victories in 2023. In 2025 it was the only year when he 'only' took one, and had he not crashed out on stage 3, it would surely have been more. It was only in 2026 when the triumphs just wouldn't arrive.This was not for lack of trying, though. Inside the top five five times in the 2026 race before Wednesday's win, more than once the team's lead-out experts had put Philipsen in pole position to go for it. However, for whatever reason, the Belgian just wasn't firing on all cylinders, with two third places his best results prior to Wednesday, and when teammate and friend Mathieu van der Poel succeeded where Philipsen couldn't, taking a stunning breakaway sprint victory on stage 9, the contrast was even more noticeable.That all ended on stage 17, where a lead from Van der Poel from a 40-rider group saw Philipsen claim a victory after a hugely-fraught and tactically complex race through the Alpine foothills. Given the peloton will be heading into the mountains for three days from Thursday, the technical run-in to Voiron was effectively the last chance for Philipsen barring Paris - which is far from guaranteed to end in a sprint, either thanks to the Montmarte climb. But finally Philipsen could make his opportunity count. The Belgian star recognised afterwards that he had not found it easy to handle the anticipation within Alpecin that a win was in the offing, but he explained, too, how he tried to keep himself from getting overly worked up about it."Since stage 1, I'm here with a team without a GC rider, just focusing on stages, and Mathieu [van der Poel] got one already. But the team expected one from me and it hadn't happened so far," Philipsen, who has also closed in on the top spot in the points competition thanks to his win, said afterwards."That's been difficult to deal with, and [with] the pressure from the team. Nobody says anything, if I don't win they're not going to shoot me, but everybody knows what we're here for. There's a full lead-out squad, we're here for stage wins, there are always expectations."He tried to cope with that weight of expectations in the time outside actual racing, he explained. "The Tour de France is everywhere, it's all Tour de France. So you try to do something that is not reading or looking at the race. Anything that has nothing to do with cycling. "Then you just try to deliver, and deliver on the expectations that everybody has. However, if I don't have the feeling I can deliver, that increases pressure on myself, which is hard to deal with."Stage 17 was far from being a standard transition stage of the type Philipsen excels at winning, either. Hilly at first and then still very lumpy and technical in the second part, the race evolved into lengthy day-long battle between various different breakaway efforts, some, but not all, containing green jersey Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) and runner-up Philipsen. Matters reached a head when Pedersen formed part of a late five-rider move, chasing behind lone breakaway Jasper Stuyven (Soudal-QuickStep). Philipsen's counter-charge from a much larger group, though, also containing three Alpecin teammates - Van der Poel, Emiel Verstrynge and Edward Planckaert - finally swept up both Pedersen's five-rider break and then Stuyven with three kilometres to go. After which, matters came down to a small bunch sprint, and Philipsen had his chance to for that elusive win. It had been touch and go throughout the day, Philipsen admitted, to the point where he only began thinking the win was possible in the last 20 kilometres."When we saw the Pedersen group ahead, I knew Stuyven was away as well. But I had some teammates to ride in my group, so I knew it was a golden opportunity," Philipsen explained later."Van der Poel is very important in all my stage wins, not just today. When he was in the first group early on, we kept control of the race because he was there, and then he was there in the final to deliver me to the last line."That's a big advantage we have in our team. When Van der Poel does a leadout you want to deliver, you want to win. For sure it's pressure, but not many guys have that privilege, either."Van der Poel actually said afterwards that he had not expected to be able to do a lead out because he felt "dead", but Philipsen argued that in the third week of the Tour, the Dutch Champion was far from being the only one with that feeling."I think everybody was dead, and of course this was completely different to a normal sprint stage, it'd been a very hard race too. And at stage 17 the fatigue is there. I'm just happy Mathieu did what he could," Philipsen said.The stage win is now in the bag, in any case, so the next step is to see what other goals are possible, and with just seven points now separating him and Pedersen, the 2023 Tour points winner said he would not be ruling out a late bid against the Dane to wear green again in Paris."It will be very hard, he also took 25 points in an intermediate sprint today and the next three days are very tough as well."The chances are I won't be in the next three intermediates sprints in the mountains, and Pedersen can gain some points again. But we'll still see what's possible when we get to Sunday."If Philipsen takes green at the last minute in Paris, it'd be a hugely prestigious triumph against the odds. But come what may this weekend, his tenacious pursuit of that elusive stage win throughout the Tour, and finally being able to capture it on a stage that was far from certain to come down to a sprint, has already made his 2026 race yet another major success in his career.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'I completely messed up' – Devastated Michael Matthews saw a chance to win for his family at the Tour de France
item.content:encoded: Michael Matthews looking devastated at the finish of a bike race is certainly not a novelty, but his body language after stage 17 of the Tour de France Voiron on Wednesday indicated that this will rank up there as one of the bigger regrets of the Australian's career.Matthews, who wears his emotions on his sleeve, hunched over his bike and buried his face in his handlebars just beyond the finish line of stage 17. He remained there for several minutes.At separate moments, his wife and his daughter offered some comfort, but paradoxically it was their presence that made this all so painful."It's obviously amazing to have them here, but you want to win for them at the same time," Matthews said."Crossing the finish line where I finished… I knew they were waiting at the finish line, so it sucks."Matthews crossed the line in ninth place out of a breakaway group of 25 riders who contested the finish in Voiron. These reduced group sprints are usually where Matthews shines, but there was a purer sprinter in the ranks, Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech), and Matthews himself never found a route through the chaos in the twisting final kilometres."I just got completely swamped in the sprint," he explained."I tried to get back to the front in the corners but I just completely messed up the sprint."There was something of a consolation in the form of Matthews' teammate Mauro Schmid taking second place on the day, but it wasn't enough of a consolation."A podium place in the Tour de France is always good, but yeah, for me I expected more out of myself. These messy bunch sprints are always quite tricky with the timing and everything. I'm just disappointed in myself."As for how long this disappointment will linger, Matthews was pretty clear."We have a 12-hour rule in our team. We debrief the race, we say what we want to say in the debrief, we have 12 hours to get over it, and then we move on."Three summit finishes await, but Matthews will have one final chance in this Tour de France on the final day in Paris, which features the cobbled climb over Montmartre plus an extended flat section towards the finish line on the Champs-Élysées. item.title: ‘He can ventilate the frustration, but we stick to the line’ – Lidl-Trek director responds to Quinn Simmons’ comments on team’s Tour de France priorities
item.content:encoded: Lidl-Trek have responded to Quinn Simmons’ striking comments about the team’s goals at the Tour de France, acknowledging a sense of “frustration” from the US champion but insisting the team’s battle plan is not going to change.“I think the team has made it quite obvious they care more that Mads wins the green than I win a stage," Simmons said on Sunday. "And you know, at the end of the day, they write my paycheck, so I have to do what I'm told, and I'm fully here to support Mads in that in that goal to win the green jersey.”Although he never threatened to step out of line, Simmons’ comments caused quite a stir. Responding on Wednesday in conversation with , Sports Director Steven De Jongh acknowledged there was a degree of discontent in the camp, but not to an unhealthy extent.“I can understand his frustration, and also I think for [Mathias] Vacek it's the same. But we came here with a good goal and the goal was to win green with Mads, so they knew that and they know also that they have to help Mads because maybe it’s one of his last chances that he can seal the green jersey.“They stick to the plan, so that's good, and they can ventilate the frustration, there's nothing wrong with that, but we stick to the line.”Simmons has been active in the breakaways in this Tour de France, and he linked up with both Pedersen and Vacek on a stage 4 that the trio dominated tactically, with Pedersen taking out the victory. Simmons was regularly up the road in the second week, and was voted best teammate of the second week, with Pedersen also making early moves to secure green jersey points at the intermediate sprints.That theme continued on stage 17 on Wednesday, with Simmons part of a large breakaway that was smashed to pieces by Pedersen, who duly claimed maximum points at the intermediate sprint. However, the Dane would describe it as a "shit day", as his big green jersey rival Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) wasn't far behind at the intermediate sprint and then won the stage, while Pedersen could only manage eighth, which cut his points lead from 31 to just seven. Simmons, all the while, had to largely take a back seat in the secondary breakaway group, which eventually rejoined Pedersen's group for the sprint finish. “I think the frustration is… I would say that it's in the right place, you know. They are sportsmen, they want to win, but Quinn and Vacek, they have still a long career ahead,” said De Jongh, who also suggested this might not be a problem in the future in light of the team’s evolving ambitions.“I think when we are taking more of a direction of the GC in the future, they will have their chances in the stages.“We want to win the Tour de France, so then we invest more on the GC side, and then there's less space of course for the green jersey goals, so that helps from the perspective of the guys on the attack as well.”The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'It was a good decision' – Is Remco Evenepoel's controversial break from racing behind his rising Tour de France form arc?
item.content:encoded: Remco Evenepoel's decision to skip the Tour Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes and therefore not complete a single day of competition between Liège-Bastogne-Liège and the Tour de France caused quite a stir, and earned the Belgian plenty of criticism. As we head deep into the final week of the Tour, is vindication imminent?Evenepoel's form had been patchy – or at least difficult to read – at this Tour de France. The Belgian lost ground on numerous occasions, only to come roaring back again. On stage 10 in the Massif Central, he was dropped from the GC group but somehow ended up finishing best of the rest behind Tadej Pogačar, while on stage 14 in the Vosges he was dropped early on the Col du Haag but only conceded a few seconds on the line."I had the impression that during the Ardennes Classics I was a bit on the limit – that's really what I felt at Liège," Evenepoel said on Vélo Club show when asked about that racing gap from late April to the start of July."After Liège, I really took my time to prepare, to lose some kilos. We knew that the Tour de France was hard in the last 10 days, so I wanted to use the first week of the Tour to find my legs. But it's better to go into the last week like I am now. There is still this hunger to go and search for nice things, and I think that helped, not to do any competition before."This planned form arc was confirmed by Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe's director, Zak Dempster, who spoke to in Chambéry at the start of stage 17 on Wednesday morning."When you catch a plane to Paris to see the Tour parcours, it's really thinking about how you can get the most out of it, and this Tour de France was all about crescendo – you could see that straight away."We really set up the preparation and the tactics with that in mind, and hopefully that will play out this week."It was a change missing the Dauphiné, but at the same time to see him do that, you know, go quite steadily in his progression, and arrive fresh and ready… especially given how hard Dauphiné was, I think it was a good decision."Dempster described Evenepoel's first half of this Tour de France as "stable", despite the yo-yoing off the back of the GC group on multiple occasions."I think – well I don't think, I know – that both Florian [Lipowitz] and Remco both managed their efforts really well in the first part of the race. Even the stage where Remco got a bit dropped, if you look at the last five minutes of the climb, Remco was even faster than Jonas [Vingegaard]."So the way that they managed their efforts, I don't think they've ever gone over the limit actually. There have been hard moments, don't get me wrong, but they were both managing their efforts well, and hopefully that pays off this week."At the moment it's looking good, although of course you never know what could happen."The German, third overall at last year's Tour, had already declared his co-leadership over but was still in the top 10 on GC and would have been a key point of support for Evenepoel as the race now heads for three days in the Alps."It's a definite loss," Dempster said. "But we still have strong riders, and there are other factors at play, for example the teams classification, which is close between Lidl and UAE."Then I'm sure [Paul] Seixas isn't happy with where he's at, so they're going to have to move the race, and I would say Lidl-Trek have stages they could win from the breakaways, which plays into the teams classification, so hopefully we can find a way to use some of those things that are happening. But at the same time, if we need to take responsibility, we will."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Forget Tadej Pogačar and the GC, the Tour de France green jersey battle on stage 17 was way more fun - Analysis
item.content:encoded: Tadej Pogačar might be dominating the battle for the overall victory in the 2026 Tour de France, but the race was anything but dull on Wednesday's stage 17 to Voiron as the fight for the green jersey sparked aggressive, entertaining, and tactically thrilling racing.The Tour de France is a race of many battles, and what played out for the green jersey on the 175-kilometre stage was nothing short of fascinating.The points competition rules were tweaked this year after Tadej Pogačar's dominance often left him in the thick of the battle for the green jersey competition, and it has made the points battle far more tactical. More points are now available on designated flat stages, and the intermediate sprint is often placed soon after the start of stages. The changes have sparked a race-long battle between current points classification leader Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) and his sprint and baroudeur rivals.The Dane has worn the green jersey almost every stage since he won in Foix on stage 4, and went into stage 17 with a 31-point lead over Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech).Pedersen had marked the testing stage from Chambery to Voiron as one he could win through tactics and aggression, and he attacked like mad, finally forcing his way into a large breakaway before winning the intermediate sprint.However, the Dane was unable to prevent the chase group with Philipsen and teammate Mathieu van der Poel from rejoining before the finish, and after so many attacks, he had no response when Van der Poel surged in the final lead-out. Philipsen scored 50 points for his win and six at the intermediate sprint, while Pedersen's tally stood at 35 - 25 points in the intermediate sprint and 10 at the finish.After seeing the majority of his advantage vanish, Pedersen was not happy, making it clear that his and the Lidl-Trek tactics could have been far better."It is a proper shit day, an expensive day. I'm super disappointed." Pedersen said."It's a bad day and a big loss of points. This fight is going to go all the way to Paris."Pedersen will start the final mountain stages in the green jersey, but after their efforts on stage 17, both Pedersen and Philipsen will struggle to get into any breakaways over the early climbs to contest the intermediate sprints.On stage 18, the points sprint comes after 129 undulating kilometres including three categorised climbs before the mountain finish at Orcières-Merlette. On stage 19, they'd have to make it over two categorised climbs to reach the sprint at kilometre 89.4 of 127.9 before the finish atop Alpe d'Huez.The early parts of these stages are reasonable enough, but stage 20's second day to Alpe d'Huez is definitely out of reach with the hors-categorie Col de la Croix de Fer coming before the sprint at kilometre 66.5.The green jersey is likely to be decided on the Champs-Élysées during Sunday's final stage to Paris, where the intermediate sprint comes after a much easier 71 kilometres, on the third passage of the finish line. Importantly, it is before the three laps of the Montmartre climb and will surely be contested by Pedersen, Philipsen and the other sprinters.Philipsen is faster in a pure sprint and could arguably be the favourite to win the green jersey. He only has one problem: he has to survive the final mountain stages in the Alps and make it to Paris.No matter the outcome, the points classification is one prime reason to tune in early to the last four Tour de France stages and watch the mayhem unfold.UAE Team Emirates-XRG have tried to play down their interest in the team classification, insisting the yellow jersey is their primary goal. Isaac del Toro is also racing for the final podium and the best young rider's white jersey. Yet UAE can't hide their desire to win the team prize, adding an extra dimension to the tactics of stage 17 and the whole Tour. UAE won the team prize in 2024 but were beaten by Visma-Lease a Bike in 2025. Like in Formula 1, UAE want to win the equivalent of an individual pilot's title with Pogačar, and the Constructors' Championship title via the team prize. Team manager Mauro Gianetti knows that most of his sponsors in the UAE and team chairman Matar Suhail Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri want to stand on the podium in Paris as the best team of the 2026 Tour de France. The best team also takes home a nice prize of €50,000.That's why UAE have often chased down the breakaways where Mads Pedersen and other Lidl-Trek riders have been present. The team classification is determined by adding the times of each team's best three riders for each of the stages of the Tour. On stage 17, Lidl-Trek chased the intermediate sprint for Pedersen but also the stage victory. They also put Derek Gee-West in the move to try to gain some time, and it worked. Lidl-Trek won the stage 17 team prize with Gee-West, Pedersen and Quinn Simmons, while UAE were stuck in the peloton and lost 17:47. Lidl-Trek now lead UAE in the team competition by 18:10. Is that enough? Perhaps not.Pogačar is likely to chase further prestigious stage victories on L'Alpe d'Huez and try to help Del Toro finish on the podium. They will try to gain time on Juan Ayuso and Mattias Skjelmose, but the third rider on each team will also play a vital role. On the mountain stages, that is likely to be Gee-West of Simmons for Lidl-Trek, and Adam Yates for UAE. However, the Briton struggled to finish stage 17 due to stomach issues, so Brandon McNulty could become UAE's third and vital rider. Between now and Paris, watch out for how these riders finish, how much time they gain or lose to each other. Like the fight for the yellow jersey, the green jersey and the team prize are a race inside the race. The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage.
