Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: 'These controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping' –UCI backs use of night-time tests at the Tour de France
item.author: cyclingnews@futurenet.com (Dani Ostanek)
item.content:encoded: The UCI has released a statement in support of the International Testing Agency (ITA), following the organisation's recent night-time anti-doping controls at the Tour de France.Being selected for a test, including out-of-hours, is a standard part of anti-doping controls and does not imply any suspicion of, or wrongdoing by, the riders concerned. Neither Pogačar nor Vingegaard have been accused of any anti-doping violation.The tests provoked several strong reactions from riders – including Remco Evenepoel – and rider unions. The Cyclistes Professionnels Associés (CPA) released a statement in response to the tests calling for a "fairer fight against doping" and stating, "It is clear that this practice hinders athletes' recovery during the most important and difficult race on the global calendar and runs counter to the intended objectives."In response to the criticisms, the UCI released a statement approving the ITA's actions on Wednesday afternoon."Following a number of reactions in recent days to unannounced anti-doping controls carried out by the International Testing Agency (ITA) during the Tour de France – in particular following the statement issued by the association of professional riders (CPA) – the Union Cycliste Internationale (UCI) wishes to respond and to reaffirm that the fight against doping is an absolute priority," the statement read."The UCI of course acknowledges that nighttime testing can disrupt riders' rest and recovery, and wishes to reiterate that carrying out such controls outside normal hours remains an exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances. While fully aware of how onerous such a measure is for riders, the UCI nevertheless underscores that these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping."The UCI had been in charge of anti-doping in the sport before handing over management of testing to the ITA five years ago. The ITA is a Swiss-based organisation, which independently manages anti-doping programmes for various international sporting federations and event organisers.The UCI reiterated its support of the ITA in its mission."In 2021, the UCI entrusted the ITA with the management of its anti-doping programme – including the planning and execution of controls – in order to guarantee its independence and maximise its effectiveness," the UCI statement continued."With this in mind, the UCI fully supports the ITA's use of every means at its disposal to carry out its mission as effectively as possible, in compliance, naturally, with the World Anti-Doping Code of the World Anti-Doping Agency (WADA), UCI Regulations, and legislation applicable in each country for the conduct of controls (as in the present case, with the approval of the competent judge) and/or the gathering of intelligence."The UCI considers it essential that all stakeholders unite and support the anti-doping programme in place in cycling, which is now recognised by the relevant sporting authorities as a benchmark in this field."The requirement for independence, rigour and effectiveness in its anti-doping programme comes at a cost to cycling, one borne collectively by all of its stakeholders: every year, more than €10 million is invested in the collection, analysis and re-analysis of samples, as well as in intelligence-gathering, across events falling within the UCI's regulatory remit, including the Tour de France."This investment reflects the UCI's determination to protect riders who respect the rules, and to relentlessly pursue the reinforcement of its arsenal against those who cheat."The UCI will continue to support the ITA in carrying out its mission, in full and complete respect for its independence, in the service of a cleaner and more credible sport of cycling."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Adam Yates struggles against stomach problems and time cut to survive stage 17 of Tour de France
item.content:encoded: One of Tadej Pogačar's most important domestiques, Adam Yates (UAE Team Emirates-XRG), had a torrid day at the Tour de France on Wednesday as he struggled through illness to make the time cut, eventually fairly comfortably after some fears.On a rapid day of racing, Yates was dropped early – and on the flat – with around 80km to go, as he just seemed unable to push the pace anymore. He had not crashed, and it was later reported that he was sick, suffering from stomach problems on Tuesday. According to his teammate Pogačar, the British rider had felt better on Wednesday, but paid for riding the stage 16 TT through illness.Despite the finale of the stage being largely flat, the racing was very active and fast, which meant that as soon as Yates started drifting back, his gap started ballooning out, and he was more than 16 minutes down with still 20km to race – and no let-up in pace up front.Eventually, teammate Tim Wellens dropped back to try and support him until the finish line, but there were points when Yates was struggling even to keep up with the Belgian. But having a teammate to shepherd him through was a clear help.With a large gap and only getting further dropped, the question of whether Yates may be time cut from the race arose, especially with the pace of the stage only making the time cut shorter.Mercifully, though, and certainly thanks to the help of Wellens, Yates managed to finish within the time cut, 26:36 down on stage winner Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) and some 18 minutes down on the main part of the peloton. The day's time limit was 39:49.Yellow jersey Pogačar finished safely in that main group, and explained what was ailing his left-hand man."Adam was sick yesterday – he felt like sick in in the morning and throughout the day, so he suffered like a dog yesterday. Today in the morning he looked much better. He was eating good and all, but when you have a bad day like him yesterday, then you pay the day later," Pogačar said."I hope that he didn't go too deep into the hole and that he will be better tomorrow."Yates finished alongside Wellens and Anders Halland Johanessen (Uno-X Mobility), with Movistar's Jefferson Alveiro Cepeda the last rider to finish the stage, at 28:29.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: The Rapha Summer Sale is offering better than half price savings – here are my 15 best picks from the Men's and Women's ranges
item.author: paul.brett@futurenet.com (Paul Brett)
item.content:encoded: If like me, the Tour de France and glorious summer weather here in the UK have fueled your desire to get out riding, you'll be pleased to hear that Rapha has just dropped its big Summer Sale with huge reductions on new cycling gear.Below I've selected 15 of the best cycling deals, and if any catch your eye, our expert testers have reviewed and rated the majority of them. Anyone requiring a detailed deep dive, then a quick search on will provide just that.Rapha has not publicly announced an exact end date for this year's summer sale. However, based on the current timeline and their historical trends, we'd expect it to run until late August 2026. Don't hang around, though, as some items are showing as limited sizing, and like all sales, it's only while stock lasts.Pro Team Long Sleeve Lightweight JerseyRapha says the Pro Team Long Sleeve Jersey is developed for year-round versatility as part of a layering system; it's ideal for cooler and changeable weather days.There are various discounts of up to 50% off and 16 colour choices, with sizing from XS-XXL.Another essential item in your cycling wardrobe is a quality rain jacket. Rapha says its Core Rain Jacket is fully waterproof, wind-resistant, and light, making it the perfect partner for wet weather. It features a woven construction and taped seams to deliver weather protection to match any of the best waterproof jackets.There are 9 colour choices, but only 4 have this big discount, including the Bright Orange pictured. Sizes range from XS-XXL.Pro Team Training Jersey IiiThe Pro Team Training Jersey is a good choice as one of the best cycling jerseys. It features a dual-material construction to manage moisture, and there are three rear pockets to carry spares and nutrition, plus an essential zip pocket for keeping valuables secure.It comes in 12 colours with various discounts and sizes XS-XXXL.Pro Team Training Bib ShortsWhen it comes to my choice for the best bib shorts, the Pro Team Bibs from Rapha have proven durable and versatile. They are built with recycled fabrics that offer high stretch for your hardest rides, and the size-specific pad provides all-day comfort.These come in 2 colourways, but the Navy/White has the biggest discount. Sizes range from XS-XXL.Pro Team Powerweave Bib ShortsThe Pro Team Powerweave are the top of the range bib shorts from Rapha, and come with a price to match the performance. Designed to provide the ultimate hot weather riding performance, these bib shorts utilise innovative sweat-wicking materials, a breathable chamois and lightweight construction.There are 6 colour choices, with various discounts, and if you don't mind colour, then the Plum/Quartz option has a huge £90 off. Sizes range from XS-XXL.I think one of the best cycling gilets is an essential item in your kit, and the Brevet Insulated Gilet ticks all the boxes. It's lightweight and packable, and its active insulation is ideal for colder days on the bike.It has been cheaper in the past, but still an attractive buy with this discount. There are 3 colours and the Khaki version has the best reduction. Sizing varies but ranges from XS-XXL.The best cycling shoes, as the foundation of every ride, need to be comfortable, lightweight, and look the part too. The Classic Shoe by Rapha has all that in abundance and, with up to £80 off, is a bargain buy. They feature a double-wall lacing system designed to eliminate pinch points, a spacious toe box, sculpted heel, and low-cut upper for added comfort.There is a White and Black option, with the Black carrying the biggest discounts and a huge range of sizes.In my opinion, you can never have enough cycling socks. The best cycling socks can be expensive. At just £10, the Pro Team Socks are well worth stocking up on. They feature a soft, comfortable and high-wicking fabric for all-day performance.There are loads of colours for the all-important colour matching, with various reductions. Sizing is from XS-XL.Another riding essential is a pair of the best cycling sunglasses, and again can be an expensive outlay. The Rapha range of sunglasses are all discounted, and the Sestri model has a huge £55 off. Designed to provide complete protection and clear vision, they feature a contrast-enhancing lens technology that helps identify hazards and adapt to changes in terrain and light conditions.There are 3 colourways, but only 2 have the discount.The Core Jersey is designed for everyday use, from training to commuting. It is also made from 100% recycled fabric. At this bargain price, it makes it a staple choice for any cyclist's wardrobe and is a perfect pairing for the Core Bibs below.There are a massive 15 colour options, but discounts vary. Sizing also varies and is from XXXS-XXL.Rapha claim its Core Bib Shorts are the perfect balance between comfort and performance. There are no additional women's-only features which appear on some of the best women's cycling shorts, making comfort breaks easier. However, at just £75, they are still a great buy.There are 6 colours available, but the Blue/White option has the biggest reduction. Sizing ranges from XXS-XXL.Women's Brevet Long Sleeve Windstopper JerseyThe Brevet Long Sleeve Windstopper Jersey is a windproof and rain-resistant outer layer with added insulation. The Gore-Tex fabric has less stretch than other Brevet jerseys, and Rapha say you may prefer to size up.There are 2 colour choices both with 50% off, but the Black version has the bigger choice of sizes, which ranges from XXS-XL.The Classic Jersey is a best seller fro Rapha, and with 50% off it's a great buy. It features a merino wool blend which is lightweight, soft, breathable and versatile.There are only 2 colours available, and limited sizing from XXS-XL.Pro Team Insulated Gore-Tex Rain JacketThe Pro Team Insulated Gore-Tex Rain Jacket is available with a huge £132 off. Highlights include a fully waterproof and insulated Gore-Tex Paclite Plus material, which is claimed to deliver breathable weather protection. There is also reflective detailing, an internal chest pocket and two oversized rear pockets for securely storing valuables and spares.Only available in Black, and sizes XXS-XL.The Pro Team Insulated Gilet is highly adaptable and designed for colder conditions. It packs down into a jersey pocket when not in use. Featuring Octa insulation and a tailored fit for added comfort.There are 3 colour options with varying reductions; sizes range from XXS-XL.The highlighted deals are from the UK Rapha Summer Sale, but for those in the USA, the has plenty of equally appealing discounts.Below you'll also find the best prices on Rapha apparel from other retailers, relevant to your currency and location.

item.title: Tour de France: Jasper Philipsen wins explosive sprint finish on stage 17 as points classification battle tightens
item.author: laura@cyclingnews.com (Laura Weislo)
item.content:encoded: Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) finally achieved his main goal in the 2026 Tour de France, taking the victory in a chaotic stage 17 over Mauro Schmid (Jayco-AlUla) and Olav Kooij (Decathlon CMA CGM) from a large breakaway.The hard-fought win came after green jersey leader Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) blew the race apart with unbridled aggression, winning the intermediate sprint but fading in the mayhem of the bunch sprint to finish eighth.Earlier in the stage, Pedersen bridged across to a 19-man breakaway after a frantic first half, successfully holding off his green jersey rival for the intermediate sprint with 28km to go. But the Belgian's chasing group far outnumbered the Dane's, and the two came together to battle for the stage win.While Pedersen slotted onto Philipsen's wheel in the final turn, he couldn't hold on as the Belgian's teammate Mathieu van der Poel started the final leadout, and was washed away as the sprint opened up.For all his efforts, Pedersen's lead in the points classification over Philipsen was reduced to just seven points at the end of the day.The race leader Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates XRG) was more than happy to let the sprinters take centre stage, and rolled across the line with the rest of the top GC riders 8:46 behind the winner to maintain his lead of 4:32 over Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe). Isaac del Toro, after a brief scare being dropped in the early part of the stage, finished in the same group to keep third overall at 6:51.Philipsen was overjoyed to finally get a stage win after two weeks of fighting."After such a roller coaster of emotions in this Tour de France, I had to wait until stage 17. I prepared very well - I was in the shape of my life, but the first 12 days were just horrible, and I couldn't find myself. I couldn't find the feeling I was hoping for. And after 17 stages to take the victory, it's really one for the team that keep on believing in me," the Belgian said."This one is for them to keep on believing and just fighting back until stage 17."Pedersen was bitterly disappointed after putting so much into the stage only to lose ground, but like Philipsen, said he would keep fighting all the way to the finish in Paris."Yeah, this was a proper shit day. Let's say it like this: it was an expensive day on points, so super disappointed," Pedersen said."It doesn't work to be impressive and not get the job done. So in the end, it's a bad day, and it's a big loss of points - that's not what we hoped. But it is what it is, and we have to deal with this situation and hope to score points on the next days."With Lidl-Trek having pinned stage 17 of the Tour de France at the top of their to-win list for green jersey leader Mads Pedersen, the first half of the 175-kilometre journey from Chambéry to Voiron was positively frantic.An early move came from perpetual attacker Baptiste Veistroffer (Lotto-Intermarché) and Jonas Abrahamsen (Uno-X Mobility), but two were never going to hold off the furious pace.The race came back together on the Côte de Bassa as Netcompany Ineos tried to get another escape started with Kévin Vauquelin and Josh Tarling, but the green jersey holder himself mowed them down.The mayhem sparked a crash that affected GC contenders Tom Pidcock (Pinarello-Q36.5) and Lenny Martinez (Bahrain Victorious), but both were quickly back up and riding.A four-man move briefly went clear on the Côte de Rossillon, but again were reeled in as polka dot jersey renter Valentin Paret-Peintre (Soudal-QuickStep) claimed more mountain points.On the longest climb, the Col des Prés, Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech) tried to pull a group away, but Pedersen himself worked to ensure the race came back together after the top.The Côte de Saint-Jean d'Arvey was next, and a split in the peloton with Isaac del Toro on the losing end led to a brief lull as the race came back together before exploding again on an uncategorised climb after the descent.When the dust cleared, a group of 16 riders went clear without Pedersen or his green jersey rivals, but the vicious opening had gotten rid of Biniam Girmay (NSN) and a load of other sprinters.The breakaway was high quality but even so, they couldn't make huge gains. Egan Bernal and Josh Tarling (Netcompany Ineos), Tiesj Benoot (Decathlon CMA CGM), Jai Hindley (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), Quinn Simmons (Lidl-Trek), Pablo Castrillo and Einer Rubio (Movistar), Guillaume Martin Guyonnet and Clement Braz (Groupama-FDJ), Alex Baudin (EF Education-EasyPost), Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech), Jasper Stuyven (Soudal-QuickStep), Rick Pluimers (Tudor), Matej Mohoric (Bahrain Victorious), Felix Engelhardt (Jayco-AlUla), and Per Strand Hagenes (Visma-Lease a Bike) were up there but more intrigue was to come.Because Olav Kooij held onto the main peloton and Lidl-Trek had a man up the road, Decathlon CMA CGM took charge of chasing the breakaway for their sprinter as Visma-Lease a Bike sent Matteo Jorgenson after the leaders along with Ben Healy (EF Education-EasyPost) and Mathis le Berre (TotalEnergies).When the trio joined the first peloton they only had half a minute as Uno-X Mobility began helping chase the leaders down. But when the gap was down to 20 seconds, Pedersen launched a huge attack and went across to the lead group with 10 others including Kooij, leaving Philipsen in pursuit with 13 companions and 65km to go.Once the green jersey joined, Simmons put in a huge effort to keep Philipsen's chasing group at bay and then Pedersen forced another split in the attack group still with 30km to go before the intermediate sprint.With Pedersen was Healy, Michał Kwiatkowski (Netcompany Ineos), Stuyven, Edward Planckaert (Alpecin-Premier Tech) and Engelhardt, and with all six cooperating, they opened up a full minute on the chase and over three on the yellow jersey group.With 42km to go, Philipsen linked up with Van der Poel in the first chase and had a 1:16 gap to Pedersen but the now 38-man group wouldn't make the catch before the intermediate sprint with 28km to go. Pedersen walked away with the 25 points uncontested while Philipsen had to settle for 7 points from the chase.Stuyven attacked after the sprint and there wasn't an immediate reaction. Instead, Pedersen eased up and allowed the Philipsen group to gain ground and catch them with 15km to go, trusting in his abilities in with the uphill run into the sprint.Jayco-AlUla led the chase of Stuyven but the Belgian was putting up a solid resistance, heading into the final 5km with 20 seconds. But the unnamed climb got in the Belgian's way and he was caught with 3.7km to go.Hagenes tried to preempt the sprint with a late attack but was marked by Pedersen's teammate Derek Gee-West. When they were caught, Simmons took charge as Pedersen followed wheels.But Philipsen had a secret weapon in Van der Poel, and when the Dutchman surged out of the final turn, Pedersen had no answer and could only watch as Philipsen waltzed away with the stage win and closed the gap in the points classification.Jasper Philipsen (Bel) Alpecin-Premier TechMauro Schmid (Swi) Jayco-AlUlaOlav Kooij (Ned) item.title: What Alonso needs from Aston Martin and F1 as career decision looms
Tour de France Stage 17: Jasper Philipsen Reignites Green Jersey Race with Breakaway Win
item.author: ahood
item.content:encoded: Tour de France Stage 17 results: Belgian star rescued his Tour with a breakaway victory featuring Quinn Simmons as UAE mountain lieutenant Adam Yates suffered before three decisive Alpine stages.

item.title: One Second, Three Titles, and Glitter on the Podium: FoCo Fondo Year 11
item.author: Kelly Matkovich
item.content:encoded: A heartbreaking disqualification handed Caleb Bottcher the men's sprint by the thinnest of margins. Lauren Stephens made it three straight. Robin Cummings stopped to reapply glitter mid-race and still won by an hour.

item.title: The Amount of Gear in a Tour de France Team Truck Is Staggering
item.author: Dave Everett
item.content:encoded: It takes a lot more than just bikes to survive the Tour. Here is a look at the exact inventory Lotto Dstny packs to fuel the riders and fix the fleet.

item.title: Garmin Buys TrainingPeaks in Fitness Landscape Power Move
item.author: Jim Cotton
item.content:encoded: Garmin acquires TrainingPeaks and TrainHeroic to bring two leading training and data platforms into its tech empire.

item.title: Manchester United
item.content:encoded: Wes Brown on Michael Carrick and the back four he'd pick to start this season.

item.title: Early Access: Watch our Van Rysel RCR-F review before it goes live on YouTube
item.author: cyclingnews@cyclingnews.com (Cyclingnews)
item.content:encoded: subscribers get early access to all of our new bike review videos before they go live on YouTube. To join our ever-growing community of cycling-obsessed subscribers and support our world-class cycling journalism, join Cyclingnews Senior Tech Writer Will Jones recently took this bike to the French Alps to put it through its paces on some of the roads that Seixas and the rest of the Tour de France peloton will be tackling this week. Not yet subscribed? Don't worry, you can get early access to this in-depth video review, plus a whole host of other brilliant features, including our Cyclingnews Labs testing, unlimited access to our unrivalled racing coverage, and member-exclusive investigations on the biggest storylines within cycling, by becoming a Cyclingnews subscriber today.

item.title: VIDEO: Tadej Pogačar Somehow Stays Upright and Avoids Tour de France Disaster
item.author: ahood
item.content:encoded: Roadside video shows Pogačar using incredible bike-handling skills to escape crashing in the same high-speed corner that ended Florian Lipowitz’s Tour.

item.title: All Tour de France riders provided biological passport samples on rest day, anti-doping body confirms
item.content:encoded: All riders still in the Tour de France provided blood samples on Monday's rest day as part of the Athlete Biological Passport (ABP) programme, a longitudinal way of monitoring biological variables in riders' blood that sits apart from regular anti-doping tests.First reported by and , has received the same information confirming that the 166 riders still left in the race on the second rest day provided samples to the International Testing Agency (ITA) with support from the French anti-doping agency, the AFLD.All riders on the Tour start list also provided samples in the days running up to the start of the race in Barcelona."The International Testing Agency (ITA) confirms that a comprehensive blood Athlete Biological Passport (ABP) testing mission was carried out during the Tour de France rest day on 20 July 2026. All riders in the peloton were included in the testing mission, which forms part of the ITA’s ongoing intelligence- and risk-based anti-doping program for the race," a statement provided by the ITA reads."This mission complements the standard pre-competition testing conducted on all riders in the days preceding the Grand Départ and any test already conducted during the race. Collecting additional blood samples enables the ITA to assess variations in relevant haematological biological markers and strengthen the longitudinal monitoring of each rider’s individual profile. It was supported by sample collection personnel from the French national anti-doping agency - AFLD."The ABP was introduced to cycling in 2008, and acts as a longitudinal way for anti-doping authorities to detect any changes over time in biological levels in the blood such as hematocrit levels and other variables. The programme "does not seek to detect a prohibited substance or method directly", in the words of the ITA."Instead, it monitors selected biological variables over time to identify changes that may indicate the effects of doping and inform further anti-doping action," it said.Providing regular samples for the ABP programme is distinct from standard anti-doping testing, and is not indicative of any wrongdoing nor suspicion of wrongdoing, and all riders in men's WorldTeams and ProTeams are automatically part of the programme, regardless of any other factors. Samples are routinely collected at regular intervals, including before, during and after big events, and out of competition.ABP samples do not provide 'results', per se; rather, the data is stored and analysed over time.Testing at the Tour de France has been in headlines over the last few days, after multiple riders spoke out about being woken up during the night by testers. Before the race, the ITA confirmed that they would be carrying out some 600 tests during the Tour, which equates to just over 25 per day.Usually, these routine tests do not make headlines, and regular testing at the Tour is nothing out of the ordinary, but has become a major talking point this year.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: 'Might be the best TT I've ever done' – Tom Pidcock continues to impress amid best Tour de France GC bid yet
item.content:encoded: Tom Pidcock has continued to enjoy the best Tour de France GC campaign of his career so far, riding the "best TT he's ever done" on stage 16 to make a top 10 finish by the time the race reaches Paris look likely. Admitting how little he trains the discipline and not known for his abilities against the clock, despite winning the junior world title in Bergen some nine years ago, Pidcock surprised himself to finish 12th on Tuesday's 26.1km test, just behind TT specialists and former teammates Filippo Ganna and Josh Tarling. "I don't know how I finished there, but I know at the top of the climb, I was basically like the fastest time from everyone who had been before me, so probably I faded at the end," said Pidcock as he recounted his effort at the finish. "I also took it super slow down the descent. It's a scary descent with those walls. That might be the best TT I've ever done, [which is] good for the fuck all training I do on this bike."The Pinarello Q36.5 rider sits eighth overall at the start of stage 17, with just under a 10-minute lead over Jordan Jegat in ninth, and, because he is 12:58 behind race leader Tadej Pogačar, the Brit is hoping to combine his GC goals with that of chasing another stage win. "Top 10 unless something goes badly wrong, it's pretty reasonable to say it's achievable," said the Brit. "I think I'm far enough down that I can maybe try and get in some more breakaways and go for some stages. But I think a top 10 will be great for me and the team. I think that's a big thing, but obviously, I want to try and win a stage too."Pidcock won his first stage at the Tour in 2022 from the breakaway, after an iconic descent off of the Col du Galibier, before winning atop Alpe d'Huez. He'll return to the area as the Tour heads for a doubleheader to Alpe d'Huez from two different sides on Friday and Saturday's Queen stage.He's admitted that he isn't at the same level as when he finished third overall at the Vuelta a España last September, confirming his long-awaited potential as a GC challenger, but Pidcock continues to find a way to maximise his standing, having been in the breakaway three days in a row before the second rest day. Expect more of the same in these final days. "I don't have the level I had in the Vuelta last year, so I think on these mountaintop finishes, I'll lose a couple of minutes every time, probably," he admitted. "The breakaway is a good way to try and get ahead, but I do think I'm getting stronger. I mean, yesterday I felt the legs after the three days in the break; that was probably the easiest rest day ride I've done this year [on Monday], and [yesterday] morning also on the bike my legs were not moving. But then now when I was going, I felt super strong. I mean, if it was a 20-minute climb, I think I would have done my best ever 20 minutes today on TT bike."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: F1 drivers raise concerns over 'self-learning' power units – what's going on?
What is holding back Bagnaia in MotoGP 2026?
'If you did six watts per kilo, you were good. Now you're a teammate' – Barguil worries about burnout for young riders as Tour de France levels rise rapidly
item.author: cyclingnews@futurenet.com (Dani Ostanek)
item.content:encoded: As 19-year-old Paul Seixas prepares to do battle for a Tour de France podium spot in the race's final week, veteran French racer Warren Barguil has warned against the dangers of burnout for young riders in the peloton and those hoping to turn pro.34-year-old Barguil, who won two stages and the mountain classification in 2017, is racing his 12th Tour de France this summer. He turned pro at the age of 20 back in 2013, and made his Tour debut aged 23.Speaking to on Monday, he voiced concern over young cyclists coming up through the system at the moment. The Picnic PostNL racer even suggested that the UCI should step in to place a limit on the races the youngest riders in the peloton could take part in."We did training sessions to show off to our friends. We had fun," Barguil said, reflecting on his younger years on the bike"Now, it's structured. My father, who coaches, has stopped working with the juniors. At one point, he realised he'd be forced to ask too much of them, just to keep up. He didn't want to contribute to the problem."Even in the junior category, he told me he has to hold back young riders who want to do four hours of cycling on their rest day. The UCI needs to do something about it: limit the age of riders in WorldTour races."Barguil said that the level is rising every year, saying that when he won stage 18 on the Col d'Izoard in 2017, his power output for the climb was 5.9 W/kg. He rode to 10th overall in Paris back then, but suggested that today's top Tour riders need to output an extra watt per kilogram to match that achievement."5.9 W/kg, back then, was among the top power outputs! Now, you need almost a watt per kilogram more. It's crazy," he said."People don't realise how much the level has risen. My generation saw the gap widen. And the new generation is coming, doing 7 W/kg for 20 minutes."Before, if you did 6 W/kg, you were good. Now you're a teammate."Barguil is currently racing his second Grand Tour of the season, having finished 57th at the Giro d'Italia. His best result so far this July has been 58th on Sunday's stage 15 to the Plateau de Solaison, and he suggested that this might be his last outing at the race"On Sunday, I tried to break away a bit more at the start of the stage. It’s the first time I’ve actually enjoyed it. It’s been a long haul. The previous fourteen stages were more about survival. But I’m thinking this might be my last Tour," Barguil said."It took 80km to get away. Everyone is giving 300%. Then, the first thing everyone does is drop back into the gruppetto to conserve energy and try again the next day. That's what makes the speed so high. "On the long 7km climb [the early Côte de Rousses], I think three-quarters of the guys who tried to get in the breakaway almost beat their 20-minute personal best. It's crazy. The level of competition means it's not like it used to be."As the peloton's performance level rises beyond anything seen before, Barguil suggested that France's new phenomenon, Seixas, the youngest man to race the Tour since 1937, is an exception among young riders. He said that many other riders will "burn out" trying to make it as pros."Paul Seixas is exceptional, but that shouldn't be possible before 20 or 21. That could reduce this phenomenon. Because while Paul is doing well, I know plenty of others who burn out because they couldn't overcome it," he said."We quickly idealise the successful guy, but many fall flat on their faces. I think we don't talk about it enough. I know a young woman who had a burnout at 17 because she was fed up with cycling."It's a societal phenomenon that needs to be addressed in sports because it's become extreme, too extreme. I'm worried about the young people who will drop out of school early. Their social circle is limited to cycling. They don't have any friends anywhere else."If it all stops overnight, what will they be able to rely on? Everyone says there's doping among the pros. But now they're going to end up trying to turn pro by doping. And I'm really afraid of that for the young people."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'Make the best of it!' – Over an emotional burger and fries, Jonas Vingegaard encouraged his Visma-Lease a Bike teammates to go on the attack at the Tour de France
item.content:encoded: Jonas Vingegaard underwent surgery on his fractured collarbone in Denmark on Tuesday, encouraging his Visma-Lease a Bike teammates to fight on in the Tour de France after an emotional farewell over a hamburger and fries.Vingegaard crashed with 21km of stage 15 remaining, as Visma-Lease a Bike worked to close down a breakaway. Victor Campenaerts led the team through a difficult turn, opting for a wide line at speed through the French traffic furniture.The Dane was several riders back and appeared to slip out and hit the kerb. He immediately knew he had fractured his collarbone and left the Tour in an ambulance.“Maybe we came into this roundabout a little too fast, and then my front wheel just slips. I can't do anything. That's how cycling races are sometimes," Vingegaard explained.He travelled home on Tuesday morning and immediately underwent surgery."Everything went well," Visma told the Danish media, with some pundits suggesting that Vingegaard could recover quickly and even target the Road World Championships in Canada in late September."They did what they had to. It wasn't the most complicated operation you can have. Everything went well," Visma sports director Marc Reef told the TV 2 Danish television channel. He also opened up on Vingegaard's plans for the rest of the season."It's something we'll discuss after the Tour, and after the Tour we'll sit down and talk about what's going to happen. It's already been a tough season," Reef said. TV2 suggested that Reef also suggested that it i possible that Vingegaard will get back on the bike this year but also possible that he won't. Vingegaard won the Giro d'Italia and raced for 51 days in 2026.Losing Vingegaard when he was second overall, and so suddenly, was a shock to his teammates. However, the Dane joined them at dinner on the eve of the rest day and encouraged them to race on, insisting the crash wasn't their fault."He came to the hotel. He had probably already had his difficult moment and he will likely take another hit when he gets home, but he had to comfort us more than the other way around," Campenaerts told after completing the stage 16 time trial."We had a nice dinner, very cosy. Usually we have burgers and fries, but those are Food Coach burgers and fries. They're tasty but different from the chip shop. There was a McDonald's in the nearby village, and Jonas went to get real burgers and fries. He sat at our table. It was very pleasant. I think everyone went back to the room feeling good."Campenaerts revealed to that Vingegaard told him not to feel any blame for the crash."He told me: 'Don't get any crazy ideas in your head, OK? There are still six days of racing left in the biggest race in the world. Make the best of it!'"Matteo Jorgenson admitted that Visma had no Plan B, but the team quickly refocused and reset their goals. Bruno Armirail finished eighth in the time trial, Davide Piganzoli could fight for a top ten place on GC, and other riders will target stage victories in the mountains."We still have a good group, and the focus is already switched. We miss Jonas, but we're looking forward to just going for the next stages together, and we should have a lot of fun," Sepp Kuss said after his time trial."That is my motivation to bring out the best in myself, to try to win at the Tour de France," Campenaerts told ."Potentially we can win all the other stages. That is a difficult switch. Trying to win stages hasn't been on my agenda for a while now. But we are going to make a good plan with the team."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: Why the 2026 F1 cars suit Antonelli's driving style more than Russell
item.content:encoded: Six wins, six pole positions and the Formula 1 championship lead. While one year ago Kimi Antonelli had faced one of his toughest weekends in F1, he has now taken a sweet revenge, claiming a victory that will mathematically allow him to head into the summer break as the championship leader.But the meaning of Spa goes well beyond that.The Belgian round, where last season he struggled with a ...Keep reading

item.title: As it happened: Alpecin-Premier Tech emerge as the masters from an epic breakaway day on Tour de France stage 17
item.content:encoded: The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!Good morning and welcome to ' live coverage of stage 17 of the Tour de France!Here's a look at the profile today, which is listed as a flat stage by the Tour's official website, but the four categorised climbs in the first 60km of the route might have something to say about that.Heading southwest from Chambéry down to Voiron, it's likely the final chance for the breakaway specialists to have their chance, with three brutal mountain stages and the finale in Paris likely to favour the top GC riders better.A key point on the route is the positioning of the intermediate sprint in Colombe, where current green jersey points leader, Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek), will be looking to defend against Alpecin-Premier Tech's Jasper Philipsen. But will either of them be able to make it and fight out the points available at the finish? Lidl will be the favourites as Pedersen is the better climber and has already won from a breakaway this Tour, but Alpecin have Mathieu van der Poel as a second option for the stage if green looks impossible. Either way, it should be fast and explosive right from the flag drop at 13:30 CEST.All of the riders are currently signing on at the start in Chambéry. You can watch the team presentation live before every stage on Le Tour's X account: Podium signature stage 17. After the leaving the race following his stage 15 crash, Jonas Vingegaard has reportedly undergone successful surgery to his fractured collarbone. Despite the sadness of having to abandon the race, the Dane showed his leadership as he bid farewell to his teammates on Monday, with the remaining riders changing focus to chasing stages in these final five days:Just 10 minutes to go now until the unofficial start of stage 17. It's more blue skies and hot weather welcoming the riders to Chambéry on this Wednesday afternoon.No prizes for guessing which bus tends to be the busiest at each of the stage starts. Anything to get a peak at the yellow jersey Tadej Pogačar, but also the ultr-popular Isaac del Toro. Maybe only Decathlon CMA CGM's bus and the furore surrounding French hope Paul Seixas rivals this.Riders are gone from the start line of stage 17 of the Tour de France in Chambéry! The flag should be dropped at kilometre 0 in around 15 minutes. From then on, expect major breakaway action all the way to the line.Pogačar's expectations for stage 17Here's what race leader Tadej Pogačar had to say at the start, and he's expecting anything but an easy day, knowing full well how aggressive the opening two hours will likely be:"Very hard start and hopefully an easier finish, but you never know. I think it's quite a difficult day in my opinion. It looks not so hard, but it's going to be a pain in the ass," said the Slovenian, though it sounds like UAE won't be overly involved if they don't have to be."We are close for team classification also, and it's something the guys would really like to have, but it's not the main priority of course, so we'll see what happens."After a long section of neutralised riding to build the tension even more, the 17th stage of the Tour is underway, and fireworks are expected. Don't take your eyes off it.The earliest moves came from Baptiste Veistroffer (Lotto-Intermarché) and Jonas Abrahamsen (Uno-X Mobility). Behind them, it was obvious more riders were ready to go – the front of the peloton very much resembling a coiled sprint right now.There's some tricky road furniture to navigate just to add to the expected madness of this stage start. The riders are heading briefly downhill out of Chambéry, before they reach the foot of a long section of climbing with 163km to go.It's still Veistroffer and Abrahamsen leading the race on stage 17, and they have a slim lead of around 20 seconds on the peloton. Kasper Asgreen (EF Education-Easypost) and Marco Haller (Tudor) had set off in pursuit, but a huge group has just attacked off the front of the peloton and joined them.Massive crowds welcoming the riders in this early phase of racing.Davide Ballerini (XDS Astana) has come down in a crash, sliding out after a roundabout. As a result, there are splits in the peloton.That big chasing group came back together to the main bunch, which is still. all in one long line as the attacks continue to fly.The two early leaders on stage 17, Abrahamsen and Veistroffer. The peloton is exploding behind them with Ben Healy (EF Education EasyPost) Julian Alaphilippe (Tudor) among the latest groups trying to break away.The two early breakaway were both reeled in at the foot of the first categorised climb of stage 17, the Côte de Bassa (1.5km at 5.5%). Alaphilippe and Healy were both in the group that came across to them, but with almost half of the peloton on the more this afternoon, the race was quickly back together with 157.5km to go.Netcompany Ineos are really looking to get something out of today's stage, knowing they likely won't be able to make much impact in the high mountains. They've had Kévin Vauquelin and Josh Tarling on the attack on the first climb.Vauquelin was solo for the opening part of the climb, as the peloton lulled momentarily. Approaching the final 500 metres, though, he was passed by a group led by green jersey Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) and polka-dot jersey Valentin Paret-Peintre (Soudal-QuickStep). The Frenchman took the maxmimum points at the crest, but it's almost straight onto the next climb with the peloton still together.Lidl-Trek have been very active, as expected, with Pedersen, Quinn Simmons and Mathias Vacek all among the attackers at the start. They've not managed to split things fully to form a break, but they will be looking for a repeat of stage 4 to Foix.On that day, it was the three riders mentioned above who got into the day's break and completely dominated proceedings, with Simmons and Vacek marking every move in the finale for Pedersen to sprint to the win.That day was carefully planned out by the team months in advance, and with today's parcours suiting their riders perfectly, similar can be expected from Pedersen and his domesqieus.Several riders have been taken down in the peloton on stage 17, as the aggresive start causes chaos. Tom Pidcock was down but quickly back on his bike and looked largely unaffected, but his Pinarello Q36.5 teammate and British champion Fred Wright came down more heavily, left with road rash and checking a wound on his left hand. Also involved in the incident was fellow GC top 10 rider Lenny Martinez (Bahrain Victorious), Marc Hirschi (Tudor), Rob Stannard (Bahrain Victorious), Dylan van Baarle (Soudal-QuickStep), and Alexandre Delettre (TotalEnergies).All of the riders involved are back on the bikes and everyone seems to have escaped anything more sinister.A new trio attacked off the front: Max Walker (EF Education-EasyPost), Harold Tejada (XDS Astana) and Ion Izagirre (Cofidis). Anthony Turgis (TotalEnergies) attacked across to make it a leading quartet, with Ben Healy also coming across to join his teammate. They are approaching the foot of the next climb on today's route, the Côte de Rossillon (1.7km at 5.3%).That leading group of five did not last long on the second categorised climb of stage 17, with the peloton quickly swallowing them back up. EF were quickly on the attack again, however, with Richard Carapaz taking the maximum King of the Mountains points at the crest and continuing on with the move. Green jersey Pedersen and polka-dot jersey Paret-Peintre were not far away as the race went over the top. 37km raced at full gas and an average speed of 45kph, mostly uphill, but still no breakaway. It's going to be a long one...Lidl-Trek are still trying to crack the race apart with Pedersen and Simmons, but with so many teams chasing to try not to miss out, the balance is swinging in favour of the sprinters who are surviving these opening stages well enough. Just as I typed, though, Mathieu van der Poel (Alpecin-Premier Tech) has blinked and attacked in the latest move. Phe peloton is exploding into life again, and the presence of Alpecin's main man would suggest they are OK to let the final sprint chance for Jasper Philipsen go in favour of their big star having a go from the break.This latest break is full of big name stars, notably Van der Poel, Matteo Jorgenson (Visma-Lease a Bike), Tiesj Benoot (Decathlon CMA CGM), Jasper Stuyven (Soudal-QuickStep), Egan Bernal (Netcompany Ineos). It's not fully going away, though, gap still at just 10 seconds. Still, any team who has missed it is pacing in the peloton behind, knowing this group of very strong riders could be the one that goes. There's around 22 riders in the front for the moment.The gap to the breakaway finally started increasing at the foot of the longest climb of the day: the Col des Prés (3.5km at 6.8%). Alpecin-Premier Tech continued to ride at the front of the peloton, even with Van der Poel up the road in the break, but with no real venom in their pace. Soon after though, having missed it, green jersey Pedersen went off on the attack on his own with a 22-second gap to make up. He was followed by several others who didn't make the initial move, George Bennett (NSN), Vlad van Mechelen (Bahrain Victorious), and Alpecin's Tim Marsman. Visma kept the pace high in the front group with the gap to the peloton coming down quickly, with that Pedersen move threatening to undo all the work of those in the latest break. US champ Simmons actually dropped from the front group to try and help the Dane, but he started to pay for his efforts towards the top. Still, the race came back together before the top of the climb, despite the efforts of Van der Poel, who took the maximum KOM points at the front of the splitting peloton.A look inside one of several groups who tried to attack away in these early stages, with Van der Poel, Simmons, Tarling and Bernal involved. That action has come back together, and the race is now onto a long section of downhill roads before the final categorised climb of the day, where riders like those seen below will surely try again...Côte de Saint-Jean d'ArveyOnto the final categorised climb of the day, the Côte de Saint-Jean d'Arvey (1.1km at 5.7%) and the attacks are coming thick and fast again. Matej Mohorič (Bahrain Victorious) led things into the foot, but it's Van der Poel, Bernal, Simmons and Tarling who are at it once more on the attack. More huge crowds welcomed the riders onto the slopes, where Van der Poel really surged to split things up. Visma have done much of the chasing in the splits behind to try and re-form a huge break at the front. 2019 yellow jersey winner Bernal led the race over the crest of the climb, with no more categorised ascents to come, but with 114km of more thrilling racing still to come.Most of the big GC names are in the front group of the race, which has come back together, but notably missing is third place overall Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates-XRG). The Mexican star is in the group at 40 seconds from the peloton – where Pogačar, Evenepoel and Seixas are – which also includes green jersey and stage win hope Pedersen, who dropped back after all of his early efforts. Finally after all of the all-out action, there seems to be a breakaway forming on stage 17. There are now 16 riders in the front: Per Strand Hagenes (Visma-Lease a Bike), Jai Hindley (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), Alex Baudin (EF Education-EasyPost), Pablo Castillo, Einer Rubio (Movistar), Clément Braz Afonso, Guillaume Martin (Groupama-FDJ United), Rick Pluimers (Tudor), Simmons, Van der Poel, Bernal, Tarling, Stuyven, Engelhardt, Benoot, and Mohorič.The splits in the peloton have been undone, with the Del Toro and Pedersen group rejoining Pogačar and the rest of the GC favourites in the second group on the road. Decathlon are pacing on the front in what is now the peloton, who are 42 seconds behind the leaders.Some of the sprinters are in the main peloton, notably Olav Kooij, Jasper Philipsen, Søren Wærenskjold, Huub Artz and, of course, Mads Pedersen. They are closing in on the breakaway of the day, just 38 seconds back with 91km to go.The likes of Biniam Girmay, Phil Bauhaus, Milan Fretin, Pascal Ackermann, Max Kanter, Pavel Bittner and Magnus Cort are all in the groupetto, around three minutes behind the leaders and 2:30 behind the main chasing group.Decathlon CMA CGM have the best of both worlds at the moment, with Tiesj Benoot in the break, young GC star Paul Seixas sat comfortably in the peloton, and sprinter Olav Kooij in the hunt for a second stage win with his teammate contributing to the chase. A lull in pace in the peloton has allowed for three more attackers to try and re-ignite the fight for the stage win: Jorgenson, Healy and Mathis Le Berre. They are 50 seconds behind the breakway – which has also been squabbling in front and failing to cooperate – with the peloton now at 1:17 with Uno-X actually committing a rider to the chase. NSN aren't giving up in the group who are 3:13 off the lead of the race, and with the peloton failing to work properly together and 75km still to ride, they are ride to keep pushing. Whether they can make it bike in time for the sprint is a different question, though. The chasing trio of Jorgenson, Healy and Le Berre is making a good impression on the deficit to the break. They are now within 27 seconds of making it a break of 19 riders in front. It would also give Hagenes and Baudin a teammate each up in front.Decathlon are committing riders to the chase, with the gap down to 54 seconds. After realising Uno-X would help, they came back to control, but it will take a fully concerted effort to bring back those in front. Breakaway up to 19 ridersContact has been made by Jorgenson, Healy and Le Berre, strengthening the break. However, they will need to get a move on in front if they want any hope of making it to fight out the win – the gap to the charging peloton is down to just 30 seconds now.Adam Yates appears to massively struggling. He dropped back curiously from the peloton, and is now two minutes behind and still barley pedalling. He doesn't look himself or in tip-top shape. He crashed out of the Giro earlier this season, but that was the first time he hadn't finished a Grand Tour in his career, so him climbing off would be a big shock. Mads Pedersen is on the attack again! With the gap to the break down to just 20 seconds or so, he scorched across the gap and joined the front group. A reminder that there's still 35km to go until the race reaches the late intermediate sprint. He's got Simmons to help drag the lead group along.Pedersen started the day in green, but he knew that if he was going to solidify his lead before a trio of stages in the mountains, he would have to light up the racing and distance the likes of Philipsen and Girmay. One of them is well out the back, but the Belgian – who started as his nearest rival – is still well in the race for the stage win. Much of the racing today has been dictated by how Lidl-Trek want to play it.When the Dane made his move to bridge across, it brought several riders with him, one of which was Philipsen, who is currently in a move about 20 seconds behind the lead breakaway where Pedersen is.The race is split all over the road in the breakaway as a result of Pedersen's daring move:The Dane is up in the front now with 50km to go, alongside Healy, Kwiatkowski, Stuyven, Planckaert and Engelhardt. Behind them is a massive group of 26 riders, notable names including Visma trio Campenaerts, Hagenes and Jorgenson, Hindley, Simmons, sprint hope Kooij and lead-out man Benoot, and Van der Poel for Alpecin. That second group is 45 seconds down the road from the leading five riders, with Philipsen in another chasing group at 1:27. After almost three hours of crazy action, the peloton has finally calmed down and is now sat more than four minutes behind the exploding breakaway. Under 20km to go until the intermediate sprint in Colombe, where Pedersen will surely be taking maximum points and extending his lead in the green jersey competition. In theory he should pay for all the efforts he's made today, but no-one gets better on a brutally hard day like Mads Pedersen does – he thrives in the chaos.Adam Yates vs the time cutHere's a look at British rider and key domestique for Tadej Pogačar, Adam Yates, before he started to struggle in the peloton and dropped like a stone through the groups and to the back of the race. As it stands, he's more than nine minutes behind the leaders on the road and five minutes behind the yellow jersey group. He's reportedly struggling with sickness and is now back at the team car and talking with a sports director. He'd be a big loss for Pogačar and Del Toro just before the final mountain stages. It will be a race to beat the time cut for Yates now.There's been a change in the breakaway groups, with the Philipsen chase group joining up with the Kooij group – the two top sprinters still in the running for the victory today – which has led to Planckaert no longer working in the group of five in front as his Alpecin team chases behind. Pedersen and his five companions still lead the day by 1:26 from the group containing the two elite sprinters, and soon, Decathlon will likely commit a man to this chase to help Philipsen's team, allowing Kooij a chance to double up after already taking a win on his Tour debut into Pau on stage 5. Tim Wellens has dropped all the way back to help Adam Yates in his efforts to stay in the race, UAE clearly not giving up on their luxury climbing domestique. They are around 14 minutes behind the leaders on the road and have Anders Halland Johannessen for company, but the Norwegian dropped much earlier in the stage.Mads Pedersen gets his wish and dominates the intermediate sprint from the leading group of six. Planckaert tried to outsprint him and take points away for his leader Philipsen, but was no match for the green jersey. Pedersen gets the 25 points at the line, and with four riders separating Philipsen from the position he wanted, he only managed to get nine, even with Lidl-Trek's other riders trying to take them from him in the chasing group.Pedersen now has 442 points, with Philipsen on 395. With 50 points on offer for the stage win, there is a world where the Belgian can take the green jersey off of the Dane's shoulders if he is to win the day and Pedersen pays for his efforts and doesn't score any.Jasper Stuyven has gone on the attack after the intermediate sprint, perhaps knowing that Pedersen wouldn't necessarily commit to chasing him after mopping up the sprint points he wanted. The Soudal-QuickStep man is now 20 seconds in front of the Pedersen group of five chasers, but a full minute ahead of the group containing Kooij and Philipsen.A former Milan-San Remo winner and all-around elite Classics rider, Stuyven has never won a Tour de France stage in his 10 appearances so far. He's come close on several occasions, notably on the gravel stage to Troyes in 2024, and stage 7 of the 2021 race when he was second in Le Creusot. He's actually only won one Grand Tour stage in his career, at the Vuelta in 2015, so this would be a massive result for him. His gap is at 45 seconds to his nearest chasers and growing now.Stuyven is all alone against the breakaway now, with Pedersen's group being re-absorbed. His gap was going in the right direction out to almost 50 seconds, but with Planckaert and Engelhardt back, it should be Alpecin, Lidl, Jayco and Decathlon all chasing together to try bring back the Soudal-QuickStep rider. Stuyven hasn't won a race since he triumphed on the Via Roma in San Remo, but he's sensing the chance to win big again here. Gap at 32 seconds and coming down quickly as Alpecin keep pressing on to bring him back and give Philipsen a final chance. Stuyven may make it to the finale with a lead intact, but here's a reminder that it isn't a totally flat run-in that awaits him in Voiron. He will need to get over this 2.6km bump to have a chance at surviving away solo. Alpecin and Jayco are on the front and pushing, but they are running out of power. Stuyven is still 23 seconds ahead and the chasing group should be confident of bringing him back – any hesitation will kill off the chase, however. Alpecin have lost two riders with Planckaert and Verstrynge dropping after their efforts. They may have to use Van der Poel early to try and ensure Philipsen has a chance, but that would mean sacrificing their elite lead-out. It's Engelhardt vs Stuyven now as Jayco try and bring things back for Michael Matthews. Lidl-Trek are also pushing on through Derek Gee-West, but Pedersen has shouted at his teammates to stop, provoking an attack from Movistar's Castrillo. This stop-start attacking will help Stuyven, but as the race is on a little drag, Pedersen is perhaps gambling that Lidl-Trek don't have to do all the work as the Belgian falters in front.It was a valliant move, but Stuyven has been reeled back in by the attackers 3.5km from the finish. He almost comes to a complete standstill as the finale plays out from this breakaway.Per Strand Hagenes attacks! We knew Visma were going to try and it's their young powerful Nowegian who has ripped off the front in search of glory. He's been follow by several riders, notably Van der Poel, Gee-West and Quinn Simmons, who counters in the US champion's jersey. It's back together and cagey as Van der Poel gets to the front but doesn't attack – a sign he must be working for Philipsen, or that he's running out of steam. Still Lidl-Trek keep the pace up as Kooij moves up for Decathlon with Benoot to look after him. Lidl-Trek and Decathlon are best placed but the attacks will surely keep coming over the top in the final kilometre. There's several names who we've barely seen in this group and could be a real threat. Josh Tarling attacks for Ineos, trying to pull off a late raid for Netcompany Ineos. The TT specialist has a gap but Van der Poel is chasing him hard to bring it back for Philipsen. He too is caught as the sprint winds up now. Tarling led through the final corner, but it's Van der Poel who kicks off the lead-out for Philipsen with an armchair ride for his Belgian sprinter. He's at the front and surely this is going to be it for Philipsen...Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) wins an absolute thriller on stage 17 of the Tour de France, with the Belgian finally delivering on all the pressure and expectation to get his victory. He can give big thanks to his whole team today, but most notably Emile Verstyrnge, Edward Planckaert, and, of course, Mathieu van der Poel, who gave everything to chase down moves in the finale and then did the perfect lead-out. When Philipsen went, no one was able to get anywhere near him – a true breakaway epic won by the sprinters. Philipsen's 11th Tour stage winA special day for Philipsen and Alpecin-Premier Tech. This victory is his 11th at the Tour de France and means he's won at least once in the past five Tours, highlighting his incredible consistency, even at the last moment. Up to this point, he'd been fifth, fifth, fourth and third twice in the sprint stages, and he was getting some flak from his team manager Christoph Roodhooft, but today they got the best out of him, showing his prowess as much more than just a pure sprinter to win an epic day from the breakaway.This big win also brings him within seven points of Mads Pedersen's lead in the green jersey points classification, who faded to eighth at the finish but picked up enough points to stay in the lead with only a few intermediate sprints and the Montmartre stage in Paris left to decide the green jersey. Here's the top 10 from a brutal stage 17 to Voiron.Jasper Philipsen (Bel) Alpecin-Premier TechMauro Schmid (Swi) Jayco-AlUlaOlav Kooij (Ned) Decathlon CMA CGMLewis Askey (GBr) NSN Cycling TeamRick Pluimers (Ned) Tudor Pro CyclingClément Russo (Fra) Groupama-FDJ UnitedHuub Artz (Ned) Lotto-IntermarchéMads Pedersen (Den) Lidl-TrekMichael Matthews (Aus) Jayco-AlUlaAaron Gate (NZl) XDS AstanaAnd here's the top 10 on GC, with no changes after a breakaway stage did eventually play out. It was far from an easy day, especially in those brutal opening two hours of breakaway formation heading out of Chambéry, so expect some of the frontrunners to feel it in their legs on the uphill roads to Orcières-Merlette on Thursday's stage 18Tadej Pogačar (Slo) UAE Team Emirates-XRGRemco Evenepoel (Bel) Red Bull-Bora-HansgroheIsaac Del Toro (Mex) UAE Team Emirates-XRGPaul Seixas (Fra) Decathlon CMA CGMJuan Ayuso (Spa) Lidl-TrekMattias Skjelmose (Den) Lidl-TrekLenny Martinez (Fra) Bahrain VictoriousTom Pidcock (GBr) Pinarello-Q36.5Jordan Jegat (Fra) TotalenergiesYannis Voisard (Swi) Tudor Pro CyclingHere's what an emotional Philipsen had to say after finally taking his first win of the 2026 Tour de France: "I was in the shape of my life, and first 12 days were just horrible, and I couldn't find myself, couldn't find the feeling I was hoping for. After 17 stages to take the victory, it's really one for the team that keep on believing in me," he said."I just want to thank everybody, I was like an angry bird at the table. I was disappointed in myself, angry that I couldn't take stage when I didn't find my feeling. The team had to deal with it, this one is for them to keep on believing and just fighting back until stage 17. "It’s a victory that cost me a few years of my life, I think, I was fighting somewhere in the middle, between groups. I thought the stage was lost. But the team kept on believing, and we tried to make a plan, and in the end, they were super strong and delivered me at the finish line."Here's what race leader Tadej Pogačar said after successfully navigating a tricky day. He was ready for the fight at the start, but that didn't make it any less difficult, even from the peloton. He also shed some more light on the situation surrounding Adam Yates, who – despite his struggles – finished safely inside the time cut, 26:36 down on the leaders alongside teammate Tim Wellens:"I was expecting that it was going to be hard and a fight until after the climbs. It was a hard day," said Pogačar."Adam was sick yesterday, felt like sick in the morning and throughout the day, so he suffered like a dog yesterday. Today he looked much better, was eating good, but when you have a bad day like him yesterday, you pay a day later. I hope he didn't go too deep into the hole and he will be better tomorrow."You can read our full story on Adam Yates' struggles on stage 17 below. As it stands, he's still in the race, but it's unknown how much he'll be able to help on the trio of upcoming mountain stages after really suffering with stomach issues:If you're after a full recap of an action-filled stage 17, read our full stage report:Mads Pedersen's undying efforts in the stage 17 breakaway did see him successfully defend the green jersey, but only narrowly. After putting in several attacks and blowing the race apart again in the final 60km, he gained 35 total points in the classification. By winning the stage and taking seventh at the intermediate sprint, Jasper Philipsen took home 59 points and moved to within seven of overtaking the Dane. It's a competition that should go right down to the wire in the final four stages, and with the intermediate sprints coming late in all three of the mountain stages, it could come down to the final IS point on stage 21 where green is decided. That is, unless either of the two star riders can score big on the Montmartre route on the final day in Paris. Alongside the yellow jersey, UAE Team Emirates-XRG also lead the King of the Mountains classification through Pogačar, and the best young rider's white jersey competition with Isaac del Toro. The Slovenian leads the former by 24 points from Valentin Paret-Peintre (Soudal-QuickStep) and, unless someone goes crazy from the breakaway in the next three days, will be expected to win the polka-dot jersey again. Del Toro's jersey is much less secure, with 19-year-old Paul Seixas breathing down his neck just 20 seconds down in the race for white, but also in pursuit of the final podium spot overall. It will be a battle to watch in the coming three days. The profile for stage 18 of the Tour de France. It's the easiest of the three incoming days in the Alps. With just under 4000 metres of elevation gain, and after today's all-out stage, it's sure to see some riders really struggling. With five categorised climbs along the route, it should be enough to see the GC group whittled down purely due to attrition, but the final climb to the Orcières-Merlette ski resort may not be enough to split the top favourites – especially when they know an Alpe d'Huez double-header looms at the end of the week. A breakaway in theory could be allowed to play out the day by UAE, especially given the likely non-full strength Adam Yates in their support, but to be honest, it would have to be someone as strong as a Richard Carapaz who gets up the road if they are going to survive the chasing peloton. It should be a good fight nonetheless, though.That concludes our live coverage of stage 17 of the Tour de France, with Alpecin-Premier Tech doubling up on wins and Jasper Philipsen being guided to the line by Mathieu van der Poel in Voiron. They'll be looking to win once more on the Paris stage with Van der Poel, or indeed Philipsen if it does end up being a sprint, but the Belgian will mostly be trying to snatch the green jersey away from Mads Pedersen in the coming days. All in all, it looks like another successful Tour for the Belgian team who always punches above their weight in July, led by their two superstar riders.Come back tomorrow to follow our live coverage of stage 18, for an 185km day of climbing from Voiron to Orcières-Merlette.

item.title: Every Tour de France Rider Tested in Rest Day Anti-Doping Sweep
item.author: ahood
item.content:encoded: The ITA collected samples from the entire Tour peloton for biological passport analysis amid criticism over surprise overnight controls as UCI shows public support.

item.title: Hamilton takes "full responsibility" for disappointing Belgian GP
I rode the Col de Sarenne to see what the newest Tour de France climb is really like – Here's what's in store for the Queen stage
item.author: william.jones@futurenet.com (Will Jones)
item.content:encoded: Alpe d’Huez is a terrible climb. It’s still enjoyable if you’re into that sort of thing, but it’s extremely monotonous, and a prime launchpad for watt-watching efforts. I truly believe you can get much the same experience by riding Alpe du Zwift and using your imagination. Without the throngs of fans by the roadside it feels sterile, not aided by the aspect, meaning it gets baked by the afternoon sun in its entirety. It's far better to ride down the Alpe, and ride up the Col de Sarenne instead.The Col de Sarenne was last used in 2013 as a descent, complete with hastily-added hay bales after much consternation about the danger the route presented. In order that you may be familiarised with this new col, I spent a hot afternoon in June riding it, on a beautiful loop from Alpe d’Huez and back. It is a truly sensational climb. A real climber's climb: narrow, undulating, with distinct phases and spectacular views. There are some horrible pitches that are hidden by the stats: 12.8km at 7.5% sounds easier than the Alpe’s 14km at 8%, but the Sarenne pitches up to 14% for its steepest 100m stretch, and its final 4km are at 9%, with the ultimate hairpins pitching up to 12% just to wring every joule of energy from your legs. I loved it, and I massively underestimated it. My route up the Sarenne began differently to that which the Tour will trace, based as I was in Alpe d’Huez for some other work engagements. While the Tour heads down the more or less totally straight valley road from La Grave, I came at it from Bourg d’Oisans. After an arrow-straight highway, descending to deprive you of precious metres of elevation, you enter the belly of the beast via a spectacular gorge, split by a river and a large Alpine highway. Reaches an altitude of 1,999mTwo very manageable ramps get you to Mizoen when the climb begins, but from my point of view, with an ascent of Alpe d’Huez in my legs from the day before, I was glad things weren’t too severe to begin with. This way is you get two short descents and a couple of refreshingly cool tunnels to steady the ship before things begin in earnest. You also get to see the full height of the dam that holds back Lac du Chambon, which you then have to ride across. Don’t look down, but don’t look up either as things head relentlessly skywards from here on in. The Sarenne begins in earnest just after the dam. You’re faced with a rusty, cubist building overlooking the expanse of water, at which point you turn left and have to tackle three ramps up to Mizoen, all over 11% and topping out, according to my Strava trace, at just over 13% on the initial pitch. It’s a very sustained 2km of effort, and while it’s very early in the climb to act as a launch ramp if the race is still together, it will reveal very early who has the legs. These ramps also mark a distinct change in feel – from here on in it feels much more Pyrenean than Alpine. Mizoen and the kilometre beyond it offers the first of two places to get some life back into your legs or, in the case of a race, a prime place to pick up a willing satellite domestique for a tow if the stars align. The end of this relatively flat stretch is marked by a short, sharp descent which slingshots you into another pair of ramps and hairpins, the first of which tops 15% before sending you onwards on a proper full width, two lane road to Clavans-le-Bas. Mizoen to Clavans-le-Bas is certainly the least interesting part of the ascent. A larger road, surrounded by nondescript woodland that hides what’s to come. From Clavans-le-Bas comes the second, longer section of flat-ish (2-3%) for about a kilometre and a half past grazing cows (at least when I did it) and a very timely sign with a trough to soak my sun-addled head before things really get serious. From Clavans-le-Bas to the summit there is no let-up, but the scenery is divided neatly into two parts. The first wends its way through beautiful woodland, with Alpine flowers blooming in every verge. I’m not too proud to admit I was in the box a little bit at this point, and so didn’t take any photos. My one memory, besides thinking ‘Hell, this is hard work’ is that I imagined the honey from here would be really excellent; I expect I was craving sugar.The woodland does offer welcome shade before what is quite an exposed final section, but with that comes a stillness and a noticeable increase in humidity, not aided by the numerous roadside streams, which occasionally spill out onto the road. From the woodlands and beyond the road surface is markedly terrible. The road is wide enough for a single vehicle, and terribly pockmarked. Patches of fresh, unset bitumen sprinkled with gravel are dotted every few metres, and cause immediate wheel slip out of the saddle, and cause hitchhikers to cling to your tyres. It’s not a descent I would relish, even as someone who usually cherishes Alpine descents. Given the increased focus on rider safety these days, I think if the route had gone down the Sarenne again this year there would have been uproar.A giant boulder and the final ascent The problem with the woodland, pretty though it is, is that there is a lot left to the imagination. It feels placid amongst the trees, secluded but not lonely and isolated. If, like me, you have the elevation profile on your computer you’ll be left wondering when things will change. Abruptly, signposted very easily for anyone watching the race on television, the treeline gives way at a stone house perched under a comically large boulder. It’s an obvious metaphor, but from here on in you are the house, and the mountain is the boulder.The upper slopes of the Sarenne are exposed, oppressive, and considering you’re only a handful of kilometres, as the crow flies, from the relatively large Alpe d’Huez ski resort, they feel extremely isolated. Even compared to the single lane up the Col d’Aspin in the Pyrenees it feels ancient. It doesn’t impose with grandeur in the same way as the upper reaches of the Col de l’Iseran, it’s much prettier than that, but there is the same feel of being totally out of reach. The main issue, beyond the gradient which never lets up and the surface which is truly awful, is the fact you can now see the top, and the ten hairpins that lie between you and the end. The first six come in quick succession, but the central portion of much longer straights and fewer hairpins is much more mentally challenging. I was glad to have to stop and take photos at this point. Four kilometres from the top is where the trees give way, and it’s a real grind. From a race perspective anyone who is dropped here will be able to see the race slipping away from them for almost the entirety of the rest of the way, sapping morale still further. The final couple of hairpins are short, steep, and spit you out onto what feels like a false flat for the last few hundred metres to the high point. Unlike the climb to Alpe d’Huez, which doesn’t seem to finish anywhere but rather diffuses into an ugly resort leaving you without any real sense of achievement, the Sarenne does at least offer a definitive finish point. It’s nondescript, marked with a sign and a modern triangular hut next to some concrete ruins, but it is a finish line nonetheless and extremely welcome. While the peak ends here, the stage does not. Like me, riders then have to descend down the balcony road to Alpe d’Huez before tackling a few final hairpins to a finish line that feels a little manufactured, especially given it will have hosted a finish just 24 hours previously. Logistically it makes sense, but from a drama perspective I’m not so sure. The descent to the resort is beautiful, but far from flat and in dire need of resurfacing, or in some places just surfacing; it’s gravel, in places, and offers a taste of the danger of what descending the Sarenne would be like.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!
