item.title: Remco Evenepoel is on the rise but the battle for the Tour de France podium is far from settled – Analysis
item.content:encoded: Remco Evenepoel is the man of the moment at the Tour de France. As I write this from the lobby of a Campanile hotel, not far from Geneva, a series of ring-led chants float in from the car park where the Belgian has gathered his teammates and team staff, telling them how much he loves them and letting rip on a bottle of champagneIt’s the second night in three that he has done this, and the second stage in two, with Evenepoel winning back-to-back in this Tour de France either side of the rest day.What’s more, the rider he has consigned to second place on both occasions is none other than Tadej Pogačar, the cannibalistic four-time champion who, when he’s out for victory, doesn’t usually come up short.But if you’re sensing a momentum shift in this Tour de France then your radar is very much off. Pogačar botched – or some would say hardly bothered with – the sprint atop the Plateau de Solaison on stage 15, and on stage 16 the UAE Team Emirates-XRG leader was beaten on Evenepoel’s home turf, against the clock, in a result that was entirely to be expected. Not even Evenepoel himself believes the yellow jersey is within his reach in what’s left of this Tour. “Tadej is on his planet, with the others behind. I focus on my second place and will do everything to defend it and stay in my second place – that’s my objective for ending the Tour de France,” Evenepoel stated on Tuesday.With Jonas Vingegaard’s sudden exit on Sunday removing all tension in the battle for the yellow jersey, the final week has become very much a race for second, and a race for the podium.In that respect, Evenepoel has seemingly taken a stranglehold on the runner-up spot. Though 4:32 behind Pogačar, he is now 2:19 ahead of third-placed Isaac del Toro (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) and 2:39 ahead of Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM), after putting more than a minute into both of them in the stage 16 time trial.It very much feels like Evenepoel is in the ascendancy. Back-to-back stage wins will do that for you. And while he was always expected to win the time trial, the way he was seemingly floating up the double-digit gradients on the Plateau de Solaison on Sunday was a massively encouraging sign after his yo-yoing efforts in the previous mountain stages.Evenepoel spoke after the time trial of how his form has been rising in this Tour de France. He made reference to his somewhat controversial lack of racing between the Spring and the Tour, arguing that the first week was about feeling his way into the race and the last week about truly hitting his stride. If he’s as good as his word, then you’re looking at your second-place finisher at the Tour de France.And yet, this is far, far from a foregone conclusion.Three big mountain stages await and they will be a stern test of Evenepoel’s credentials, both physically and tactically.On the physical side, it’s fair to point out that Evenepoel has not always been the most consistent high-mountain climber. He has won the Vuelta a España and podiumed at the Tour, so his GC credentials cannot seriously be questioned, but he is prone to having bad days as well as good days. We’ve seen it already in this Tour, sometimes within the same stage.On that note, it was interesting that Pogačar noted that Sunday’s stage, boiling down to one final climb, suited Evenepoel, but that he struggled comparatively on Saturday’s multi-mountain stage in the Vosges. Stages 18 and 19 of this Tour, finishing at Orcières-Merlette and Alpe d’Huez respectively, look similar but the final mountain stage on Saturday is a different beast, with three hors-catégorie climbs and one category-1 climb on the menu. It’s a crazy stage, where any weakness can be punished by minutes, and it’s a risk not just for Evenepoel but for all involved.Realistically, there are two other fighters in the battle for the final two spots on the podium: Del Toro and Seixas (Juan Ayuso is two minutes further back after the TT and appears to be on the decline). And like Evenepoel, they too have been inconsistent in this Tour. Del Toro won a stage early on and has been right up there in the past two mountain stages, but the Mexican has also been dropped from the GC group on numerous occasions – in the Massif Central on stage 10 he lost a minute to Evenepoel and Seixas.For his part, Seixas, who rode a contained start to the Tour, appeared to be emerging from his shell and moving through the gears in the Vosges on stage 14, only to slip back and lose that momentum at Solaison on stage 15. With Seixas, the uncertainty is even greater, given this is, remarkably, his debut Grand Tour. Whatever happens now, it has been a success for the teenager and every day is effectively a step further into the unknown at this point.And so we have three riders who, unlike Pogačar, are unpredictable in terms of their performance in the remaining mountains. Evenepoel certainly holds the upper hand by far, but big swings in fortune would not be a surprise, and so it still feels open at this point. And then there’s the tactical equation. In this respect, once the glow of his time trial victory wears off, Evenepoel might start to rue the day his best teammate, Florian Lipowitz, exited the race in a crash. The pair have had their frictions but Evenepoel had already taken enough of a nudge in front for Lipowitz to declare his loyalty to his teammate’s cause even in advance of the TT, and he was set to be a crucial crutch in the final week. With Jai Hindley, Red Bull’s next-best climber, not firing on all cylinders, this leaves Evenepoel somewhat isolated in the closing stages. Now that might not ordinarily be a problem if this was a Pogačar vs Vingegaard battle, and Evenepoel was in another race behind them, but the latter’s absence has upended the tactical complexion of this race.We saw it immediately on the climb to the Plateau de Solaison. Pogačar, without his main rival to worry about, immediately turned domestique and worked almost the entire climb for Del Toro. It’s bizarre seeing the yellow jersey do this, but it puts Del Toro in a luxury position. The other factor to consider is the white jersey, for which Del Toro and Seixas (and also Ayuso) are eligible.“It will be a nice race within a race,” said Evenepoel, who will surely hope that Del Toro and Seixas simply ride against one another, with that white jersey and the final podium spot effectively settled in the same breath.When the organisers pitched this Tour route, they spoke of ‘suspense to the end’, and compared to the two Alpine stages that come before it, that final one over the Croix de Der, Télégraphe, Galibier and Sarenne very much feels like judge, jury and executioner. It may not represent that in the fight for the yellow jersey anymore, but it still does in a fight for the podium that very much remains open-ended and intriguing.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. From Barcelona through to Paris, our experienced team will bring you breaking news, expert insight, and in-depth coverage from every stage as the battle for the yellow jersey plays out. Plus, access the Cyclingnews app to follow the action on the go!

item.title: Former BMX World Champion calls cancellation of final day of UCI racing at this year's competition in Australia 'a disgrace to this sport'
item.author: jackie.tyson@futurenet.com (Jackie Tyson)
item.content:encoded: Strong winds in Brisbane, Australia led to the cancellation of racing at the 2026 UCI BMX Racing World Championships on Sunday and six winners of the rainbow stripes were then determined from the Saturday results in what the UCI called "unprecedented circumstances". Two-time BMX world and Olympic champion Maris Strombergs took to social media to blast the decision as "crazy" and "wrong", saying the UCI decision to cancel remaining rounds and not try to delay or reschedule competition was "a disgrace to this sport"."The best BMX riders in the world deserve better than this. Decisions like today's damage the credibility of our sport, undermine the athletes, and send the wrong message to everyone who cares about BMX," Strombergs wrote on Instagram."I'd welcome the opportunity to sit down with whoever made this decision and hear the reasoning behind it. This is crazy. It's wrong. And it needs to stop!!!"It had been windy on Saturday for the opening rounds, which saw fields end competitions at different points. The elite men's competition went to 1/ 8 Final on Saturday, while junior races completed quarterfinals. The elite women and U23 women and men only completed first rounds and Last Chance qualifier runs. The single ‘main’ final run of the best eight riders in each division did not take place.Strong winds then continued on Sunday, with a local marine wind warning issued by the Bureau of Meteorology in the early morning hours. While gale-force winds offshore brought huge waves into Brisbane and the Gold Coast, with lower impacts inland but still strong winds across the venue, the Brisbane SX International BMX Centre at the Sleeman Sports Complex."Following multiple delays and repeated assessments of the prevailing conditions, it was determined that racing could not be conducted safely within the available competition window," the UCI said in a statement."The decision to cancel the remainder of the competition programme was taken in the interest of rider safety, with the support of the UCI Athletes' Commission representative on site, the National Federation team managers and the Local Organising Committee."The new world champions were then confirmed from limited rounds using the best times from the most recently completed heats from Saturday.With the best score after three rounds, Jesse Asmus of Australia was crowned the elite men's BMX world champion on Sunday when the UCI cancelled additional rounds. Great Britain's Ross Cullen claimed the silver medal, while France's Mathis Ragot Richard took bronze.In the elite women's category, Olympic champion Bethany Shriever of Great Britain added another rainbow jersey to her collection with the best score after Round 1. Australia's Saya Sakakibara earned the silver medal while the Netherlands' Laura Smulders completed the podium in third.Asmus, a 23-year-old rider from the Gold Coast, used his technical abilities to win both his first round and 1/8 Final heats. He said knowing there was a bad weather forecast for the next day gave him motivation for Saturday."I had an inkling yesterday that the weather wasn’t looking good, so I knew the plan was to lay down some good laps and go as fast as I could. It turns out that strategy played in my favour pretty well," he said in an AusCycling statement."I feel like it's not the way we all wanted it to go; but it's still good to get it done. I feel like I've been making really good strides in my riding lately and it [a world title] was going to come eventually. But to happen right now, especially in front of the home crowd, it doesn't feel real yet."In the under-23 category, Australia's Joshua Jolly won the men's world title while Dutch rider Renske van Santvoort won the women's U23 title. Great Britain's Freia Challis claimed the Women Junior title while Lucas Zhou of Canada took the Men Junior title.Training runs on Sunday were initially postponed and rescheduled for 2:30 p.m. local time, with competitions expected to start afterwards at 4:00 p.m. However, winds did not calm and the UCI then made the decision to end the competition for safety reasons.

item.title: Mauro's stage win was old-school GreenEDGE, and it's been like a pressure valve release for the whole team - Luke Plapp's Tour de France diary
item.content:encoded: A lot has happened since my last diary: we’ve got a week more racing in the legs, we’ve crossed to the other side of France, and the team has a stage win thanks to Mauro Schmid! For every team, there’s always so much pressure to get something out of the Tour de France, so having that win has been like a bit of a pressure valve release for the whole team. We still want more, but we can take on each stage now knowing that this year’s race has already been a success and that everything else from here on in is a bonus.It was an amazing day all around and such a bloody awesome team effort from all of the boys. Mauro was the first of us to get in the big break at the start, but we knew that we needed more of us in there if we wanted to make it work. Bling [Michael Matthews], Ben [O'Connor], and I managed to get into a chase group further into the stage. It was a big group, but the boys at the front were going fast, so it was a proper job to get across the gap. Once we were there with Mauro, it was a case of getting ourselves over that category-one climb in the best position possible. I thought the way we rode as a team was amazing, and for Mauro to finish it off was impressive. He’s had such an incredible season, so it was great to see him continue that form, especially after losing out on stage 11 in Toulouse last year. When we got back to the hotel, we had a beautiful dinner with the whole team, staff and riders, with a bit of a toast. It was a really nice gathering, and we were able to reflect on how special it was to win as a team, and how old-school GreenEDGE it was in the way we won the stage. With that victory in the bank now, we’ve been able to race with a bit more freedom and with even more confidence. Not many teams will finish this Tour de France with a win, so to have already done it is pretty sweet. It was a special week.Now we’re heading into the final week, and it feels like Paris is within touching distance. I really maxed out on the 'rest' part of the second rest day, basically doing as little as possible before we got back to racing. I’m writing this diary after the stage 16 individual time trial. I haven’t been feeling great the last couple of days, but I was still hopeful of what I could do. Very early on, though, I knew that it wasn’t going to be a day for me, so I parked it up and decided to focus on the days to come. It was a bit of a shame not to have the legs I was hoping for today, but there’s still a week of racing to go and some stages that suit us.Mauro’s win was a bit early for our team owner Gerry Ryan to see; he arrived at the race the day after, so we’re hoping that we can give him a win while he’s here. He’s done so much for GreenEDGE over the years, so it’s always special when we can pay him back with a victory. Needless to say, the boys are super motivated. Looking at where my teammates and I can target during this final week, I feel that stage 17 is really good for Bling with that climb at the end. Stage 18 is another good opportunity for the breakaway, hopefully, and obviously Paris is another chance for Pascal [Ackermann]. It will be interesting to see how the final week plays out, especially with Jonas [Vingegaard] out of the race. It was a shame to see him go like that. Hopefully, the breakaways will be given some opportunities to have a go. The weather is cooling down, which is good; the feistiness of the peloton has definitely simmered down too, but everyone is getting tired.

item.title: Bike broken and police on the scene as Xandro Meurisse struck from behind by car driver after Tour de France time trial
item.content:encoded: Pinarello-Q36.5 rider Xandro Meurisse suffered a major scare following the stage 16 individual time trial in the Tour de France when the Belgian racer was hit by a spectator's car as he was heading away from the finish.The incident took place in the town of Thonon-les-Bains after the 26.1km race against the clock, when a spectator's car struck Meurisse from behind, causing the rider to fall on the ground, a team source told .The 34-year-old Belgian racer, who had completed the stage in 52nd place behind stage winner Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), suffered a number of cuts and bruises, as well as some road rash. It was expected, subject to final confirmation, that he would be able to start stage 17 as normal. His bike, however, was completely destroyed as a result of the accident, as well as some of his race clothing being shredded. The team source indicated that the police attended the scene, although they were not called by Meurisse or the team, and that the driver was asked to provide documentation.Images were subsequently posted by Meurisse's fan club on social media showing the damage to his kit and body.The incident was a near-mirror image of the collision suffered by Brandon McNulty (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) before the stage, when the American was hit by a spectator's car during the countdown to the time trial.The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'The focus is already switched' - Visma-Lease a Bike regroup following the crash-out of Tour de France leader Jonas Vingegaard
item.content:encoded: After two full weeks of racing flat out for Visma-Lease a Bike leader Jonas Vingegaard, the Dane's untimely exit on stage 15 has forced a full change of mentality for his teammates, and that was already in evidence on stage 16.In the absence of their leader, the final week is now an opportunity to look for stage wins, American Sepp Kuss told reporters at the time trial finish line after wheeling to a halt.The increasingly strong form shown all-round by the team is an encouraging factor, and that was already shown by Bruno Armirail's excellent performance against the clock, coming home in eighth place."It was definitely disappointing because our part of the race was just kind of starting in that stage [where Vingegaard crashed out]," Kuss told reporters, "and just before the last climb it happened, so it's a bit of - what could have been?"But we still have a good group and the focus is already switched. We miss Jonas, but we're looking forward to just going for the next stages together, and we should have a lot of fun."After nearly a decade as a pro, Kuss pointed out that there are always going to be hard knocks in cycling, and over the years you get used to setbacks."It's part of the sport. Crashes happen. And for us already with Jonas, that [Sunday] night, of course he was disappointed, but he was in a good mood. He also knows that it could have been worse; he's had worse crashes in his career. And he knows he'll be back here next year, and he's already looking forward to that."It's just about switching mentally, which you're already used to doing as a cyclist because there are always more disappointments than victories." Alpe d'Huez and Orcières-MerletteFor a mountain specialist like Kuss, the time trial, where he finished 125th, 6:19 down on winner Remco Evenepoel (Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe), was definitely a day to get through rather than a day to shine. But along with his teammates, the American certainly has some potential targets in mind before the end of the race.Top of those challenges is, logically, Alpe d'Huez, where a born climber like Kuss would relish the chance of success. And with a double summit finish, having twice the opportunities makes for two options at individual glory on one of cycling's most emblematic ascents. But that's not the only mountain stage, either - Friday to Orcières-Merlette also is another moment where he could be in on the action."Definitely. The harder mountain stages are where I'd like to be in the break and mostly just enjoy being in the front of the race in the Tour, which is special on its own. Stage 19 and 20 - we'll see. It depends on what UAE do, but we'll try every day that we can."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. item.title: 'It's a bizarre situation' - Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe encounters mixed emotions as Evenepoel soars to second stage win and Lipowitz crashes out
item.content:encoded: The mood at the Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe team bus at the Tour de France on Tuesday evening was a very mixed one indeed, with some excellent news regarding one of their co-leaders jarring sharply against the regrets of losing the other.For all Remco Evenepoel's triumph was widely predicted, that time-honoured cliché about nothing being certain until the finish line is crossed held as true as ever for the Belgian star. It was equally true for Florian Lipowitz, well inside the top five on all the provisional checkpoints, only to crash in the final part of the 26.1km ITT.Overall, Evenepoel's result amply consolidates his second place behind Tadej Pogačar (UAE Team Emirates-XRG) overall, and his victory was all the sweeter in that it came on a very similar course to the one in Nice in 2024 where he had last lost to the Slovenian in a Tour time trial.But to lose Lipowitz, running fifth overall and potentially a podium finisher in the last week given how strongly he'd performed last year en route to third in Paris, represented a major setback. Any global evaluation of the stage, then, was bound to be tricky."I was in the car with Remco," Head Sports Director Patxi Vila told journalists, "but we heard over the radio that he [Lipowitz] had crashed at km 19.8. He's on his way to the hospital, it doesn't look like it's really serious, but he's out of the race."Vila immediately warned Evenepoel, who had started several minutes later than Lipowitz given his better place overall, to take extra care on the same right-hand corner where the German had fallen, and as he said later, the same corner also proved a difficult one for Pogačar."We just had to focus on Remco and make sure he was OK," Vila added.What had been one of the few two-headed GC challenges in the 2026 Tour - the other being Juan Ayuso and Mattias Skjelmose in Lidl-Trek - has now shrunk to just a single effort, it was pointed out, which will inevitably force some major changes in Red Bull's overall strategy. At the same time, with memories of Jonas Vingegaard's crash out on stage 15 still reverberating through the peloton, it was impossible to avoid remembering what had happened just two days earlier to another major overall contender."First we're one man down, so we have to build our strategy with one less man," Vila said. "And one of our two leaders, so it's a big loss. It's still very early but it's not ideal."For Lipo, I hope it's nothing too serious and he'll be back soon."Having one rider win and another crash out made for what Vila readily agreed was a "bizarre situation, one where it's hard to think about the victory. I just hope he's OK".Yet at the same time, Vila was logically delighted with Evenepoel's knock-out victory, his second for the team in three days. "It was spectacular," he said, "I felt really lucky, privileged to be just 10 metres behind him in the team car, watching how he raced. It was a very special experience."Nor was it just about the day itself, Vila said. There had been months and months of preparation both for the victory on the Solaison and again on the shores of Lake Leman."We've helped him get there with all the material and advice and so on, but finally it came down to him to do all the hard work and stay so dedicated to those goals."Seeing where he could improve on the technical side without losing anything in terms of his strength was key. But I think his approach in terms of his mentality, more than anything else, enabled him to make the biggest difference."As for where Evenepoel can go from here in the third week of the Tour, Vila argued that "after what we saw on Solaison, it shows that we're getting stronger and stronger - not just him, the whole team, and that was what we planned for the last part of the race".However, he all but burst out laughing when asked if there was any chance of Evenepoel taking on Pogačar, saying "Come on, let's get serious." But regarding the third place for the podium, he predicted a "good fight between Paul [Seixas] and Isaac [Del Toro]. Isaac was maybe better in the first week, but Paul has been getting better. So right now, it's very even."The world’s biggest bike race deserves world-class coverage. Subscribe to Cyclingnews for unlimited access to our unrivalled reporting of the 2026 Tour de France. item.title: Manchester United supporters groups in uproar over suspension of season tickets
item.author: Exclusive by Jamie Jackson
item.content:encoded: Fans claim they are being ‘treated as suspects’Club planning to reinstate tickets before season beginsManchester United’s official Fans’ Forum, Manchester United Suppor­ters Trust (Must), The 1958 and five other such groups have complained to the club over supporters being “treated as suspects” after some had season tickets suspended as part of a move to stop the illegal reselling of seats.United last week contacted fans who used six or more devices to access accounts linked to the club or to forward tickets, as in their view this suggested the potential buying of seats to resell. Members are allowed only to forward tickets to other members, or sell them back to United.Continue reading...

item.author: PA Media
item.content:encoded: Ferguson says Keegan’s Newcastle was their best team‘Kevin carried the love of the game on his sleeve’Ferguson and Keegan were involved a fierce battle for Premier League supremacy, with Ferguson’s Manchester United pipping Newcastle to the title in 1996. “I had the utmost respect for him,” Ferguson told Track Radio. “We had many duels when he was manager of Newcastle, but the job he did at Newcastle was fantastic. He re-energised that club and he brought that enthusiasm and fanaticism great supporters can give.Continue reading...

item.title: The key factor as Alonso weighs up F1 future
item.content:encoded: Fernando Alonso insists his Formula 1 future will not depend upon Aston Martin’s performance over the remainder of 2026, but whether he can enjoy driving the current hybrid cars.The Spaniard’s future has become a major talking point in recent months, with his contract with the Silverstone-based squad due to expire at the end of the year.Aston Martin is banking on a major upgrade at ...Keep reading
